> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Differential Equations Between Load, Shear Force, and Bending Moment ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of beam analysis within **Strength of Materials / Mechanics of Solids**, three fundamental internal force quantities govern the structural response of any transversely loaded slender member: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Load Intensity $w(x)$**: The distributed transverse force acting on a beam per unit length, expressed in **$\mathbf{N/m}$** or **$\mathbf{kN/m}$**. It may be uniform (UDL), uniformly varying (UVL), or an arbitrary function of position $x$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shear Force $V(x)$**: The algebraic sum of the vertical (transverse) forces acting on either side of a beam cross-section. It is measured in **$\mathbf{N}$** or **$\mathbf{kN}$** and represents the internal resistance to transverse sliding. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bending Moment $M(x)$**: The algebraic sum of the moments of all forces (about the cross-section) acting on either side of the section. It is measured in **$\mathbf{N \cdot m}$** or **$\mathbf{kN \cdot m}$** and represents the internal resistance to bending (rotation). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board Imagine a diver standing at the free end of a diving board. Think of the board as being sliced into countless infinitesimally thin horizontal "ribbons" stacked along its length. - The **weight of the diver pressing down** is the *load* — distributed along a small region of the board. - As you slice the board at any cross-section, the upper half of the board tries to **slide downward** past the lower half. The internal resistance to this sliding is exactly the **shear force** $V$. - The diver also tries to **rotate (bend)** the board downward. The internal moment resisting this rotation is the **bending moment** $M$. The differential equations are simply the mathematical translation of two fundamental equilibrium laws applied to an infinitesimally small slice: $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$. ### 1.3 Standard Sign Convention (KTU Board Examiner Expectation) > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention Used in Examinations:** > - **Distributed load $w$**: Positive when acting **downward**. > - **Shear force $V$**: Positive when the **right portion moves downward** relative to the left portion (i.e., on the left face of an element, $V$ acts **upward**). > - **Bending moment $M$**: Positive when it produces **sagging** (concave upward) — i.e., tension at the bottom fibre. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Relationships The differential relationships are obtained by isolating an infinitesimal element of the beam of length $dx$ and applying static equilibrium. **Step 1 — Force Equilibrium Gives the First Relation:** For a differential element of length $dx$, the change in shear force across the element must balance the load intensity acting upon it. If $w$ is positive (downward) and the left-face shear is $V$, the right-face shear becomes $V + dV$ (acting downward, by positive sign convention). $$\text{Sum of vertical forces} = 0 \implies V - (V + dV) - w \, dx = 0$$ This reduces to $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$. **Step 2 — Moment Equilibrium Gives the Second Relation:** Taking moments about the left face of the element (neglecting higher-order infinitesimals like $w\,dx^2/2$): $$\sum M_{\text{left}} = 0 \implies -M + (M + dM) - V \, dx = 0$$ This yields $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$. **Step 3 — Combining Both Yields the Third Relation:** Differentiating the second relation with respect to $x$ and substituting the first: $$\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = \frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ ### 2.2 The Logic Behind the Chain — Integration Path These three equations form a beautiful integration chain that allows us to move between load, shear force, and bending moment: $$\boxed{w(x) \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{integrate (with } -)} \;\; V(x) \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{integrate}} \;\; M(x)}$$ | From | To | Mathematical Operation | |---|---|---| | $w(x)$ | $V(x)$ | $V(x) = -\int w(x)\, dx + C_1$ | | $V(x)$ | $M(x)$ | $M(x) = \int V(x)\, dx + C_2$ | | $w(x)$ | $M(x)$ | $M(x) = -\iint w(x)\, dx\, dx + C_1 x + C_2$ | The constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ are determined from **boundary conditions** (support reactions, free end conditions $V=0$ or $M=0$). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Formula Table for KTU Board Examinations:** | # | Differential Relation | Integrated Form | Physical Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ | $V(x) = -\displaystyle\int_0^x w(\xi)\, d\xi + V(0)$ | Slope of SFD equals negative of load intensity | | 2 | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ | $M(x) = \displaystyle\int_0^x V(\xi)\, d\xi + M(0)$ | Slope of BMD equals shear force | | 3 | $\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w$ | $M(x) = -\displaystyle\iint w(x)\, dx^2 + C_1 x + C_2$ | Curvature of BM curve relates to load | | 4 | At point of **zero shear**: $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = 0$ | — | Location of **maximum / minimum bending moment** | | 5 | At **free end** (cantilever tip) | $V = 0, \; M = 0$ | Common boundary conditions | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Structural Design of Bridges & Buildings**: These equations form the foundation of every SFD and BMD drawn in design offices — without them, no reinforced concrete or steel member can be safely proportioned. > - **Aerospace & Mechanical Machine Design**: Shafts carrying gears and pulleys are designed using the same differential chain to find the critical section under combined torsion + bending. > - **Cantilever Sensors & MEMS Devices**: Micro-beams in sensors obey identical $V$–$M$–$w$ relationships scaled by cross-sectional stiffness. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proof that $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ Consider a differential element of the beam of length $dx$, located at an arbitrary position $x$ from the left support. **Step A — Identify forces acting on the element:** - On the **left face**: shear force $V$ acts **upward** (positive convention). - On the **right face**: shear force $V + dV$ acts **downward** (positive convention). - Distributed load $w \cdot dx$ acts **downward** over the element. **Step B — Apply vertical equilibrium $\sum F_y = 0$:** $$V - (V + dV) - w \, dx = 0$$ **Step C — Simplify:** $$V - V - dV - w \, dx = 0$$ $$- dV - w \, dx = 0$$ **Step D — Divide throughout by $dx$:** $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x) \quad \blacksquare$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proof that $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ **Step A — Identify moments acting on the element:** - On the **left face**: bending moment $M$ acts **counter-clockwise** (positive sagging convention). - On the **right face**: bending moment $M + dM$ acts **clockwise**. - Shear force $V$ on the right face produces an additional moment $-V \cdot dx$ about the left face (clockwise sense taken as negative about the chosen pivot). - The distributed load $w\,dx$ acting at the midpoint produces a moment of order $dx^2$, which is a second-order infinitesimal and is **neglected**. **Step B — Apply rotational equilibrium about the left face:** $$\sum M_{\text{left face}} = 0$$ $$-M + (M + dM) - V \, dx = 0$$ **Step C — Simplify:** $$-M + M + dM - V \, dx = 0$$ $$dM - V \, dx = 0$$ **Step D — Divide throughout by $dx$:** $$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x) \quad \blacksquare$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Second-Order Relationship Differentiate Derivation 2 with respect to $x$: $$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{dM}{dx}\right) = \frac{dV}{dx}$$ $$\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = \frac{dV}{dx}$$ Now substitute Derivation 1: $$\boxed{\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)} \quad \blacksquare$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Special Load Cases > [!NOTE] > The following matrix tabulates how the differential chain simplifies under common KTU-prescribed loading types: | Loading Type | $w(x)$ | $V(x)$ | $M(x)$ | |---|---|---|---| | **No load (empty span)** | $0$ | Constant $= C_1$ | Linear $= C_1 x + C_2$ | | **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | $w_0$ | Linear: $-w_0 x + C_1$ | Quadratic: $-\dfrac{w_0 x^2}{2} + C_1 x + C_2$ | | **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)** | $w(x) = kx$ | Quadratic: $-\dfrac{kx^2}{2} + C_1$ | Cubic: $-\dfrac{kx^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$ | | **Point Load at end** | $0^+$ & $0^-$ (Dirac delta) | Step jump | Kink (slope change) | | **Point Couple** | $0$ | $0$ | Step jump | ### 3.5 Worked Example — UDL on Simply Supported Beam **Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a UDL of intensity $w_0$ over its entire length. Using the differential relations, derive $V(x)$ and $M(x)$ at a section located at distance $x$ from the left support. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute support reactions by symmetry:** By symmetry, each support carries half the total load: $$R_A = R_B = \frac{w_0 L}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the load intensity:** $$w(x) = w_0 \quad \text{(constant, positive downward)}$$ **Step 3 — Integrate using $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$:** $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w_0 \implies V(x) = -w_0 x + C_1$$ **Step 4 — Apply boundary condition at $x = 0$:** At the left support, $V(0) = R_A = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2}$ (upward reaction produces positive shear on left face). $$\frac{w_0 L}{2} = -w_0 (0) + C_1 \implies C_1 = \frac{w_0 L}{2}$$ Therefore: $$V(x) = \frac{w_0 L}{2} - w_0 x$$ **Step 5 — Integrate using $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$:** $$M(x) = \int V(x)\, dx = \int \left(\frac{w_0 L}{2} - w_0 x\right) dx$$ $$M(x) = \frac{w_0 L}{2} x - \frac{w_0 x^2}{2} + C_2$$ **Step 6 — Apply boundary condition at $x = 0$:** At the left support (pin/roller), $M(0) = 0$. $$0 = 0 - 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$ **Final Equations:** $$\boxed{V(x) = \frac{w_0}{2}\,(L - 2x) \quad ; \quad M(x) = \frac{w_0 x}{2}\,(L - x)}$$ **Verification (sanity check):** - At $x = 0$: $V = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2}$, $M = 0$ ✓ - At $x = L$: $V = -\dfrac{w_0 L}{2}$, $M = 0$ ✓ - Maximum moment occurs where $V = 0$, i.e., at $x = L/2$, giving $M_{\max} = \dfrac{w_0 L^2}{8}$ ✓ (matches classical result) ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Free Body Diagram of a Beam Element (Block-Level Architecture Flow) The following Mermaid block diagram maps the equilibrium analysis of an infinitesimal beam slice — illustrating how the load, shear forces, and bending moments interact to produce the two classical differential relations. ```mermaid flowchart TD elemA["BEAM ELEMENT of length dx"]:::elem loadBlock["Distributed Load w dx
acting DOWNWARD"]:::load leftV["Shear Force V
acting UPWARD on LEFT face"]:::shear rightV["Shear Force V + dV
acting DOWNWARD on RIGHT face"]:::shear leftM["Bending Moment M
acting COUNTERCLOCKWISE on LEFT face"]:::moment rightM["Bending Moment M + dM
acting CLOCKWISE on RIGHT face"]:::moment eqF["Sum F vertical equals 0"]:::eq eqM["Sum M about left face equals 0"]:::eq resA["RESULT 1 : dV by dx equals negative w"]:::result resB["RESULT 2 : dM by dx equals V"]:::result resC["COMBINED : d2M by dx2 equals negative w"]:::result elemA --> loadBlock elemA --> leftV elemA --> rightV elemA --> leftM elemA --> rightM leftV --> eqF rightV --> eqF loadBlock --> eqF eqF --> resA leftM --> eqM rightM --> eqM leftV --> eqM eqM --> resB resA --> resC resB --> resC classDef elem fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef load fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000 classDef shear fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 classDef moment fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#000 classDef eq fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000 classDef result fill:#fef08a,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Integration Chain Flow — From Load to Bending Moment ```mermaid flowchart LR W["Load w of x"]:::loadNode V["Shear Force V of x"]:::shearNode M["Bending Moment M of x"]:::momentNode BC["Boundary Conditions
R A, R B, M 0, V 0"]:::bcNode SFD["Shear Force Diagram SFD"]:::outputNode BMD["Bending Moment Diagram BMD"]:::outputNode W -->|integrate with negative sign| V V -->|integrate| M BC -.->|determine constants| V BC -.->|determine constants| M V --> SFD M --> BMD classDef loadNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000 classDef shearNode fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000 classDef momentNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 classDef bcNode fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Sign Convention Reference Chart ```mermaid flowchart TB sc1["POSITIVE Load w"]:::sc sc1d["Acts DOWNWARD on beam"]:::desc sc2["POSITIVE Shear V"]:::sc sc2d["Right portion tends to move DOWN
relative to left portion"]:::desc sc3["POSITIVE Moment M"]:::sc sc3d["Produces SAGGING curvature
Tension on BOTTOM fibre"]:::desc sc1 --- sc1d sc2 --- sc2d sc3 --- sc3d classDef sc fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 classDef desc fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#374151,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the differential relation between the rate of change of shear force and the intensity of distributed load on a beam. Briefly explain its physical significance. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The differential relation between shear force and load intensity is: $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ where $w(x)$ is the intensity of the distributed load (positive downward) and $V$ is the shear force at section $x$. **Physical Significance (1 Mark):** The slope of the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) at any point is equal to the negative of the load intensity at that point. In regions where $w = 0$, the SFD is horizontal (constant); where $w$ is constant, the SFD is a straight line; where $w$ varies linearly, the SFD is parabolic. **Mark Distribution:** Statement of equation — **1 Mark**, Explanation of sign — **1 Mark**, Physical interpretation — **1 Mark**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** How is the location of maximum bending moment on a beam identified using the shear force diagram? State the relevant differential equation. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** From the differential relation $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$, the slope of the Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) at any section equals the shear force at that section. Therefore, the bending moment is **maximum or minimum** at the section where: $$V(x) = 0 \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad \frac{dM}{dx} = 0$$ **Procedure (1 Mark):** 1. Sketch the SFD. 2. Locate the point where the SFD crosses the zero axis. 3. That section corresponds to the point of maximum/minimum bending moment. **Mark Distribution:** Differential relation — **1 Mark**, Identification condition $V=0$ — **1 Mark**, Procedure — **1 Mark**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / May 2023 Module 2 Choice Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the differential equations relating the load intensity $w$, the shear force $V$, and the bending moment $M$ for a beam. State clearly the sign convention used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A simply supported beam of span $5\,\text{m}$ carries a uniformly distributed load of $20\,\text{kN/m}$ over its entire length. Using the differential relations, determine: - (i) The equations of shear force and bending moment at a section $x$ from the left support. **(3 Marks)** - (ii) The position and magnitude of maximum bending moment. **(2 Marks)** - (iii) The values of shear force and bending moment at $x = 1\,\text{m}$ and $x = 2.5\,\text{m}$. **(2 Marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** Consider an infinitesimal beam element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the left support. **Sign Convention (1 Mark):** Load $w$ positive downward; Shear $V$ positive when right portion slides down; Moment $M$ positive when it causes sagging. **Free Body Diagram of Element (1 Mark):** - Left face: $V$ (up), $M$ (CCW) - Right face: $V+dV$ (down), $M+dM$ (CW) - Distributed load $w \cdot dx$ acting downward. **Applying $\sum F_y = 0$ (2 Marks):** $$V - (V + dV) - w \, dx = 0 \implies \frac{dV}{dx} = -w$$ **Applying $\sum M_{\text{left}} = 0$ (2 Marks):** $$-M + (M + dM) - V \, dx = 0 \implies \frac{dM}{dx} = V$$ **Combined Relation (1 Mark):** Differentiating the second equation and substituting the first gives: $$\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$ --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** Given: $L = 5\,\text{m}$, $w_0 = 20\,\text{kN/m}$. **(i) Reactions (1 Mark):** By symmetry, $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2} = \dfrac{20 \times 5}{2} = 50\,\text{kN}$. **(ii) Equation of Shear Force (1 Mark):** $$V(x) = R_A - w_0 x = 50 - 20x \;\; \text{kN}$$ **(iii) Equation of Bending Moment (1 Mark):** $$M(x) = R_A x - \frac{w_0 x^2}{2} = 50x - 10x^2 \;\; \text{kN·m}$$ **(iv) Location of Maximum Bending Moment (1 Mark):** Setting $V(x) = 0$: $\;50 - 20x = 0 \implies x = 2.5\,\text{m}$ (mid-span). $$M_{\max} = 50(2.5) - 10(2.5)^2 = 125 - 62.5 = 62.5\,\text{kN·m}$$ **(v) Values at Specific Points (2 Marks):** - At $x = 1\,\text{m}$: $V = 50 - 20 = 30\,\text{kN}$ ; $M = 50(1) - 10(1) = 40\,\text{kN·m}$. - At $x = 2.5\,\text{m}$: $V = 0$ ; $M = 62.5\,\text{kN·m}$ (maximum). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023 Module 2 Choice Pattern]** **(a)** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is subjected to a uniformly varying load (UVL) whose intensity varies from $0$ at the free end to $w_0$ at the fixed end. Using the differential relations, derive expressions for the shear force and bending moment at a distance $x$ measured from the free end. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A simply supported beam of span $6\,\text{m}$ carries a concentrated point load of $40\,\text{kN}$ at a distance $2\,\text{m}$ from the left support. Using the differential equations approach (with proper sign convention and boundary conditions), determine: - (i) The support reactions. **(2 Marks)** - (ii) The shear force and bending moment equations in both segments of the beam. **(3 Marks)** - (iii) The position and value of maximum bending moment. **(2 Marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Cantilever with UVL (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Load function (1 Mark):** Measuring $x$ from the free end, the load intensity varies linearly: $$w(x) = \frac{w_0}{L}\, x$$ **Step 2 — Apply $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ (1 Mark):** $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{w_0}{L} x$$ **Step 3 — Integrate (1 Mark):** $$V(x) = -\frac{w_0}{2L} x^2 + C_1$$ **Step 4 — Boundary condition at free end (1 Mark):** At $x = 0$ (free end), $V = 0$. Therefore $C_1 = 0$. $$\boxed{V(x) = -\frac{w_0 x^2}{2L}}$$ **Step 5 — Apply $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ (1 Mark):** $$M(x) = \int V\, dx = -\frac{w_0 x^3}{6L} + C_2$$ **Step 6 — Boundary condition at free end (1 Mark):** At $x = 0$, $M = 0$. Therefore $C_2 = 0$. $$\boxed{M(x) = -\frac{w_0 x^3}{6L}}$$ **Step 7 — Verification (1 Mark):** At fixed end ($x = L$): $V = -\dfrac{w_0 L}{2}$ and $M = -\dfrac{w_0 L^2}{6}$. The magnitudes match classical textbook results for a triangular UVL on a cantilever. --- **(b) Simply Supported Beam with Point Load (7 Marks):** Given: $L = 6\,\text{m}$, $P = 40\,\text{kN}$ at $a = 2\,\text{m}$ from left support. **(i) Reactions (2 Marks):** Taking moments about A: $$R_B \times 6 = 40 \times 2 \implies R_B = 13.33\,\text{kN}$$ $$R_A = 40 - 13.33 = 26.67\,\text{kN}$$ **(ii) Segment Equations (3 Marks):** **Segment AC ($0 \le x < 2\,\text{m}$):** Here no point load lies to the left of the section. $$V_1(x) = R_A = 26.67\,\text{kN}$$ $$M_1(x) = 26.67\,x \;\; \text{kN·m}$$ **Segment CB ($2 < x \le 6\,\text{m}$):** The point load lies to the left of the section. $$V_2(x) = R_A - P = 26.67 - 40 = -13.33\,\text{kN}$$ $$M_2(x) = 26.67\,x - 40(x - 2) = -13.33\,x + 80 \;\; \text{kN·m}$$ **Verification:** At $x = 6$: $M_2 = -13.33(6) + 80 = -80 + 80 = 0$ ✓ **(iii) Maximum Bending Moment (2 Marks):** In segment AC, $V \ne 0$, so $M$ is linearly increasing. In segment CB, $V = -13.33 \ne 0$, so $M$ is linearly decreasing. Hence maximum moment occurs **at the point load location** $x = 2\,\text{m}$: $$M_{\max} = M_1(2) = 26.67 \times 2 = 53.33\,\text{kN·m}$$ (Equivalently using lever-arm formula: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{Pab}{L} = \dfrac{40 \times 2 \times 4}{6} = 53.33\,\text{kN·m}$ ✓) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 to –3 marks):** Forgetting to apply the negative sign in $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ is the single most frequent mistake. Always re-check by substituting a known UDL case and verifying the sign of $V$. > 2. **Skipping Boundary Conditions (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often integrate but forget to evaluate the constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ using $V=0$ at free ends or $M=0$ at simple supports. > 3. **Confusing $w$ with $W$:** Lower-case $w$ is *intensity* (per unit length); upper-case $W$ is *total load*. Mixing these leads to dimensional errors. > 4. **Neglecting Higher-Order Terms:** When applying moment equilibrium, students sometimes retain $w\,dx^2/2$. This must be neglected as it is second-order infinitesimal. > 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $\text{kN}$ and $\text{N}$ (or $\text{m}$ and $\text{mm}$) in the same numerical problem without conversion is a guaranteed mark-deduction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2** - $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ — Slope of SFD equals negative of load intensity. - $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ — Slope of BMD equals shear force at that section. - $\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w$ — Curvature of BM curve is governed by distributed load. - **Point of zero shear** ($V = 0$) $\Rightarrow$ **Location of extremum (max/min) bending moment**. - **Free end** of a cantilever: $V = 0$ AND $M = 0$ (both simultaneously). - **Pin / roller support** of a simply supported beam: $M = 0$ (but $V \ne 0$, equal to reaction). - **UDL** $\Rightarrow$ SFD is **linear**, BMD is **parabolic**. - **UVL** $\Rightarrow$ SFD is **parabolic**, BMD is **cubic**. - **Point load** $\Rightarrow$ SFD has a **vertical jump (step)**, BMD has a **kink (slope change)**. - **Point couple** $\Rightarrow$ SFD is **unaffected** ($V$ unchanged), BMD has a **vertical jump**. - The **integration chain** always proceeds from $w \to V \to M$ with constants determined by **boundary conditions**. - When asked to "derive using differential equations," examiners expect the FBD, $\sum F_y = 0$, and $\sum M = 0$ steps explicitly — not just a statement of the final relations. - Always **state the sign convention** at the beginning of every answer — examiners award a dedicated mark for this. - Verification using limiting cases ($x=0$, $x=L$, point of symmetry) is a hallmark of a confident answer and earns goodwill marks.

topics.title: Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams of Cantilever and Simply Supported Beam with Point Load/UDL, Point of Inflection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams of Cantilever and Simply Supported Beam with Point Load/UDL, Point of Inflection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Shear Force (SF) at a Section**
> The algebraic sum of the transverse (vertical) forces acting on either side of the section. It represents the internal resistance offered by the beam cross-section to the sliding of one part over the other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bending Moment (BM) at a Section**
> The algebraic sum of the moments of all the forces (about the section) acting on either side of the section. It represents the internal resistance offered by the beam cross-section to the bending (rotation) caused by the external loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention (Standard Sign Convention)**
> - **Shear Force (V)**: Positive if the left portion of the section tends to move **upward** relative to the right portion (i.e., upward force on the left side, downward on the right).
> - **Bending Moment (M)**: Positive if it causes **sagging** (concave upward bending, tension at the bottom fibre). Negative if it causes **hogging** (concave downward, tension at the top fibre).

## 1.2 Types of Beams (Module 2 Focus)

| Beam Type | Description | Reaction Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Cantilever Beam** | Fixed at one end, free at the other | Vertical force + Moment at fixed end |
| **Simply Supported Beam (SSB)** | Pinned at one end, roller at the other | Two vertical reaction forces only |

## 1.3 Types of Loadings Considered

- **Point Load (Concentrated Load, P)**: A single force acting at a specific point (units: **N** or **kN**).
- **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL, w)**: A load spread uniformly over a length (units: **N/m** or **kN/m**).

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **The Diving Board Analogy**
> Imagine a **cantilever beam** as a diving board fixed to a swimming pool wall.
> - When you stand at the free end, the board bends **downward** → the **Bending Moment is negative (hogging)**.
> - The board experiences a sliding tendency between its fibres → this is resisted by the **Shear Force**.
> - The fixed end must "hold back" both the slide and the rotation → it provides a vertical reaction AND a reaction moment.

> [!TIP]
> **The See-Saw Analogy**
> A **simply supported beam** with a load in the middle is just a see-saw with a child sitting in the middle.
> - The board sags in the middle (bends downward) → the **Bending Moment is positive (sagging)**.
> - The two supports push **up** with half the load each (when the load is centered).
> - Internal fibres near the bottom are stretched (tension) and the top is compressed — that is the essence of bending.

## 1.5 Point of Inflection (Point of Contraflexure)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**
> A point on the beam where the **bending moment is zero** AND the curvature of the elastic line **changes sign** (concave up to concave down, or vice versa). At this point, the beam is momentarily straight (zero slope of BM diagram).

For a simple cantilever or SSB under standard point/UDL loadings, the moment is of a single sign throughout the span, so the **point of inflection occurs at the supports (where M = 0) but not within the span itself**. It becomes significant in beams with mixed loading that reverses moment sign (e.g., overhanging beams, propped cantilevers, fixed beams).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Relationships (Governing Differential Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a beam subjected to a distributed load $w(x)$ (positive upward), the following relationships hold universally:
> $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$
> $$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$
> $$\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

## 2.2 Systematic Procedure for Drawing SFD & BMD (KTU Board Standard)

**Step 1: Calculate Support Reactions**
- Apply equilibrium equations: $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$.

**Step 2: Divide the Beam into Distinct Regions**
- A "region" begins/ends at a support, a point load, or a change in distributed load.

**Step 3: Construct Shear Force Diagram (SFD)**
- Use sign convention. For a section at distance $x$, take forces from the **left** (or right).
- Note: At a point load, the SFD has a **vertical jump** equal to the load magnitude.
- Under a UDL, the SFD varies **linearly** with slope $-w$.

**Step 4: Construct Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)**
- Compute moment at each critical point.
- Under a point load, BMD is **linear**.
- Under a UDL, BMD is **parabolic** (quadratic).
- Locate where $V = 0$ → that section has **maximum (or minimum) BM**.

**Step 5: Identify the Point of Inflection**
- Solve $M(x) = 0$ within the span. If multiple sign changes exist, locate each.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Table 2.3.1: Cantilever Beam Formulas (Fixed at Left, Free at Right)

| Loading Condition | Reaction at Fixed End A | $V(x)$ from free end | $M(x)$ from free end | Max $\vert V \vert$ | Max $\vert M \vert$ | Point of Inflection |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Point load $P$ at free end | $R_A = P \uparrow$, $M_A = PL$ | $V = -P$ (constant) | $M = -Px$ | $P$ at fixed end | $PL$ at fixed end | Not within span |
| UDL $w$ over full length $L$ | $R_A = wL \uparrow$, $M_A = \frac{wL^2}{2}$ | $V = -wx$ | $M = -\frac{wx^2}{2}$ | $wL$ at fixed end | $\frac{wL^2}{2}$ at fixed end | Not within span |
| UDL $w$ over length $a$ from fixed end | $R_A = wa \uparrow$, $M_A = \frac{wa^2}{2}$ | $V = -wx$ for $0 \le x \le a$ | $M = -\frac{wx^2}{2}$ for $0 \le x \le a$ | $wa$ at fixed end | $\frac{wa^2}{2}$ at fixed end | Not within span |

### Table 2.3.2: Simply Supported Beam Formulas (Span $L$)

| Loading Condition | Reactions | $V(x)$ from left A | $M(x)$ from left A | Max BM | Point of Inflection |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Point load $P$ at midspan | $R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$ | $\frac{P}{2}$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$ | $\frac{Px}{2}$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$ | $\frac{PL}{4}$ at center | Not within span (BM is sagging throughout) |
| Point load $P$ at distance $a$ from A | $R_A = \frac{Pb}{L}$, $R_B = \frac{Pa}{L}$ where $b = L-a$ | $\frac{Pb}{L}$ for $0 \le x \le a$ | $\frac{Pbx}{L}$ for $0 \le x \le a$ | $\frac{Pab}{L}$ at the load point | Not within span |
| UDL $w$ over full length $L$ | $R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$ | $\frac{wL}{2} - wx$ | $\frac{wLx}{2} - \frac{wx^2}{2}$ | $\frac{wL^2}{8}$ at $x = L/2$ | Not within span (BM is sagging throughout) |
| UDL $w$ from A to C (length $a < L$) | $R_A = \frac{wa}{2}\left(2 - \frac{a}{L}\right)$, $R_B = \frac{wa^2}{2L}$ | Two distinct linear regions | Two distinct parabolic regions | $\frac{w a^2}{8L}\left(4L - 3a\right)$ at $x = \frac{a}{2} + \frac{a^2}{2L} \cdot \frac{L}{a}$ (approximately) | Not within span if loading is sagging only |

> [!WARNING]
> When the BM diagram does not cross the axis within the span, the **point of inflection does not exist within the beam**; it lies conceptually at the supports where $M=0$ and curvature transitions to the support condition.

### Table 2.3.3: Quick Reference — Shape of SFD & BMD

| Loading | SFD Shape | BMD Shape |
|---|---|---|
| Point Load | Rectangle (jump) | Triangle |
| UDL | Trapezoid / Triangle | Parabola (2nd degree) |
| UVL | 2nd degree parabola | Cubic (3rd degree) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Steel Design (IS 800)**: Maximum BM governs the selection of section modulus ($Z$ required $\ge M_{max}/f_y$).
- **Reinforced Concrete Design (IS 456)**: Maximum BM and locations of zero/inflection points dictate placement of tension reinforcement.
- **Deflection Prediction**: The double integration of $M/EI$ gives the slope and deflection — knowing $M(x) = 0$ is essential for boundary conditions.
- **Bridge & Crane Girder Analysis**: Shear force diagrams identify regions requiring closely spaced stirrups (high shear zones).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 CASE A: Cantilever Beam — Point Load $P$ at Free End

**Setup:** A cantilever of length $L$ is fixed at A (left end) and free at B (right end). A downward point load $P$ acts at the free end B.

### Step 1: Reactions at Fixed End A

Apply equilibrium. The fixed support must resist both a vertical force and a moment.

Vertical equilibrium: $R_A - P = 0 \Rightarrow R_A = P$ (upward).

Moment equilibrium about A (taking clockwise positive, or the convention that resists the applied moment):
$$M_A = P \cdot L \quad \text{(counter-clockwise, fixing moment)}$$

### Step 2: Shear Force at Section $x$ (Measured from Free End B)

Consider the right portion (from cut to free end B). Only the point load $P$ acts downward.

$$V(x) = -P \quad \text{(constant for } 0 \le x \le L\text{)}$$

The negative sign indicates that the left side of the section tends to move **downward** relative to the right side (consistent with negative shear for downward loads on the right portion).

### Step 3: Bending Moment at Section $x$ (Measured from Free End B)

Take moments of forces to the **right** of the section about the section. The downward force $P$ at distance $x$ from the cut creates a **hogging** moment:

$$M(x) = -P \cdot x \quad \text{(linear, negative throughout)}$$

At $x = 0$ (free end): $M = 0$.
At $x = L$ (fixed end): $M = -PL$.

### Step 4: Sketch the Diagrams

- **SFD**: A horizontal line at $V = -P$ from $x = 0$ to $x = L$.
- **BMD**: A straight line from $M = 0$ at free end to $M = -PL$ at fixed end (a triangle with maximum ordinate at the wall).

### Step 5: Point of Inflection Analysis

Set $M(x) = 0$:
$$-Px = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$$
The only zero is at the free end (which is a support-equivalent boundary). **No point of inflection within the span** (the moment is hogging throughout).

---

## 3.2 CASE B: Cantilever Beam — UDL $w$ over Entire Length $L$

**Setup:** Cantilever fixed at A, free at B, with a UDL of intensity $w$ (kN/m) acting downward over the entire length $L$.

### Step 1: Reactions at Fixed End A

Total downward load: $wL$.

Vertical equilibrium: $R_A = wL$ (upward).

Moment about A: $M_A = wL \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{2}$ (counter-clockwise, fixing moment).

### Step 2: Shear Force at Section $x$ (Measured from Free End B)

Consider the right portion. The UDL of length $x$ contributes a total downward force $wx$.

$$V(x) = -w x \quad \text{(linear, varying from 0 at free end to } -wL \text{ at fixed end)}$$

### Step 3: Bending Moment at Section $x$ (Measured from Free End B)

The UDL on the right portion (length $x$) has a resultant $wx$ acting at the centroid, which is at distance $x/2$ from the cut.

$$M(x) = -w x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = -\frac{w x^2}{2} \quad \text{(parabolic, negative throughout)}$$

At $x = 0$: $M = 0$.
At $x = L$: $M = -\frac{wL^2}{2}$.

### Step 4: Maximum Values

- Maximum $\vert V \vert = wL$ at the fixed end.
- Maximum $\vert M \vert = \frac{wL^2}{2}$ at the fixed end.

### Step 5: Point of Inflection Analysis

$$-\frac{w x^2}{2} = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$$
The only solution is the free end. **No point of inflection within the span.**

---

## 3.3 CASE C: Simply Supported Beam — Point Load $P$ at Midspan

**Setup:** SSB of span $L$, pinned at A (left), roller at B (right). Point load $P$ acts downward at midspan ($L/2$).

### Step 1: Reactions

By symmetry:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2} \quad \text{(both upward)}$$

### Step 2: Shear Force

From the left side:
$$V(x) = R_A = \frac{P}{2} \quad \text{for } 0 \le x < \frac{L}{2}$$
$$V(x) = R_A - P = -\frac{P}{2} \quad \text{for } \frac{L}{2} < x \le L$$

At the load point, $V$ jumps by $-P$ (from $+P/2$ to $-P/2$).

### Step 3: Bending Moment

From the left side:
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} x \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le \frac{L}{2}$$
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - P\left(x - \frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{P}{2}x - Px + \frac{PL}{2} = -\frac{P}{2}x + \frac{PL}{2} \quad \text{for } \frac{L}{2} \le x \le L$$

At $x = 0$: $M = 0$.
At $x = L/2$: $M = \frac{PL}{4}$ (maximum).
At $x = L$: $M = 0$.

### Step 4: Point of Inflection Analysis

Setting $M(x) = 0$ in the first region: $\frac{P}{2} x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$.
Setting $M(x) = 0$ in the second region: $-\frac{P}{2} x + \frac{PL}{2} = 0 \Rightarrow x = L$.

Both solutions correspond to the supports. **No point of inflection within the span** (BM is sagging throughout).

---

## 3.4 CASE D: Simply Supported Beam — UDL $w$ over the Entire Span

**Setup:** SSB of span $L$ with a UDL of intensity $w$ (kN/m) over the full length.

### Step 1: Reactions

By symmetry:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

### Step 2: Shear Force at Section $x$ (from Left A)

$$V(x) = R_A - wx = \frac{wL}{2} - wx \quad \text{(linear)}$$

At $x = 0$: $V = +\frac{wL}{2}$.
At $x = L$: $V = -\frac{wL}{2}$.
$V = 0$ at $x = \frac{L}{2}$ (point of zero shear = point of maximum BM).

### Step 3: Bending Moment at Section $x$

The moment at distance $x$ is the moment of $R_A$ minus the moment of the UDL on length $x$.

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - wx \cdot \frac{x}{2} = \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{w x^2}{2} = \frac{w x}{2}(L - x) \quad \text{(parabolic, positive throughout)}$$

This can be verified:
$$M(x) = \frac{wx}{2}(L-x) = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

Differentiating: $\frac{dM}{dx} = \frac{w}{2}(L - 2x)$. Setting to zero: $x = L/2$.

Maximum BM:
$$M_{max} = \frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \left(L - \frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

### Step 4: Point of Inflection Analysis

$$M(x) = \frac{wx}{2}(L-x) = 0$$

This yields $x = 0$ or $x = L$ (the supports). No internal root since $(L - x) > 0$ for $0 < x < L$. **No point of inflection within the span.**

---

## 3.5 CASE E: Simply Supported Beam with Eccentric Point Load — Location of Point of Inflection

**Setup:** SSB of span $L$, point load $P$ at distance $a$ from A (so $b = L - a$ from B).

### Step 1: Reactions

Taking moments about B:
$$R_A \cdot L = P \cdot b \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_A = \frac{Pb}{L}$$
$$R_B = P - R_A = P - \frac{Pb}{L} = \frac{P(L - b)}{L} = \frac{Pa}{L}$$

### Step 2: Shear Force

Region 1 ($0 \le x < a$): $V = R_A = \frac{Pb}{L}$ (constant, positive).
Region 2 ($a < x \le L$): $V = R_A - P = \frac{Pb}{L} - P = -\frac{Pa}{L}$ (constant, negative).

### Step 3: Bending Moment

Region 1:
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{Pbx}{L}$$
At $x = a$: $M = \frac{Pab}{L}$ (this is the maximum BM under a point load — always when the load is eccentric).

Region 2:
$$M(x) = R_B \cdot (L - x) = \frac{Pa}{L}(L - x)$$
At $x = a$: $M = \frac{Pa}{L}(L - a) = \frac{Pab}{L}$ ✓ (continuity check).
At $x = L$: $M = 0$ ✓.

### Step 4: Point of Inflection

For both regions, $M(x) = 0$ only at $x = 0$ and $x = L$. **No point of inflection within the span** for a simple SSB with a single point load — moment is sagging throughout.

---

## 3.6 Bonus Derivation: Point of Inflection in a Propped Cantilever (UDL)

To illustrate the genuine point of inflection, consider a beam fixed at A and simply supported at B, span $L$, UDL $w$.

The fixed-end moment (using standard result):
$$M_A = -\frac{wL^2}{8} \quad \text{(hogging)}$$

Using superposition or the three-moment theorem:
$$M(x) = M_A\left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) + \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{w x^2}{2}$$
$$M(x) = -\frac{wL^2}{8}\left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) + \frac{wLx}{2} - \frac{w x^2}{2}$$

Setting $M(x) = 0$:
$$-\frac{wL^2}{8} + \frac{wLx}{8} + \frac{wLx}{2} - \frac{wx^2}{2} = 0$$

Multiply by $8/w$:
$$-L^2 + Lx + 4Lx - 4x^2 = 0$$
$$-L^2 + 5Lx - 4x^2 = 0$$
$$4x^2 - 5Lx + L^2 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:
$$x = \frac{5L \pm \sqrt{25L^2 - 16L^2}}{8} = \frac{5L \pm 3L}{8}$$

Two solutions: $x = L$ (the support) and $x = L/4$ (the **point of inflection** within the span).

This is the classic result: for a propped cantilever with UDL, **point of inflection occurs at $L/4$ from the fixed end**.
