# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Shear Force and Bending Moment with proper sign convention. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Shear Force (SF)** at a section of a beam is the algebraic sum of the transverse forces acting on either side of the section. It represents the tendency of one part of the beam to slide over the adjacent part.
>
> **Bending Moment (BM)** at a section is the algebraic sum of the moments of all the forces (about that section) acting on either side of the section. It represents the tendency of the beam to bend at that section.
>
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - SF is **positive** when the left portion tends to move **up** (or right portion tends to move **down**).
> - BM is **positive** when it produces **sagging** (concave up) — tension at the bottom fibre.
> - BM is **negative** when it produces **hogging** (concave down) — tension at the top fibre.

---

### Question 2: What is a Point of Inflection? Mention its significance in beam analysis. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **point of inflection** (also called a point of contraflexure) is a section of a beam where the **bending moment is zero** and the curvature of the deflected beam **changes sign** (i.e., the beam changes from sagging to hogging or vice versa).
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. At a point of inflection, the **extreme fibre stresses change from tensile to compressive** (or vice versa). This is critical in design of RC beams, where reinforcement may need to be curtailed or changed.
> 2. It defines the **boundary of the tension zone** in continuous or fixed beams.
> 3. It is essential for **boundary conditions** when integrating the beam equation $EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$.
>
> For simple cantilevers and simply supported beams under standard point/UDL loads, the BM is of a single sign in the span, so **no internal point of inflection exists**; it appears only in overhanging, propped, or continuous beams.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
> A cantilever beam of length 6 m is fixed at end A and free at end B. It carries a point load of 30 kN at the free end and a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over the entire length.
>
> **(a)** Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) showing all critical values. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the point of inflection (if any) and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a): SFD and BMD**

**Step 1: Calculate Total Load and Reaction at A**
- Point load at B: $P = 30$ kN (downward)
- UDL: $w = 10$ kN/m over $L = 6$ m, total $= 10 \times 6 = 60$ kN
- Total downward load: $30 + 60 = 90$ kN
- Reaction at A (vertical): $R_A = 90$ kN (upward)
- Fixing moment at A: 
$$M_A = 30 \times 6 + 60 \times 3 = 180 + 180 = 360 \text{ kN·m (counter-clockwise)}$$

**Step 2: Shear Force at Section $x$ (measured from free end B)**

Considering forces to the right of the cut:
- Point load contribution: $-30$ kN (constant)
- UDL contribution over length $x$: $-10x$ kN

$$V(x) = -30 - 10x \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le 6 \text{ m}$$

At $x = 0$ (free end): $V = -30$ kN.
At $x = 6^-$ (just before wall): $V = -30 - 60 = -90$ kN.

**Step 3: Bending Moment at Section $x$ (measured from free end B)**

$$M(x) = -30 \cdot x - 10x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = -30x - 5x^2$$

At $x = 0$: $M = 0$.
At $x = 6$ (fixed end): $M = -30(6) - 5(36) = -180 - 180 = -360$ kN·m.

**Step 4: Diagrams (Critical Values Table)**

| Section (from B) | SF (kN) | BM (kN·m) |
|---|---|---|
| $x = 0$ (free end B) | $-30$ | $0$ |
| $x = 3$ (midspan) | $-30 - 30 = -60$ | $-30(3) - 5(9) = -90 - 45 = -135$ |
| $x = 6$ (fixed end A) | $-90$ | $-360$ |

SFD: Linear, going from $-30$ kN to $-90$ kN.
BMD: Parabolic (concave down with negative ordinate), going from $0$ to $-360$ kN·m.

**Part (b): Point of Inflection**

Set $M(x) = 0$:
$$-30x - 5x^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -5x(6 + x) = 0$$

Solutions: $x = 0$ or $x = -6$ (rejected, as $x \ge 0$).

The only valid solution is $x = 0$ (the free end). **No point of inflection within the span** because the bending moment is hogging (negative) throughout the length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** The absence of a point of inflection confirms that the entire top fibre is in tension along the beam length. In RC design, this means top reinforcement (hogging steel) must run the **full length** of the cantilever; it cannot be curtailed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
> A simply supported beam of span 8 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over the entire span and a concentrated load of 60 kN at a distance of 2 m from the left support.
>
> **(a)** Determine the support reactions and draw the SFD and BMD for the beam. Indicate all principal values. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the maximum bending moment and the location where it occurs. Check for the existence of a point of inflection. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a): Reactions, SFD, and BMD**

**Step 1: Calculate Reactions**

Total UDL: $20 \times 8 = 160$ kN acting at midspan ($4$ m from A).
Point load: $P = 60$ kN at $2$ m from A.

Taking moments about A:
$$R_B \times 8 = 160 \times 4 + 60 \times 2 = 640 + 120 = 760$$
$$R_B = 95 \text{ kN (upward)}$$

Vertical equilibrium:
$$R_A = 160 + 60 - 95 = 125 \text{ kN (upward)}$$

**Step 2: Shear Force at Distance $x$ from A**

**Region 1:** $0 \le x < 2$ m (before the point load)
$$V(x) = 125 - 20x$$

At $x = 0$: $V = +125$ kN.
At $x = 2^-$: $V = 125 - 40 = +85$ kN.

**At $x = 2$ m:** Jump of $-60$ kN due to point load → $V = +85 - 60 = +25$ kN.

**Region 2:** $2 \le x \le 8$ m (after the point load)
$$V(x) = 125 - 20x - 60 = 65 - 20x$$

At $x = 2^+$: $V = 65 - 40 = +25$ kN ✓.
At $x = 8$: $V = 65 - 160 = -95$ kN ✓ (matches $-R_B$).

**Zero Shear Location:**
$$65 - 20x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 3.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Bending Moment**

**Region 1:** $0 \le x \le 2$
$$M(x) = 125x - 10x^2$$
At $x = 0$: $M = 0$.
At $x = 2$: $M = 250 - 40 = 210$ kN·m.

**Region 2:** $2 \le x \le 8$
$$M(x) = 125x - 10x^2 - 60(x - 2) = 65x - 10x^2 + 120$$

At $x = 2$: $M = 130 - 40 + 120 = 210$ kN·m ✓.
At $x = 3.25$: $M = 65(3.25) - 10(3.25)^2 + 120 = 211.25 - 105.625 + 120 = 225.625$ kN·m.
At $x = 8$: $M = 520 - 640 + 120 = 0$ ✓.

**Part (b): Maximum BM and Point of Inflection**

- **Maximum BM** = $225.625$ kN·m at $x = 3.25$ m from A.

- **Point of Inflection Check:** In both regions, $M(x) > 0$ for $0 < x < 8$. The moment is **sagging throughout the span**. No sign change, so **no point of inflection within the span**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign Convention Slip:** Students frequently forget to add the jump in SFD at the point load. Always account for concentrated loads as **vertical jumps** in the SFD.
> 2. **Location of Max BM:** The maximum BM is **NOT** always at midspan for UDL + point load combinations. It occurs where $V = 0$, which must be found by solving $V(x) = 0$ **after** accounting for the point load jump.
> 3. **Parabola Direction:** The BMD under a downward UDL is **concave downward** (cup-shaped, $\smile$), with vertex at the section of maximum BM. Drawing it as concave up is a common error that costs full marks.
> 4. **Units in Final Answer:** Always state $V$ in kN and $M$ in kN·m — units carry marks in KTU papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — SF/BMD Quick-Fire Revision List**
>
> - **Beam Types**: Cantilever (fixed + moment reaction); Simply Supported (two vertical reactions only).
>
> - **Sign Convention**: Upward SF = Positive; Sagging BM = Positive.
>
> - **Key Differential Relationships**:
>   - $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$
>   - $\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$
>
> - **Diagram Shapes**:
>   - Point load → SFD: vertical jump; BMD: straight line (triangle).
>   - UDL → SFD: linear; BMD: parabolic (2nd degree).
>
> - **Critical Locations**:
>   - Max BM occurs where $V = 0$ OR at a point load / fixed end.
>
> - **Standard Results to Memorize**:
>   - Cantilever with point load $P$ at tip: $\vert M_{max} \vert = PL$.
>   - Cantilever with UDL $w$: $\vert M_{max} \vert = \frac{wL^2}{2}$.
>   - SSB with point load at center: $M_{max} = \frac{PL}{4}$.
>   - SSB with UDL: $M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$ at center.
>   - SSB with eccentric point load: $M_{max} = \frac{Pab}{L}$ at the load point.
>
> - **Point of Inflection (POI)**:
>   - Defined as the point where $M(x) = 0$ AND the curvature changes sign.
>   - For simple Cantilever / SSB (with point load or UDL), **no POI within the span** — the BM is of single sign.
>   - POI **does occur** in overhanging, propped, fixed, and continuous beams.
>   - **Classic result**: Propped cantilever with UDL → POI at $L/4$ from fixed end.
>
> - **SFD Jump Magnitude** = Magnitude of the point load.
>
> - **BMD Slope** at any section = Shear force at that section.
>
> - **Free End Conditions** (Cantilever): $V = 0$ only if no load applied at free end; $M = 0$ always.
>
> - **Roller/Pin End Conditions** (SSB): $V \ne 0$ generally; $M = 0$ always at simple supports.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pure Bending: Flexure Formula for Beams, Assumptions and Limitations, Section Modulus, Flexural Rigidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pure Bending: Flexure Formula for Beams, Assumptions and Limitations, Section Modulus, Flexural Rigidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Pure Bending

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure Bending (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *Pure bending* is a specific condition of flexural loading in which the **bending moment remains constant along the length of the beam segment**, while the **shear force is identically zero** ($V = 0$) across that region. Under pure bending, a straight beam deforms into a circular arc, and every cross-section rotates through the same angle relative to its original orientation, while remaining plane and normal to the deformed neutral surface.

The governing mathematical condition for pure bending is therefore:

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad M = \text{constant}$$

A practical engineering example of near-pure bending is the section of a beam located **between the two concentrated loads in a four-point bending test**, or the central span region of a simply supported beam under uniformly distributed load where the shear force changes sign (inflection of shear diagram = zero shear location).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bending a Ruler" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a flexible plastic ruler held between your two hands, palms up, with both hands pushing the ends inward equally. The middle portion of the ruler curves into a smooth arc. Notice that:
> - The top surface of the ruler **stretches** (gets longer) → fibres in **tension**.
> - The bottom surface of the ruler **compresses** (gets shorter) → fibres in **compression**.
> - Somewhere inside, there is a layer that **does not change length at all** → the **Neutral Axis / Neutral Surface**.
> - The cross-sections themselves do not warp; they remain flat and only rotate.
>
> This is exactly what happens in a beam under pure bending. The "stretching" and "squashing" of longitudinal fibres creates internal **bending stress** ($\sigma$), and the resistance of the material to this stretching is quantified by Young's Modulus ($E$).

## 1.3 The Flexure Formula — Preview

The **Flexure Formula** (also called the **Bending Equation**) is the foundational relationship that links the internal bending moment to the induced bending stress at any point in a beam cross-section:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

Where the symbols have standard, KTU-accepted meanings:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $M$ | Bending moment at the section | $\text{N·m}$ |
| $I$ | Area moment of inertia of the cross-section about the Neutral Axis (NA) | $\text{m}^4$ |
| $\sigma$ | Bending (flexural) stress at a distance $y$ from NA | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ |
| $y$ | Perpendicular distance from the Neutral Axis to the fibre of interest | $\text{m}$ |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus of the beam material | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ |
| $R$ | Radius of curvature of the bent beam (deformed neutral axis) | $\text{m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:**
> The Flexure Formula is the **single most tested equation** in Module 3 of PCMUT402. Expect at least one full 14-mark derivation question and multiple 3-mark direct formula applications in every KTU University Examination cycle.

## 1.4 Section Modulus — The "Strength Shortcut"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Modulus Definition (KTU Board-Examiner phrasing):**
> *Section Modulus* is defined as the **ratio of the area moment of inertia ($I$) of a beam cross-section about its neutral axis to the maximum distance ($y_{max}$) of any fibre in the cross-section from the neutral axis.**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

It is a purely **geometric property** of the cross-section (units: $\text{m}^3$ or $\text{mm}^3$) that quantifies the **strength efficiency** of a beam shape in resisting bending. A higher $Z$ means the beam can carry a larger moment for the same maximum allowable stress. The maximum bending stress can then be written in the compact form:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

## 1.5 Flexural Rigidity — The "Stiffness Shortcut"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexural Rigidity Definition:**
> *Flexural Rigidity* is the **product of Young's Modulus ($E$) and the area moment of inertia ($I$)** of the beam cross-section about its neutral axis. It is denoted as $EI$ and has units of $\text{N·m}^2$.

$$EI = \text{Flexural Rigidity}$$

A higher $EI$ means the beam will deflect *less* under a given bending moment — it is the beam's **resistance to bending deformation**. Flexural rigidity is central to the **deflection equations** of beams (Module 4 and 5 of PCMUT402).

> [!NOTE]
> **Section Modulus vs. Flexural Rigidity — Examiner's Favourite Trap:**
> Students frequently confuse the two. Remember:
> - **Section Modulus ($Z = I/y_{max}$)** → governs **STRENGTH** (stress / failure).
> - **Flexural Rigidity ($EI$)** → governs **STIFFNESS** (deflection / deformation).
> - Both use $I$, but $Z$ also includes $y_{max}$ and $EI$ also includes $E$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of the Pure Bending Theory (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory)

The flexure formula rests on a clearly defined set of kinematic and material assumptions. The KTU 2024 examiner expects the student to **list and explain these assumptions verbatim** in any 14-mark derivation question.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Cardinal Assumptions of Pure Bending Theory:**

1. **The beam material is homogeneous and isotropic.**
   Homogeneous = same composition at every point. Isotropic = same elastic properties in every direction at a point. This allows us to assume a single, direction-independent value of Young's Modulus $E$.

2. **The beam material obeys Hooke's Law within the elastic limit.**
   The stress is directly proportional to strain: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$. No plastic deformation occurs.

3. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral surface after bending.**
   This is the **kinematic assumption** due to Bernoulli. It implies that cross-sections do not warp; they only translate and rotate.

4. **The beam is initially straight and has a uniform cross-section along its length.**
   Prismatic beam assumption — required for $I$ to be constant.

5. **The radius of curvature $R$ is large compared to the cross-sectional dimensions.**
   This is a **geometrically linear** (small-deflection) assumption that allows us to neglect the square of the slope $(dy/dx)^2$ in the curvature expression.

6. **Self-weight of the beam is neglected unless explicitly included.**
   Bending moment is considered to be caused by the externally applied transverse loads only.

## 2.2 Limitations of the Flexure Formula

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pitfall — Limitations Must Be Memorised:**
> The flexure formula is **NOT** universally valid. The following conditions violate the underlying assumptions and render the formula inapplicable or approximate:

- **Non-linear materials:** Plastics, concrete (uncracked), rubber, and wood (across the grain) — Hooke's law does not hold.
- **Beams with shear-dominated deformation:** Short, deep beams (span-to-depth ratio $< 5$) — shear deformation becomes significant. Timoshenko beam theory is needed.
- **Non-prismatic (tapered) beams:** $I$ varies along the length; the simple form $\sigma = My/I$ requires modification.
- **Curved beams:** Beams whose initial centreline is curved (hooks, crane arms) — the neutral axis does **not** coincide with the centroidal axis; curved-beam formula by Winkler is used.
- **Composite / non-homogeneous beams:** Sandwich beams, reinforced concrete beams — transformed-section or layered analysis is required.
- **Unsymmetrical bending:** When the load plane does not contain a principal axis — biaxial bending formula must be used.
- **Plastic / inelastic region:** Beyond the yield stress, the elastic flexure formula is invalid; plastic moment capacity governs.
- **Points of stress concentration:** Near holes, notches, or sudden changes in cross-section — local stresses are not predicted by the simple flexure formula.

## 2.3 The Six-Part Logical Derivation Roadmap (How to Write the Answer in the Exam)

The flexure formula is derived by combining **geometry of deformation**, **kinematics of strain**, and **constitutive law (Hooke's law)**. The KTU 2024 marker will look for these six steps in order:

| Step | Logic Block | Governing Equation / Concept |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Step 1** | Geometry of deformed beam: $CD$ is a fibre at distance $y$ above NA, $AB$ is the NA itself. Original length of both = $dx$. Deformed lengths: $AB' = R\,d\theta$, $CD' = (R+y)\,d\theta$. | Length change of fibre $CD$ = $y\,d\theta$. |
| **Step 2** | Definition of strain in fibre $CD$ (kinematics): $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{change in length}}{\text{original length}}$. | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y\,d\theta}{dx}$ |
| **Step 3** | Curvature of the neutral axis: From the geometry, $dx = R\,d\theta$, so $d\theta/dx = 1/R$. | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ |
| **Step 4** | Apply Hooke's Law: Stress in fibre is $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. | $\sigma = E\cdot \dfrac{y}{R}$ |
| **Step 5** | Internal force and moment equilibrium: The bending stress $\sigma$ on elemental area $dA$ produces an elemental force $\sigma\,dA$. Sum of all such forces = 0 (no net axial force). Sum of moments about NA = applied bending moment $M$. | $\int \sigma\,dA = 0$, $\int \sigma\,y\,dA = M$ |
| **Step 6** | Combine Steps 4 and 5 to obtain the **Flexure Formula**. | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every high-yield equation for Module 3 of PCMUT402. This is your one-stop reference.**

| # | Formula | Name / Description | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | **Flexure Formula** (Bending Equation) | Master relation for bending stress, curvature, and moment. |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M\,y}{I}$ | Bending stress at distance $y$ from NA | Finding stress at a specific fibre. |
| 3 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{M\,y_{max}}{I}$ | Maximum bending stress | Design check: $\sigma_{max} \le \sigma_{allowable}$. |
| 4 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | **Section Modulus** | Beam selection from standard tables. |
| 5 | $M = \sigma_{max}\cdot Z$ | Bending moment capacity | Strength-based design. |
| 6 | $EI$ | **Flexural Rigidity** | Appears in all beam-deflection equations. |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Curvature of the bent beam | Deflection slope integration base. |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{EI}{M}$ | Radius of curvature | Geometric understanding of bending. |
| 9 | $\theta = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{ML}{EI}$ | Angle of bending (radians) over length $L$ | For uniform $M$ over segment. |
| 10 | $I_{rect} = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ | Moment of inertia — rectangle ($b$ = width, $h$ = height) | Most common beam shape. |
| 11 | $I_{circle} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | Moment of inertia — solid circular section | Shafts, pins. |
| 12 | $I_{hollow\,circle} = \dfrac{\pi (D^4-d^4)}{64}$ | Moment of inertia — hollow circular section | Hollow shafts, pipes. |
| 13 | $Z_{rect} = \dfrac{bh^2}{6}$ | Section modulus — rectangle | Quick design of rectangular beams. |
| 14 | $Z_{circle} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$ | Section modulus — solid circle | Quick design of circular shafts. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Convention Used in the Table Above:**
> All absolute-value / distance notation in the table has been written using clean math characters. In your exam scripts, prefer the **virgule** notation $\vert y \vert$ or simply state the *distance* explicitly to avoid markdown-rendering issues.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Flexure Formula

The flexure formula is the workhorse equation in every branch of structural and mechanical engineering:

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of reinforced-concrete beams, steel I-girders in buildings and bridges, wooden joists in residential construction.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of machine shafts, crane jibs, leaf springs, railway axles, helicopter rotor blades.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Sizing of wing spars, fuselage longerons, helicopter rotor masts.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Analysis of bone (femur) as a curved beam, dental implant loading, orthopaedic fixation devices.
- **Marine Engineering:** Hull girder bending analysis of ships under wave loading.

The single most important design equation derived from the flexure formula is the **Section Modulus Equation** $M_{allow} = \sigma_{allow}\cdot Z$, which forms the basis of every standard beam-design code (IS 800 for steel, IS 456 for RCC, AISC, Eurocode 3, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flexure Formula (Bending Equation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Setup:** Consider a small element of a beam of length $dx$ subjected to a **constant pure bending moment** $M$. The element is taken from a region of pure bending (where shear force $V = 0$).

### **Step 1 — Geometry of the Deformed Beam (Kinematic Block)**

Consider a longitudinal segment of the beam of original length $dx$. Let:

- $AB$ be the portion of the **Neutral Axis** within this element.
- $CD$ be a longitudinal fibre at a perpendicular distance $y$ **above** the Neutral Axis.

Before bending, both $AB$ and $CD$ have the same length, namely $dx$.

After bending, the beam deforms into a circular arc of radius $R$. The neutral axis (which does not change length) becomes the arc $AB'$ of radius $R$. The fibre $CD$, at distance $y$ from the NA, becomes the arc $CD'$ of radius $(R + y)$.

The angle subtended at the centre of curvature by both arcs is the same small angle $d\theta$.

Therefore:

$$\text{Deformed length of NA: } AB' = R\,d\theta$$

$$\text{Original length of NA: } AB = dx$$

$$\text{Deformed length of fibre } CD: CD' = (R + y)\,d\theta$$

$$\text{Original length of fibre } CD: CD = dx$$

### **Step 2 — Change in Length of the Fibre $CD$**

The change in length of fibre $CD$ is the difference between its deformed length and its original length:

$$\Delta L_{CD} = CD' - CD = (R + y)\,d\theta - dx$$

But since $AB$ also equals $dx$, and $AB' = R\,d\theta$ also equals $dx$ (because the NA does not stretch), we have:

$$dx = R\,d\theta$$

Substituting this into the expression for $\Delta L_{CD}$:

$$\Delta L_{CD} = (R + y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta$$

$$\Delta L_{CD} = R\,d\theta + y\,d\theta - R\,d\theta$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L_{CD} = y\,d\theta}$$

### **Step 3 — Strain in the Fibre $CD$ (Kinematics → Strain)**

By definition, the longitudinal strain in a fibre is the change in length divided by the original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L_{CD}}{CD} = \frac{\Delta L_{CD}}{dx}$$

Substituting $\Delta L_{CD} = y\,d\theta$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{y\,d\theta}{dx}$$

But from Step 2, $R\,d\theta = dx$, so $d\theta = dx/R$. Substituting:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{y}{dx}\cdot\frac{dx}{R}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{y}{R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Strain is **linearly proportional to the distance $y$ from the neutral axis**. Fibres farther from the NA experience more strain (more stretching or compressing).

### **Step 4 — Stress in the Fibre $CD$ (Hooke's Law — Constitutive Block)**

By **Assumption 2** (Hooke's Law holds in the elastic range):

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting the strain from Step 3:

$$\boxed{\sigma = E \cdot \frac{y}{R} = \frac{E\,y}{R}}$$

This is the **bending stress** in a fibre at distance $y$ from the NA.

### **Step 5 — Internal Force and Moment Equilibrium (Static Block)**

Consider an elemental area $dA$ of the cross-section at distance $y$ from the NA. The stress $\sigma$ on this area produces an elemental axial force:

$$dF = \sigma \cdot dA$$

This force is directed along the longitudinal axis (along the beam) — it is a **tangential** (axial) force, not transverse.

**Equilibrium Condition 1 — No Net Axial Force:**

Pure bending is caused by a couple (moment) only; there should be **no net axial (longitudinal) force** on the cross-section. Therefore:

$$\int_A \sigma\,dA = 0$$

Substituting $\sigma = Ey/R$:

$$\int_A \frac{E\,y}{R}\,dA = 0$$

Since $E$ and $R$ are constants (homogeneous material, pure bending ⇒ constant curvature):

$$\frac{E}{R}\int_A y\,dA = 0$$

$$\int_A y\,dA = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conclusion (often asked separately as a 3-mark question):**
> The condition $\int_A y\,dA = 0$ defines the **Neutral Axis**. Since $\int_A y\,dA$ is the first moment of area about the axis, this equation means the NA passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section. Therefore, the **neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis** of the cross-section for a beam in pure bending (symmetric bending).

**Equilibrium Condition 2 — Moment Equilibrium:**

The moment about the NA produced by all elemental forces $dF = \sigma\,dA$ acting at lever arm $y$ must equal the applied bending moment $M$:

$$M = \int_A (\sigma \cdot y)\,dA = \int_A \sigma\,y\,dA$$

Substituting $\sigma = Ey/R$:

$$M = \int_A \frac{E\,y}{R}\cdot y\,dA = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y^2\,dA$$

By definition, $\int_A y^2\,dA$ is the **second moment of area** (area moment of inertia) about the neutral axis, denoted $I$. Therefore:

$$M = \frac{E\,I}{R}$$

### **Step 6 — The Flexure Formula — Final Combination**

From Step 4: $\sigma = Ey/R$, which gives $E/R = \sigma/y$.

From Step 5: $E/R = M/I$.

Equating these two expressions for $E/R$:

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}}$$

This is the **Flexure Formula** for pure bending. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Used (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> - Positive $M$ → sagging beam → **tension below** NA, **compression above** NA.
> - Distance $y$ is measured from the NA, taken positive in the direction of the fibre.
> - Stress $\sigma$ is taken positive in **tension** and negative in **compression**.
> - Always state the sign convention explicitly at the start of any derivation in the KTU exam script.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Curvature Equation

Starting from the flexure formula $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

This is the **fundamental curvature equation** of beam theory. It tells us that:

- Curvature $\dfrac{1}{R}$ is **directly proportional** to the bending moment $M$.
- Curvature is **inversely proportional** to flexural rigidity $EI$.

In differential form, using the exact expression for curvature of a slender beam:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

By **Assumption 5** (small deflections, slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \ll 1$), the denominator is approximately unity:

$$\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

Therefore the **governing differential equation of the elastic curve** is:

$$\boxed{EI\,\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

This is the starting point of every **deflection problem** in Module 4 of PCMUT402.

## 3.3 Section Modulus — Formal Derivation

The maximum bending stress in a cross-section occurs at the fibre farthest from the neutral axis. Let $y_{max}$ be this maximum distance (for a symmetric section, $y_{max} = h/2$, where $h$ is the overall depth).

From the flexure formula:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M\,y_{max}}{I}$$

Rearranging:

$$M = \frac{I}{y_{max}}\cdot\sigma_{max}$$

The factor $\dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ is defined as the **Section Modulus** $Z$:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}}$$

The maximum bending stress can then be written in the compact **strength form**:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $Z$ important?** The bending *strength* of a beam depends only on $\sigma_{max}$ and $Z$. Material and cross-section selection in design codes is therefore based on the lookup of $Z$ for standard rolled sections (I-beams, channels, angles, hollow sections).

## 3.4 Flexural Rigidity — Concept and Derivation

The product $EI$ appears naturally in Step 5 of the flexure-formula derivation. Its units:

$$[E] = \text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2 \quad;\quad [I] = \text{m}^4$$

$$[EI] = \text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^4 = \text{N·m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering meaning of $EI$:**
> $EI$ is the **moment required to produce a unit curvature** in the beam. The deflection of a beam under any given loading is **inversely proportional** to $EI$. A beam with high $EI$ (stiff material + large moment of inertia) deflects very little; a beam with low $EI$ (compliant material + thin section) deflects a lot.

**Worked Mini-Example (Curvature):** A steel beam ($E = 200$ GPa) of rectangular cross-section $50$ mm $\times 100$ mm carries a constant pure bending moment of $M = 5$ kN·m. Find the radius of curvature.

Step 1: Moment of inertia about NA (NA is at mid-height for symmetric section):

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12} = \frac{(0.050)(0.100)^3}{12} = \frac{0.050 \times 10^{-3}}{12}$$

$$I = 4.1667 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^4$$

Step 2: Flexural rigidity:

$$EI = (200 \times 10^9)(4.1667 \times 10^{-6}) = 8.333 \times 10^5\ \text{N·m}^2$$

Step 3: Radius of curvature:

$$R = \frac{EI}{M} = \frac{8.333 \times 10^5}{5000} = 166.67\ \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A radius of curvature of nearly 167 metres confirms Assumption 5: the beam bends very gently, justifying the small-deflection, large-$R$ simplification.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Frequently Asked Numericals

| # | Problem Type | Governing Formula | Typical Inputs |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stress at a given fibre | $\sigma = My/I$ | $M$, $I$, $y$ |
| 2 | Maximum stress | $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$ | $M$, $Z$ |
| 3 | Section modulus required | $Z_{req} = M/\sigma_{allow}$ | $M$, $\sigma_{allow}$ |
| 4 | Radius of curvature | $R = EI/M$ | $E$, $I$, $M$ |
| 5 | Curvature (slope) | $1/R = M/(EI)$ | $M$, $E$, $I$ |
| 6 | Design of rectangular beam | $bh^2/6 \ge M/\sigma_{allow}$ | $M$, $\sigma_{allow}$, $b$ or $h$ |
| 7 | Comparison of beam strengths | Compare $Z$ values for same $M$ | $Z_1$, $Z_2$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — Pure Bending Segment with Curvature Geometry

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the kinematic setup of pure bending, mapping the geometric relationships between the original beam element, the deformed arc, the neutral axis, and a typical fibre at distance $y$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Original Beam Element of Length dx"] --> node2["Neutral Axis AB"]
    node1 --> node3["Fibre CD at Distance y above NA"]
    node2 --> node4["After Bending: NA forms Arc AB prime of Radius R"]
    node3 --> node5["After Bending: Fibre CD forms Arc CD prime of Radius R + y"]
    node4 --> node6["Subtended Angle dtheta at Centre of Curvature O"]
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7["Strain in Fibre CD = y dtheta divided by dx = y over R"]
    node7 --> node8["Apply Hooke Law: sigma = E times y over R"]
    node8 --> node9["Apply Moment Equilibrium: M = EI over R"]
    node9 --> node10["Final Flexure Formula: M over I = sigma over y = E over R"]
    node1 --> node11["Pure Bending Condition: V = 0, M = constant"]
    node11 --> node2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of the Flexure Formula

The flexure formula is a **three-pillar structure** that bridges three different sub-domains of mechanics. The diagram below maps these three pillars and their interaction:

```mermaid
graph LR
    pillar1["Pillar 1: Geometry of Deformation - Strain equals y over R"]
    pillar2["Pillar 2: Constitutive Law - Hooke's Law sigma = E times epsilon"]
    pillar3["Pillar 3: Static Equilibrium - Moment M equals Integral of sigma y dA"]

    pillar1 --> junction["Combined Flexure Formula M over I equals sigma over y equals E over R"]
    pillar2 --> junction
    pillar3 --> junction

    junction --> output1["Output A: Bending Stress sigma equals M y over I"]
    junction --> output2["Output B: Curvature 1 over R equals M over EI"]
    junction --> output3["Output C: Strength M equals sigma allow times Z"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Bending Stress Distribution Across Section Depth

The bending stress $\sigma$ varies **linearly** across the depth of the cross-section, being zero at the neutral axis and reaching a maximum magnitude at the outermost fibres. The diagram below depicts this linear distribution for a rectangular beam under sagging (positive) moment.

```mermaid
graph TD
    topComp["Top Fibre: Maximum Compression sigma_c"]
    upLinear["Linear Variation: sigma equals M y over I"]
    midZero["Neutral Axis NA: sigma equals 0"]
    downLinear["Linear Variation: sigma equals M y over I"]
    bottomTen["Bottom Fibre: Maximum Tension sigma_t"]

    topComp --> upLinear
    upLinear --> midZero
    midZero --> downLinear
    downLinear --> bottomTen

    neutralAxisCheck["Neutral Axis Location: First Moment of Area equals 0 → NA passes through Centroid"]
    midZero --> neutralAxisCheck
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flow — From Section Modulus to Strength Design

```mermaid
graph TD
    startInput["Given: Bending Moment M and Allowable Stress sigma_allow"]
    computeZ["Compute Section Modulus Z required: Z req equals M divided by sigma_allow"]
    selectSection["Select Standard Section with Z actual greater than or equal to Z req"]
    checkDef["Check Deflection: delta_actual less than or equal to delta_allow using EI"]
    finalDesign["Final Beam Design: M over Z less than or equal to sigma_allow and EI large enough"]

    startInput --> computeZ
    computeZ --> selectSection
    selectSection --> checkDef
    checkDef --> finalDesign
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> The Mermaid block diagrams above map the **logical flow of derivation and design** rather than attempting to draw physical cross-sections. This is intentional — pure Mermaid cannot accurately render physical geometry, so the focus is on functional architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Similar Style]**

### **Q1. Define pure bending. State the assumptions made in the theory of pure bending.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Pure Bending:** Pure bending is a condition of flexural loading in which the **bending moment is constant** along the length of the beam segment and the **shear force is zero** in that region. Mathematically, $V = 0$ and $M = \text{constant}$.
>
> **Assumptions of Pure Bending Theory:**
> 1. The beam material is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
> 2. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
> 3. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and normal to the neutral surface after bending** (Bernoulli's hypothesis).
> 4. The beam is **initially straight** and has a **uniform cross-section** (prismatic).
> 5. The **radius of curvature $R$ is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions (small-deflection assumption).
> 6. The **self-weight of the beam is neglected**.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for assumptions, or 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming 4 assumptions. KTU examiners typically expect a minimum of 4–5 named assumptions.)*

---

### **Q2. Define section modulus and flexural rigidity. State their units and physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Section Modulus ($Z$):** It is the ratio of the area moment of inertia $I$ of a beam cross-section about the neutral axis to the maximum distance $y_{max}$ of any fibre from the neutral axis:
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$
> **Units:** $\text{m}^3$ (or $\text{mm}^3$).
> **Significance:** It is a purely geometric property that quantifies the strength of a beam section in resisting bending. A higher $Z$ means a lower maximum bending stress for the same applied moment.
>
> **Flexural Rigidity ($EI$):** It is the product of Young's Modulus $E$ and the area moment of inertia $I$ of the cross-section about the neutral axis.
> **Units:** $\text{N·m}^2$.
> **Significance:** It represents the resistance of the beam to **bending deformation** (i.e., it controls the deflection of the beam). A higher $EI$ results in smaller deflection under the same loading.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark for each definition + unit, 1 mark for combined significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — OR — Question B (14 Marks)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation-Focused**

**(a)** *Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam subjected to pure bending, clearly stating all assumptions. [7 marks]*

**(b)** *A simply supported beam of span $L = 4$ m carries a point load of $50$ kN at its midspan. The beam is of rectangular cross-section, $120$ mm wide and $240$ mm deep. Find:*
- *(i) The maximum bending stress in the beam.* [3 marks]
- *(ii) The radius of curvature of the bent beam at the section of maximum moment. Take $E = 200$ GPa.* [4 marks]

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Solution to Part (a) — Full Derivation:**

> **Assumptions of Pure Bending Theory (state at the very beginning):**
> 1. Homogeneous and isotropic material.
> 2. Hooke's Law holds within the elastic limit.
> 3. Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
> 4. The beam is initially straight and prismatic.
> 5. The radius of curvature is large compared to the cross-sectional dimensions.
> 6. Self-weight is neglected.
>
> **Derivation:**
>
> Consider an elemental segment of length $dx$ of a beam under pure bending moment $M$. Let $AB$ be the neutral axis and $CD$ be a fibre at distance $y$ above the neutral axis. After bending, both subtend an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature $O$.
>
> **Lengths:**
> - $AB = dx$
> - $AB' = R\,d\theta$
> - $CD = dx$
> - $CD' = (R + y)\,d\theta$
>
> Since the neutral axis does not change length:
> $$dx = R\,d\theta$$
>
> **Change in length of fibre $CD$:**
> $$\Delta L = CD' - CD = (R + y)d\theta - R\,d\theta = y\,d\theta$$
>
> **Strain in fibre $CD$:**
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{dx} = \frac{y\,d\theta}{dx} = \frac{y}{R}$$
>
> **Stress by Hooke's Law:**
> $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{E\,y}{R} \quad \text{...(i)}$$
>
> **Equilibrium — No net axial force:**
> $$\int_A \sigma\,dA = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_A \frac{E\,y}{R}\,dA = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_A y\,dA = 0$$
> This shows that the **NA passes through the centroid** of the section.
>
> **Equilibrium — Moment:**
> $$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \,dA = \int_A \frac{E\,y}{R} \cdot y\,dA = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y^2\,dA = \frac{EI}{R}$$
> Hence:
> $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$
>
> **Combining (i) and (ii):**
> $$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}} \quad \text{(Flexure Formula)}$$

**Solution to Part (b) — Numerical:**

> **Step 1: Maximum bending moment.**
> For a simply supported beam with central point load $P$:
> $$M_{max} = \frac{P L}{4} = \frac{50 \times 4}{4} = 50\ \text{kN·m} = 50{,}000\ \text{N·m}$$
>
> **Step 2: Section properties.**
> $$b = 120\ \text{mm} = 0.120\ \text{m}, \quad h = 240\ \text{mm} = 0.240\ \text{m}$$
> $$I = \frac{b h^3}{12} = \frac{(0.120)(0.240)^3}{12} = \frac{(0.120)(0.013824)}{12} = 1.3824 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^4$$
> $$y_{max} = \frac{h}{2} = 0.120\ \text{m}$$
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{1.3824 \times 10^{-4}}{0.120} = 1.152 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$$
>
> **Part (b)(i) — Maximum bending stress:**
> $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.152 \times 10^{-3}} = 43.40 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}$$
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 43.4\ \text{MPa}}$$
>
> **Part (b)(ii) — Radius of curvature at maximum moment section:**
> $$EI = (200 \times 10^{9})(1.3824 \times 10^{-4}) = 27.648 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N·m}^2$$
> $$R = \frac{EI}{M} = \frac{27.648 \times 10^{6}}{50{,}000} = 552.96\ \text{m}$$
> $$\boxed{R \approx 553\ \text{m}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Design and Application-Focused**

**(a)** *Define the term **Section Modulus**. Derive the expression $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$ for a beam in pure bending, starting from the flexure formula.* [7 marks]

**(b)** *A cast iron beam of rectangular cross-section $60$ mm wide and $120$ mm deep is simply supported over a span of $3$ m. It carries a uniformly distributed load of $12$ kN/m over the entire span. Find:*
- *(i) The maximum tensile and maximum compressive stresses in the beam.* [3 marks]
- *(ii) If the allowable tensile and compressive stresses for cast iron are $30$ MPa and $90$ MPa respectively, check whether the section is safe.* [4 marks]

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Solution to Part (a) — Definition + Derivation:**

> **Definition:** Section modulus $Z$ of a beam cross-section is the ratio of the area moment of inertia $I$ about the neutral axis to the maximum distance $y_{max}$ of the outermost fibre from the neutral axis:
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$
>
> **Derivation of $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$:**
> From the flexure formula:
> $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = \frac{M\,y}{I}$$
>
> Bending stress $\sigma$ is **maximum** at the fibre farthest from the neutral axis, i.e., at $y = y_{max}$:
> $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = M \cdot \frac{y_{max}}{I} = \frac{M}{I / y_{max}} = \frac{M}{Z}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}}$$

**Solution to Part (b) — Numerical:**

> **Step 1: Maximum bending moment.**
> For a simply supported beam with UDL $w$:
> $$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{(12)(3)^2}{8} = \frac{108}{8} = 13.5\ \text{kN·m} = 13{,}500\ \text{N·m}$$
>
> **Step 2: Section properties.**
> $$b = 60\ \text{mm}, \quad h = 120\ \text{mm}, \quad y_{max} = h/2 = 60\ \text{mm} = 0.060\ \text{m}$$
> $$I = \frac{b h^3}{12} = \frac{(60)(120)^3}{12} = \frac{(60)(1{,}728{,}000)}{12} = 8.64 \times 10^{6}\ \text{mm}^4$$
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{8.64 \times 10^{6}}{60} = 1.44 \times 10^{5}\ \text{mm}^3$$
>
> **Part (b)(i) — Maximum stresses:**
> $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{13{,}500\ \text{N·m}}{1.44 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^3} = \frac{13{,}500}{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}$$
> $$\sigma_{max} = 93.75 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 93.75\ \text{MPa}$$
>
> For a **sagging** simply supported beam:
> - **Top fibre:** Compression $\Rightarrow \sigma_c = 93.75$ MPa.
> - **Bottom fibre:** Tension $\Rightarrow \sigma_t = 93.75$ MPa.
>
> **Part (b)(ii) — Safety Check:**
> $$\sigma_t = 93.75\ \text{MPa} \;\; \text{vs} \;\; \sigma_{t,allow} = 30\ \text{MPa} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 93.75 > 30 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{NOT SAFE in tension.}$$
> $$\sigma_c = 93.75\ \text{MPa} \;\; \text{vs} \;\; \sigma_{c,allow} = 90\ \text{MPa} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 93.75 > 90 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{NOT SAFE in compression.}$$
>
> **Conclusion:** The given section is **NOT safe** for the applied loading. Either the section must be increased in size, or the load / span must be reduced, to bring the stresses within allowable limits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 3 Exam Scripts (PCMUT402):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $I$:** Always express $I$ in $\text{m}^4$ (or $\text{mm}^4$) consistently with $M$ in $\text{N·m}$ (or $\text{N·mm}$) and $\sigma$ in $\text{Pa}$ (or $\text{MPa}$). A common error is mixing $\text{mm}^4$ with $\text{N·m}$, which produces a stress off by a factor of $10^6$. **(2-mark penalty per occurrence)**
>
> 2. **Not stating the assumptions of pure bending before the derivation:** The KTU 2024 scheme mandates a minimum of 4–5 explicit assumptions before the derivation begins. Skipping this costs a flat **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Sign convention confusion:** For a simply supported beam with downward load, the maximum moment is **positive (sagging)**, giving tension at the **bottom** and compression at the **top**. Reversing these costs **1 mark** in design problems.
>
> 4. **Confusing Section Modulus with Moment of Inertia:** $Z = I/y_{max}$ is **not** the same as $I$. A common student error is to use $I$ in the formula $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$ instead of $Z$. **(1–2 mark penalty)**
>
> 5. **Forgetting to convert $E$ to Pa:** If $E$ is given in GPa, multiply by $10^9$ before substitution. The KTU marker will not give partial credit for an answer that is off by a factor of $10^9$.
>
> 6. **Sketching the deformed beam incorrectly:** The deformed segment of the beam in pure bending is an **arc of a circle**, not a parabola. A parabolic sketch indicates a misunderstanding and may cost **1 mark**.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to check the position of the Neutral Axis:** In a symmetric section, the NA is at mid-height. In an unsymmetric section (T-beam, angle section), the NA is at the centroid — which must be located before $y_{max}$ is found. **(1–2 mark penalty)**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Pure Bending (Module 3, PCMUT402):**
>
> - [x] **Pure Bending:** $V = 0$ and $M = \text{constant}$. Occurs between the loads in four-point bending and at the inflection of the shear diagram.
> - [x] **Flexure Formula:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — the single most important equation of the module.
> - [x] **Six Assumptions:** Homogeneous & isotropic, Hooke's law, plane sections remain plane, prismatic beam, large $R$, no self-weight.
> - [x] **Six Limitations:** Non-linear material, short deep beams (shear deformation), non-prismatic, curved beams, composite beams, plastic region, unsymmetrical bending.
> - [x] **Neutral Axis:** Located at the **centroid** of the cross-section for pure bending of a homogeneous beam.
> - [x] **Strain variation:** $\varepsilon = y/R$ → **linear** with $y$.
> - [x] **Stress variation:** $\sigma = Ey/R$ → **linear** with $y$, maximum at outermost fibre.
> - [x] **Section Modulus:** $Z = I/y_{max}$ (units: $\text{m}^3$). Governs **strength**.
> - [x] **Flexural Rigidity:** $EI$ (units: $\text{N·m}^2$). Governs **stiffness / deflection**.
> - [x] **Maximum stress formula:** $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$ — used in **design**.
> - [x] **Radius of curvature:** $R = EI/M$ — large for slender beams, justifying small-deflection theory.
> - [x] **Curvature equation:** $1/R = M/(EI)$ — base of all beam-deflection analysis.
> - [x] **Common cross-section formulas:** $I_{rect} = bh^3/12$, $Z_{rect} = bh^2/6$, $I_{circle} = \pi d^4/64$, $Z_{circle} = \pi d^3/32$.
> - [x] **Sign convention (KTU standard):** Sagging = positive $M$, tension at bottom, compression at top.
> - [x] **Design equation:** $Z_{required} = M/\sigma_{allow}$; select a standard section with $Z_{actual} \ge Z_{required}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shearing Stress Formula for Beams, Shear Stress Distribution in Beam Section, Derivation and Problems for Rectangular Section Only, Assumptions and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shearing Stress Formula for Beams, Shear Stress Distribution in Beam Section, Derivation and Problems for Rectangular Section Only, Assumptions and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Transverse Shearing Stress in Beams** is the vertical (or transverse) stress that acts on the horizontal cross-sectional plane of a beam due to the internal shear force developed at that section. Unlike bending stress, which varies **linearly** across the depth of the section, shear stress is **non-uniformly distributed** across the width and depth of the cross-section.

The governing relationship is given by the **Zhuravskii–Bredt shear formula** (commonly called the **shear stress formula for beams**):

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}}$$

where the parameters are defined in the table below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** The complete theoretical derivation and numerical problem-solving is **restricted to the Rectangular Section only**. For I-section, T-section, and built-up sections, the topic is **NOT** assessed as per the current 2024 scheme regulation.

## 1.2 Variable Definitions and Units

| Symbol | Quantity | S.I. Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:---------:|
| $\tau$ | Transverse shear stress at a fiber level | $\text{N/m}^2$ or **Pa** |
| $V$ | Net transverse shear force at the section | **N** (Newton) |
| $Q$ | First moment of area ($A \bar{y}$) of the portion above (or below) the fiber about the Neutral Axis | $\text{m}^3$ |
| $I$ | Second moment of area (Moment of Inertia) of the **entire** cross-section about the Neutral Axis | $\text{m}^4$ |
| $b$ | Width of the cross-section at the level where shear stress is being calculated | **m** (meter) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Deck of Cards" 🍂

Imagine a thick book resting flat on a table. If you push the top cover horizontally while holding the bottom cover fixed, the pages (which act like horizontal fibers of the beam) **slide** against one another. The page near the bottom doesn't move; the page near the top moves the most; the page right in the middle moves the *most* of all if the friction between pages is high.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** The **middle fiber** of a beam experiences the maximum sliding effect (shear). The **top and bottom fibers** are clamped by the bending stress and experience almost **zero** transverse shear stress. This is why the shear stress distribution is **parabolic** in a rectangular section — zero at top/bottom, maximum at the Neutral Axis (NA).

## 1.4 Physical Reality Check

When a beam bends due to transverse loading:
- The **bending stress** $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ acts **horizontally** (normal to the cross-section).
- The **shear stress** $\tau$ acts **vertically** (parallel to the shear force $V$).
- Both stresses coexist at every point inside the beam.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often assume shear stress is *uniform* across the depth of the beam, equating it to $V/A$. This is **only an average**. The true distribution is **parabolic for rectangular sections** and varies differently for other sections. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Logic

The shear stress in a beam does **not** arise in isolation. It emerges because of a fundamental **equilibrium requirement**:

> When two adjacent cross-sections of a beam carry different bending moments, the bending stresses on those sections are different. To prevent the horizontal fibers from separating or overlapping, an **equalizing horizontal shear stress** must act on the **horizontal plane** between the fibers. By the **complementary property of shear stresses**, an equal vertical shear stress acts on the vertical cross-section.

This is the cornerstone of the derivation.

## 2.2 Assumptions Made in the Derivation (KTU Frequently Tested)

The shear stress formula $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ is derived under the following idealizations:

1. **The beam is initially straight** and possesses a longitudinal plane of symmetry.
2. **Loading is applied in the plane of symmetry**, producing **pure bending** with transverse shear.
3. **Cross-section is uniform** (prismatic beam) along its length.
4. **Hooke's Law is obeyed** — material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
5. **Plane sections remain plane** after bending (Euler–Bernoulli assumption) — *this implies a linear strain distribution*.
6. **Shear deformation is neglected** — cross-sections do *not* warp; they remain perpendicular to the deformed neutral axis.
7. **No resultant horizontal force** exists on the cross-section — only bending and shear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption 6 is the most critical for KTU valuation.** The shear stress formula gives the **average shear stress** across the width $b$. It cannot capture the true **stress concentration** that occurs at the web–flange junction of an I-beam. This is a known limitation of the theory.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Rectangular Section Specifics

The table below is the **high-yield** reference for solving KTU university exam problems.

| # | Parameter | Formula for Rectangular Section ($b \times d$) | Notes |
|:-:|:----------|:---------------------------------------------|:------|
| 1 | Cross-sectional Area | $A = b \cdot d$ | — |
| 2 | Moment of Inertia (about NA) | $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | About centroidal axis |
| 3 | Maximum Bending Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{6M}{b d^2}$ | $Z$ = section modulus |
| 4 | First Moment $Q$ at NA | $Q_{\max} = A_{\text{above}} \cdot \bar{y} = b \cdot \dfrac{d}{2} \cdot \dfrac{d}{4} = \dfrac{b d^2}{8}$ | Half area × half-depth |
| 5 | First Moment $Q(y)$ at distance $y$ from NA | $Q(y) = b \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{y + \dfrac{d}{2}}{2}\right) = \dfrac{b}{2}\left(\dfrac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)$ | Area of strip above $y$ × centroid distance |
| 6 | Shear Stress at NA (Maximum) | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd}$ | 1.5 × average |
| 7 | Shear Stress at distance $y$ from NA | $\tau(y) = \dfrac{V}{2I}\left(\dfrac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)$ | Parabolic distribution |
| 8 | Shear Stress at top/bottom fiber ($y = \pm d/2$) | $\tau = 0$ | Consistent with parabolic curve |
| 9 | Average Shear Stress | $\tau_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{V}{bd}$ | Reference value |
| 10 | Ratio of Max to Average | $\dfrac{\tau_{\max}}{\tau_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{3}{2}$ | **KTU favorite numerical** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip for KTU Exam:** The factor **3/2 = 1.5** is so important for rectangular sections that the examiner will expect you to derive the relation $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd}$ from the general formula in your working steps.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The shear stress distribution is critical in the design of:

- **Wooden beams** (rectangular cross-section) — governs failure along the neutral axis due to horizontal shear.
- **Short, deep beams** — where shear, not bending, controls the design.
- **Steel rolling-mill beams** — the parabolic distribution is used to check **shear failure** near supports.
- **Reinforced concrete beams** — shear stress at the neutral axis determines the **stirrup spacing** in the shear design.

## 2.5 Limitations of the Shear Stress Formula (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| # | Limitation | Engineering Implication |
|:-:|:-----------|:------------------------|
| 1 | Gives **average** shear stress over width $b$ | Misses stress concentrations at fillets, notches, holes |
| 2 | Assumes plane sections remain plane | Fails for **deep beams** ($L/d < 5$) and **short beams** |
| 3 | Assumes isotropic, homogeneous material | Inapplicable to **composite** or **fibrous** beams (e.g., wood, FRP) |
| 4 | Cannot predict **shear warping** | I-section flanges show non-uniform distribution; this formula only gives an average |
| 5 | Material assumed linearly elastic | Fails in the **plastic** design regime where redistribution occurs |
| 6 | Width $b$ must be continuous | Fails at sections where the width changes abruptly (e.g., step beams) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked about limitations, students often write *only* "It is derived for elastic materials." Examiner expects at least **3–4 specific** limitations (see table). Mark loss of 1–2 marks is common here.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Shear Stress Formula $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$

### 3.1.1 Free Body Setup

Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ subjected to a transverse load. Two cross-sections are taken:
- Section 1-1 at distance $x$ (bending moment $M$)
- Section 2-2 at distance $x + dx$ (bending moment $M + dM$)

By equilibrium, the shear forces at the two sections differ by $dV$.

> **For the KTU 2024 examination, you are expected to:**
> 1. Draw a labeled FBD.
> 2. State the equilibrium of the longitudinal (horizontal) forces on the portion above a fiber at distance $y$ from the NA.
> 3. Introduce complementary shear to obtain $\tau$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Bending stress at a fiber distance $y$ from the NA (flexure formula).**

By the Euler–Bernoulli bending theory:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

**Step 2: Consider a small horizontal strip of area $dA$ at distance $y$ from the NA.**

The horizontal force on this strip at section 1-1 is:

$$dF_1 = \sigma \cdot dA = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \cdot dA$$

**Step 3: Total horizontal force on the area above the strip (from NA to the top fiber) at section 1-1.**

$$F_1 = \int_{y}^{d/2} \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \, dA = \frac{M}{I} \int_{y}^{d/2} y \, dA$$

The integral $\displaystyle \int_{y}^{d/2} y \, dA$ is, by definition, the **first moment of area** $Q$ of the portion above the fiber about the NA.

$$F_1 = \frac{M \cdot Q}{I}$$

**Step 4: Similarly, at section 2-2, the horizontal force is:**

$$F_2 = \frac{(M + dM) \cdot Q}{I}$$

**Step 5: Net unbalanced horizontal force on the upper portion (over the length $dx$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
dF &= F_2 - F_1 = \frac{(M + dM) \cdot Q}{I} - \frac{M \cdot Q}{I} \\
dF &= \frac{Q \cdot dM}{I}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: This unbalanced force must be resisted by the horizontal shear stress $\tau_h$ acting on the horizontal plane (of area $b \cdot dx$) at the fiber level.**

$$dF = \tau_h \cdot b \cdot dx$$

**Step 7: Equate and simplify:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_h \cdot b \cdot dx &= \frac{Q \cdot dM}{I} \\
\tau_h &= \frac{Q}{I \cdot b} \cdot \frac{dM}{dx}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8: From the elementary beam theory relation** $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ (the rate of change of bending moment equals the shear force):

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used:** The result is taken as a magnitude. KTU expects the **derivation with the FBD clearly shown**, and the relation $dM/dx = V$ is a permissible direct substitution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation for Rectangular Section — Shear Stress at Distance $y$ from NA

### 3.2.1 Geometry

A rectangular section has:
- Width: $b$ (constant across depth)
- Depth: $d$
- Moment of Inertia about NA: $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$

### 3.2.2 First Moment of Area $Q(y)$ at Fiber Level $y$

The area of the rectangular portion **above** the fiber (from $y$ to $d/2$) is:

$$A(y) = b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)$$

The centroid of this portion lies at a distance $\bar{y}$ from the NA:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{y + d/2}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left(y + \frac{d}{2}\right)$$

Therefore, the first moment of area about the NA:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q(y) &= A(y) \cdot \bar{y} \\
Q(y) &= b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(y + \frac{d}{2}\right) \\
Q(y) &= \frac{b}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} + y\right) \\
Q(y) &= \frac{b}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.3 Substitute into the General Formula

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau(y) &= \frac{V \cdot Q(y)}{I \cdot b} \\
\tau(y) &= \frac{V}{I \cdot b} \cdot \frac{b}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right) \\
\tau(y) &= \frac{V}{2I} \cdot \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\tau(y) = \frac{V}{2I} \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)}$$

### 3.2.4 Maximum Shear Stress (at Neutral Axis, $y = 0$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{V}{2I} \cdot \frac{d^2}{4} \\
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{V \cdot d^2}{8I}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{V \cdot d^2}{8 \cdot \dfrac{b d^3}{12}} \\
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{12 V}{8 b d} \\
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{3V}{2bd}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \cdot \tau_{\text{avg}}}$$

### 3.2.5 Minimum Shear Stress (at Extreme Fibers, $y = \pm d/2$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\min} &= \frac{V}{2I}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - \frac{d^2}{4}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the **parabolic distribution** with zero at the top and bottom fibers.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problems (Rectangular Section)

### 🔢 Problem 1: Shear Stress at a Specific Fiber

> A simply supported beam of rectangular cross-section **120 mm × 200 mm** (width × depth) carries a uniformly distributed load. At a section where the shear force is **40 kN**, find the shear stress at:
> (a) The Neutral Axis
> (b) A fiber 60 mm above the NA

**Given:**
- $b = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.12 \text{ m}$
- $d = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- $V = 40 \text{ kN} = 40 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Compute $I$**

$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{0.12 \times (0.20)^3}{12}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{0.12 \times 0.008}{12} \\
I &= \frac{0.00096}{12} \\
I &= 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (a): Shear stress at NA ($y = 0$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{3V}{2bd} \\
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{3 \times 40 \times 10^3}{2 \times 0.12 \times 0.20} \\
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{120 \times 10^3}{0.048} \\
\tau_{\max} &= 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
\tau_{\max} &= 2.5 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (b): Shear stress at $y = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$**

Using the distribution formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau(y) &= \frac{V}{2I}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right) \\
\tau(0.06) &= \frac{40 \times 10^3}{2 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-5}}\left(\frac{0.20^2}{4} - 0.06^2\right) \\
\tau(0.06) &= \frac{40 \times 10^3}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}\left(0.01 - 0.0036\right) \\
\tau(0.06) &= 2.5 \times 10^8 \times 0.0064 \\
\tau(0.06) &= 1.6 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
\tau(0.06) &= 1.6 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative shortcut using ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\tau(y)}{\tau_{\max}} &= \frac{\dfrac{d^2}{4} - y^2}{\dfrac{d^2}{4}} = 1 - \left(\frac{2y}{d}\right)^2 \\
\frac{\tau(0.06)}{2.5} &= 1 - \left(\frac{2 \times 0.06}{0.20}\right)^2 = 1 - (0.6)^2 = 1 - 0.36 = 0.64 \\
\tau(0.06) &= 0.64 \times 2.5 = 1.6 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> The **dimensionless ratio form** $\dfrac{\tau(y)}{\tau_{\max}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{2y}{d}\right)^2$ is an excellent time-saver in KTU exams. Memorize it.

### 🔢 Problem 2: Shear Force from Shear Stress

> A rectangular timber beam 100 mm wide and 150 mm deep is loaded such that the maximum shear stress developed is **1.2 MPa**. Find the shear force the section can carry.

**Given:**
- $b = 100 \text{ mm}$, $d = 150 \text{ mm}$, $\tau_{\max} = 1.2 \text{ MPa} = 1.2 \text{ N/mm}^2$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{3V}{2bd} \\
1.2 &= \frac{3V}{2 \times 100 \times 150} \\
1.2 &= \frac{3V}{30000} \\
V &= \frac{1.2 \times 30000}{3} \\
V &= 12000 \text{ N} = 12 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Variation Summary for Rectangular Section

| Distance $y$ from NA | $\dfrac{d^2}{4} - y^2$ | $\tau(y) / \tau_{\max}$ | $\tau(y)$ (in terms of $\tau_{\max}$) |
|:--------------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------------:|:-------------------------------------:|
| $0$ (NA) | $\dfrac{d^2}{4}$ | $1$ | $\tau_{\max}$ |
| $\dfrac{d}{4}$ | $\dfrac{d^2}{4} - \dfrac{d^2}{16} = \dfrac{3d^2}{16}$ | $\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $0.75 \, \tau_{\max}$ |
| $\dfrac{d}{2}$ (extreme) | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $\dfrac{d}{6}$ | $\dfrac{d^2}{4} - \dfrac{d^2}{36} = \dfrac{2d^2}{9}$ | $\dfrac{8}{9}$ | $0.889 \, \tau_{\max}$ |

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **complete logical flow** of the derivation, replacing the physical FBD with a functional block architecture for clarity.

    A --> C[Section 2-2 at x+dx with Moment M+dM]
    B --> D[Bending stress sigma1 = M*y/I on upper fiber area]
    C --> E[Bending stress sigma2 = M+dM * y/I on upper fiber area]
    D --> F[Horizontal Force F1 = M*Q/I]
    E --> G[Horizontal Force F2 = M+dM * Q/I]
    F --> H[Net unbalanced force dF = Q*dM/I]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Equilibrium with horizontal shear stress tau_h on area b*dx]
    I --> J[tau_h * b * dx = Q * dM / I]
    J --> K[Apply dM/dx = V]
    K --> L[Final Result: tau = V*Q / I*b]
    L --> M[For Rectangular Section substitute Q expression]
    M --> N[tau_y = V/2I * d^2/4 - y^2]
    N --> O[tau_max at NA = 3V/2bd = 1.5 * V/A]

## 4.4 Schematic Description of the Parabolic Variation

Since Mermaid cannot directly plot a 2D curve, the **schematic of the parabolic distribution** is described textually for reproduction in your exam answer:

```
Shear Stress τ (MPa)

       │           ●     ●

       │      ●                ●
   1.0 ┤    ●                     ●
       │  ●                         ●
       │●                             ●

       └────┬─────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬────►
           -d/2  -d/4    0    +d/4   +d/2    Distance y from NA
```

The distribution is a **parabola symmetric about the Neutral Axis**, with the vertex at the NA and zeros at the top and bottom surfaces.
