# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the formula for transverse shear stress in beams. Explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The transverse shear stress at a fiber in a beam cross-section is given by:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

- $V$ = Shear force at the section (in **N**).
- $Q$ = First moment of area of the portion of the cross-section above (or below) the fiber about the Neutral Axis (in **m³**).
- $I$ = Moment of inertia of the **entire** cross-section about the Neutral Axis (in **m⁴**).
- $b$ = Width of the cross-section at the level of the fiber (in **m**).

**(3 marks — 1 for formula, 2 for term explanation)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**For a rectangular cross-section beam, derive the ratio of maximum shear stress to average shear stress.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Average shear stress:

$$\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{V}{bd}$$

Maximum shear stress (at NA):

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{3V}{2bd}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{\tau_{\max}}{\tau_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{3V/2bd}{V/bd} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

**(3 marks — 1 for each formula, 1 for the final ratio)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions for Module 3 follow a strict internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A simply supported wooden beam of span 4 m has a rectangular cross-section 150 mm wide and 250 mm deep. It carries a concentrated load of 60 kN at the midspan.**

**(a)** Draw the SFD and BMD for the beam, clearly marking the maximum shear force and maximum bending moment values. (7 marks)

**(b)** At a section located 1 m from the left support, find the shear stress at the Neutral Axis and at a fiber 80 mm above the NA. Also, determine the maximum shear stress in the beam. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Reactions.** By symmetry of loading and geometry:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Shear Force Diagram (SFD).**

- For $0 \leq x < 2$ m (left half): $V = +30$ kN
- At $x = 2$ m: Point load drops the SFD by 60 kN.
- For $2 < x \leq 4$ m (right half): $V = -30$ kN

$$\boxed{V_{\max} = 30 \text{ kN (constant between support and midspan)}}$$

**Step 3: Bending Moment Diagram (BMD).**

- At $x = 0$ and $x = 4$ m: $M = 0$
- At $x = 2$ m (midspan): $M = R_A \times 2 = 30 \times 2 = 60$ kN·m

$$\boxed{M_{\max} = 60 \text{ kN·m at midspan}}$$

**SFD/BMD Sketch (textual):**

```
SFD:
+30 kN ┤━━━━━━━━━━┓
       │          ┃
   0   ┤          ┣━━━━━━━━━━┓
       │          ┃          ┃
-30 kN ┤          ┛          ┗━━━━━━━━━━ (continued to -30 at right)
       0         2m          4m

BMD (Triangular, peak at midspan):
   0   ┤╲                          ╱┃
       │  ╲                      ╱  ┃
       │    ╲                  ╱    ┃
  60kNm┤      ╲              ╱      ┃
       │        ╲__________╱        ┃
       0         2m        4m
```

**(7 marks — 2 for reactions, 2 for SFD with max value, 3 for BMD with max value)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given for shear stress:**
- $b = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m}$
- $d = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.250 \text{ m}$
- $V = 30 \text{ kN} = 30 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$ (since the section is in the left half)

**Step 1: Compute $I$.**

$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{0.150 \times (0.250)^3}{12} = \frac{0.150 \times 0.015625}{12}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{0.00234375}{12} \\
I &= 1.9531 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Shear stress at NA ($y = 0$).**

Using $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{3 \times 30 \times 10^3}{2 \times 0.150 \times 0.250} \\
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{90 \times 10^3}{0.075} \\
\tau_{\max} &= 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
\tau_{\max} &= 1.2 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Shear stress at $y = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$ above NA.**

Using the parabolic distribution:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau(y) &= \frac{V}{2I} \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right) \\
\tau(0.080) &= \frac{30 \times 10^3}{2 \times 1.9531 \times 10^{-4}} \left(\frac{0.250^2}{4} - 0.080^2\right) \\
\tau(0.080) &= 7.68 \times 10^7 \left(0.015625 - 0.0064\right) \\
\tau(0.080) &= 7.68 \times 10^7 \times 0.009225 \\
\tau(0.080) &= 0.7085 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
\tau(0.080) &= 0.7085 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Maximum shear stress in the beam.**

Since the shear force is constant at 30 kN throughout the left half (and right half), and the cross-section is uniform:

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max, \text{beam}} = 1.2 \text{ MPa, occurring throughout the beam (at all NA points)}}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for $I$, 2 for $\tau$ at NA, 2 for $\tau$ at $y=80$ mm, 1 for final statement)**

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A rectangular beam of cross-section 200 mm × 300 mm (width × depth) is subjected to a maximum shear force of 50 kN.**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the shear stress at a distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis, starting from the general formula $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the maximum shear stress, the shear stress at 75 mm from the NA, and the percentage of the total shear force carried by the material in the upper half of the beam. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Starting with the general formula:**

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

**Step 1:** For a rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $d$, the area above the fiber at distance $y$ from the NA is:

$$A_{\text{above}} = b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)$$

**Step 2:** The centroidal distance of this area from the NA is:

$$\bar{y}_{\text{above}} = \frac{y + d/2}{2}$$

**Step 3:** First moment of area:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= A_{\text{above}} \cdot \bar{y}_{\text{above}} \\
Q &= b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(y + \frac{d}{2}\right) \\
Q &= \frac{b}{2} \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the general formula (with $I = bd^3/12$ for later use):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{V \cdot \frac{b}{2}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)}{I \cdot b} \\
\tau &= \frac{V}{2I} \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right) \\
\boxed{\tau(y) &= \frac{V}{2I}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for $A_{\text{above}}$, 2 for $\bar{y}$, 2 for algebra, 1 for final boxed result)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $b = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.200 \text{ m}$
- $d = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}$
- $V_{\max} = 50 \text{ kN} = 50 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Compute $I$.**

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{0.200 \times (0.300)^3}{12} = \frac{0.200 \times 0.027}{12} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2: Maximum shear stress (at NA, $y = 0$).**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = \frac{3 \times 50 \times 10^3}{2 \times 0.200 \times 0.300} = \frac{150 \times 10^3}{0.120}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 1.25 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3: Shear stress at $y = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau(0.075) &= \frac{50 \times 10^3}{2 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4}} \left(\frac{0.300^2}{4} - 0.075^2\right) \\
&= 5.556 \times 10^7 \times (0.0225 - 0.005625) \\
&= 5.556 \times 10^7 \times 0.016875 \\
&= 0.9375 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
\boxed{\tau(0.075) &= 0.9375 \text{ MPa}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Percentage of shear force carried by the upper half.**

The shear force on the upper half is the integral of $\tau \cdot b$ from $y = 0$ to $y = d/2$:

$$F_{\text{upper}} = \int_0^{d/2} \tau(y) \cdot b \, dy$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{upper}} &= \int_0^{d/2} \frac{V \cdot b}{2I} \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right) dy \\
&= \frac{V \cdot b}{2I} \int_0^{d/2} \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right) dy \\
&= \frac{V \cdot b}{2I} \left[\frac{d^2}{4} y - \frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^{d/2} \\
&= \frac{V \cdot b}{2I} \left[\frac{d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{d}{2} - \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{d^3}{8}\right] \\
&= \frac{V \cdot b}{2I} \left[\frac{d^3}{8} - \frac{d^3}{24}\right] \\
&= \frac{V \cdot b}{2I} \cdot \frac{d^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{V \cdot b}{2I} \cdot I \\
&= \frac{V}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{upper}} = \frac{V}{2} = 50\% \text{ of total shear force}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The upper half of the beam carries exactly **50%** of the total shear force, and the lower half carries the remaining **50%**. This is a unique property of the rectangular section due to its symmetric parabolic distribution. KTU examiners often pose this as a "show that" problem.

**(7 marks — 1 for $I$, 2 for $\tau_{\max}$, 2 for $\tau$ at 75 mm, 2 for the 50% result)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $I = bd^3/12$ explicitly** — Many students directly substitute into $\tau = 3V/2bd$ without showing $I$ calculation. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign errors in $Q$ calculation** — The centroid of the area above the fiber is at $(y + d/2)/2$, NOT at $d/2$. Common mistake is using $d/2$ as the centroid distance.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing mm and m within the same expression. KTU prefers a **single unit system** throughout the problem.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of complementary shear** — When deriving the formula, examiners expect the statement: "The horizontal shear stress on the horizontal plane equals the vertical shear stress on the vertical plane (complementary property)."
> 5. **Skipping the relation $dM/dx = V$** — This step is a **mandatory intermediate** in the derivation. Skipping it leads to a 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **For Problem B(b) Step 4:** Students often skip the integration and claim 50% "by symmetry." This is **not** rigorous — the integration must be shown.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **General Formula:** $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ — also called the **Zhuravskii–Bredt formula**.
- ✅ **Rectangular section specifics:** $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$, $Q_{\max} = \dfrac{bd^2}{8}$, $b = $ width at fiber.
- ✅ **Maximum shear stress** for rectangular section: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \cdot \tau_{\text{avg}}$ — occurs at the **Neutral Axis**.
- ✅ **Minimum shear stress** at the top/bottom fibers = **0**.
- ✅ **Shear stress distribution** is **parabolic** for a rectangular section.
- ✅ **Dimensionless ratio shortcut:** $\dfrac{\tau(y)}{\tau_{\max}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{2y}{d}\right)^2$.
- ✅ **Key derivation step:** $dM/dx = V$ is a non-negotiable intermediate in the derivation.
- ✅ **Upper half carries exactly 50% of $V$** for a rectangular section.
- ✅ **Major assumptions:** Euler–Bernoulli (plane sections remain plane), isotropic material, prismatic beam, linear elasticity.
- ✅ **Major limitations:** Gives **average** $\tau$ over width $b$; fails for **deep beams** ($L/d < 5$); cannot capture **stress concentrations**.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 exam scope:** **Rectangular section only** for derivation and problems. I/T/circular sections are out of scope for this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deflection of Beams: Moment-Curvature Relation, Assumptions and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deflection of Beams: Moment-Curvature Relation, Assumptions and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moment-Curvature Relation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> The **Moment-Curvature Relation** is the fundamental equation of the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory** that establishes a direct proportional relationship between the **bending moment** $M$ acting at a section of a beam and the resulting **curvature** $\left(\frac{1}{R}\right)$ of the deflected neutral axis at that section, with the constant of proportionality being the **flexural rigidity** $EI$.

Mathematically, the canonical form is:

$$M = \frac{EI}{R}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section (N·m)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity of the beam material (N/m² or Pa)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Area Moment of Inertia) about the Neutral Axis (m⁴)
- $R$ = Radius of curvature of the bent neutral axis (m)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Diving Board & The Ruler"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition for First-Time Learners**

Imagine you are standing at the free end of a **swimming pool diving board** (a cantilever beam). The further you walk towards the tip:
- The **bending moment** at the fixed end increases progressively.
- The board bends into a curve — the **curvature** $\left(\frac{1}{R}\right)$ becomes more pronounced (i.e., $R$ becomes smaller).
- If the board were made of **steel** (high $E$), it would resist bending strongly — small curvature.
- If the board were made of a thicker section (higher $I$), it would also resist bending — small curvature.

**The Moment-Curvature Relation simply states**: *The amount of "bending" (curvature) produced is directly proportional to the "bending effort" (moment), and inversely proportional to the beam's "stiffness" ($EI$).*

A simpler everyday analogy: Think of a thin plastic **ruler** held firmly on a table with one end free. Pressing down on the free end (creating a moment) causes the ruler to curve. Press harder → curve more. Use a wooden plank instead → curve much less (higher $EI$).

## 1.3 Why is this Relation Important?

> [!TIP]
> The Moment-Curvature Relation is the **cornerstone of beam deflection analysis**. Every major method taught in this module — the **Double Integration Method**, **Macaulay's Method**, **Moment-Area Method**, and **Conjugate Beam Method** — is ultimately derived from this single governing equation:

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

This is the starting differential equation from which the entire **deflection curve** $y(x)$ of a beam is computed.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Mastery

| Term | Physical Meaning | Symbol/Unit |
|------|-----------------|-------------|
| Curvature | Rate of change of slope of the deflected beam | $1/R$ (m⁻¹) |
| Radius of Curvature | Radius of the osculating circle fitting the beam axis | $R$ (m) |
| Neutral Axis | The line in the cross-section where fibre length is unchanged | N.A. |
| Flexural Rigidity | Beam's resistance to bending | $EI$ (N·m²) |
| Deflection | Vertical displacement of any point on the beam axis | $y$ or $v$ (m) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — "Why does bending cause curvature?"

When a beam is subjected to a **pure bending moment** (no shear force, no axial force):
1. **Convex side fibres** get **stretched** → tensile strain $\varepsilon_t = +\frac{y}{R}$
2. **Concave side fibres** get **compressed** → compressive strain $\varepsilon_c = -\frac{y}{R}$
3. The **Neutral Axis** (at the centroid) experiences **zero strain**.

Since the material obeys **Hooke's Law** (linear elastic regime):
$$\sigma = E\varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = \frac{Ey}{R}$$

The internal moment $M$ is the moment of these stress distribution about the Neutral Axis:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \cdot dA = \int_A \frac{E y^2}{R} dA = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y^2 dA$$

Since $\int_A y^2 dA = I$ (Second Moment of Area), we obtain:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{R} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every Part B question of Module 3.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Form |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | **Moment-Curvature Relation** (Basic) | Core equation |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ | Curvature for **small deflections** (slope $\frac{dy}{dx} \ll 1$) | Approximate form |
| 3 | $EI\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | **Governing Differential Equation** of beam deflection | Workhorse equation |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$ | **Exact** curvature formula (large deflections) | General form |
| 5 | $EI\theta = EI\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Slope of the beam | $\theta$ = slope |
| 6 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress | $Z$ = Section Modulus |
| 7 | $y_{max}$ (limits) | Deflection limits per IS 800 / IS 456 | $L/250$, $L/360$, $L/325$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **PITFALL**: In formula (4) — the **exact** curvature equation — the denominator contains $\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}$. For KTU examinations, unless explicitly stated otherwise, students **MUST** use the **approximate** form (formula 2) assuming small slopes. Using the exact form will make the differential equation non-linear and unsolvable by elementary methods.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why practising engineers care about the Moment-Curvature Relation:**

- **Bridge Design** (IS 800, IRC Codes): Limiting the curvature (and hence deflection) to $L/325$ or $L/250$ to avoid user discomfort and cracking.
- **Aerospace Wing Spars**: Predicting the deflected shape of wings under aerodynamic loading.
- **Machine Tool Frames**: Ensuring micro-precision by limiting deflection to microns.
- **Civil Structural Frames**: Computing drift in multi-storey buildings under lateral loads (wind/seismic).
- **Mechanical Spring Design**: Predicting elastic curves of leaf and helical springs.

## 2.4 The Transition: From $M/R$ to the Deflection Equation

> [!TIP]
> The single most critical intellectual step in Module 3 is recognizing:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI} \quad \text{and for small deflections} \quad \frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

Therefore:

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

This **second-order linear ordinary differential equation** is what we integrate (twice) to get the deflection curve $y(x)$. The constants of integration are found from **boundary conditions** (e.g., $y=0$ at fixed support, $\frac{dy}{dx}=0$ at fixed end of cantilever, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Moment-Curvature Relation (From First Principles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Goal**: Starting from the geometry of bending and Hooke's Law, derive $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$.

### Step 1: Consider an initially straight beam segment

Consider an elemental length $dx$ of a beam, initially straight. After bending, this segment subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature $O$, with a radius of curvature $R$.

### Step 2: Geometry of Deformation (Strain Calculation)

For a fibre at a distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis (N.A.):
- Original length of the fibre = $dx$ (same as the neutral fibre)
- New length of the fibre = $(R - y) \cdot d\theta$
- Change in length = $(R - y) d\theta - dx = (R - y) d\theta - R d\theta = -y \, d\theta$

The **longitudinal strain** in this fibre is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Change in length}}{\text{Original length}} = \frac{-y \, d\theta}{R \, d\theta} = -\frac{y}{R}$$

The negative sign indicates **compression** above the N.A. and **tension** below. The magnitude of strain is:

$$|\varepsilon| = \frac{y}{R}$$

### Step 3: Apply Hooke's Law

Assuming the material remains within the **elastic limit**, the bending stress at distance $y$ is:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{Ey}{R}$$

### Step 4: Apply Moment Equilibrium

The internal resisting moment $M$ is the integral of the stress-moment about the N.A.:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA \\
  &= \int_A \left(\frac{Ey}{R}\right) y \, dA \\
  &= \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Recognise the Second Moment of Area

By definition:

$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

### Step 6: Final Result — The Moment-Curvature Relation

Substituting $I$ back:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}}$$

**Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Deflection Differential Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Goal**: Convert $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ into a usable differential equation $EI\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$.

### Step 1: Recall the Exact Curvature Formula from Differential Calculus

For any plane curve $y = y(x)$, the radius of curvature $R$ is given by:

$$R = \frac{\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}{\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Small-Deflection Assumption

In the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory, we assume the slope $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is very small (typically $\frac{dy}{dx} < \frac{1}{100}$). For such small angles, by Taylor's series:

$$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \approx 0 \quad \text{(negligible compared to 1)}$$

Substituting this approximation:

$$\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{[1 + 0]^{3/2}} = \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention adopted in KTU textbooks**: The downward deflection is often taken as positive (in civil/structural practice) or upward as positive (in classical mechanics). This sign convention affects the sign of $M(x)$. The most common form in KTU modules is:
>
> $$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$
>
> where $y$ is the **deflection** (positive downward) and $M$ is the bending moment using standard sagging-positive convention.

### Step 3: Equating the Two Expressions for Curvature

$$\frac{M}{EI} = \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

This gives the **Governing Differential Equation of the Elastic Curve**:

$$\boxed{EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

### Step 4: The Integration Sequence

This second-order ODE is integrated twice:

$$\begin{aligned}
EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} &= M(x) \\
EI\frac{dy}{dx} &= \int M(x) \, dx + C_1 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Slope equation} \\
EI \cdot y &= \iint M(x) \, dx \, dx + C_1 x + C_2 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Deflection equation}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are constants of integration, determined from **boundary conditions** (e.g., $y=0$ at supports, $\frac{dy}{dx}=0$ at fixed ends).

---

## 3.3 The Critical Assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board examiners frequently test these assumptions directly. Memorize them.**

The derivation of the moment-curvature relation and the resulting deflection equation rests on the following **fundamental assumptions**:

### Assumption 1: Hooke's Law is Valid (Linear Elasticity)
The material must be **linearly elastic**, isotropic, and homogeneous. Stress is directly proportional to strain ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$). The beam should not be stressed beyond the **proportional limit**.

### Assumption 2: Plane Sections Remain Plane and Perpendicular to the Neutral Axis (Euler-Bernoulli Hypothesis)
Cross-sections that are plane and perpendicular to the axis **before bending** remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis **after bending**. This implies that **shear deformation is neglected**.

### Assumption 3: Small Slopes and Small Deflections
The slope $\frac{dy}{dx} \ll 1$, allowing the approximation $\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \approx 0$. The deflection $y$ is small compared to the span $L$ (typically $y < L/250$).

### Assumption 4: The Beam is Initially Straight
The unloaded beam axis is perfectly straight. Any initial curvature is neglected.

### Assumption 5: The Beam has a Uniform Cross-Section
$E$ and $I$ are **constants** along the length of the beam, i.e., $EI$ does not vary with $x$.

### Assumption 6: The Neutral Axis Coincides with the Centroidal Axis
The neutral axis passes through the centroid of the cross-section. This requires:
- Material is homogeneous and isotropic.
- Beam is not subjected to axial forces (pure bending only).
- The cross-section is **symmetric** about the plane of bending (or unsymmetric loading is avoided).

### Assumption 7: Self-Weight of the Beam is Neglected
Unless specified, the dead weight of the beam is ignored (or treated as a separate UDL).

### Assumption 8: Radius of Curvature is Large Compared to Beam Dimensions
$R \gg h$ (depth of beam). This is consistent with the small-deflection assumption.

### Assumption 9: No Torsional Deformation
The beam is loaded in its **plane of symmetry** (principal plane), so twisting/torsion effects are excluded.

### Assumption 10: Shear and Axial Deformations are Negligible
The deflection is due **purely to bending moment**, not due to shear or axial forces.

---

## 3.4 Limitations of the Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory

> [!WARNING]
> **The Moment-Curvature Relation has important limitations — examiners test these!**

### Limitation 1: Failure for Deep Beams
For **deep beams** (where depth $h > L/10$ of span, e.g., transfer girders, short-span footings), shear deformation becomes significant. The Euler-Bernoulli theory **under-predicts** the deflection. **Timoshenko Beam Theory** must be used instead.

### Limitation 2: Inaccurate for Short Beams
For beams where $L/h < 10$, the plane-sections-remain-plane assumption breaks down due to significant transverse shear strain.

### Limitation 3: Cannot Capture Local Buckling / Local Effects
The theory is for **global beam behaviour**, not local phenomena like web buckling, flange local buckling, or stress concentrations at holes.

### Limitation 4: Only Linear Material Behaviour
Cannot analyse **plastic hinge** formation, yielding, or post-elastic behaviour. It is strictly an **elastic** analysis.

### Limitation 5: Neglect of Shear Effects
In beams made of materials with low shear modulus (e.g., wood, composites), shear deflection is comparable to bending deflection and must be added separately.

### Limitation 6: Fails for Very Flexible Beams / Large Rotations
When the deflection is large (e.g., fishing rod, fishing pole, flexible manipulator), the small-angle assumption is violated. **Geometric non-linearity** dominates.

### Limitation 7: Neglect of Warping of Cross-Section
In thin-walled open sections (e.g., I-beams, channel sections), the cross-section may **warp** (out-of-plane distortion). The theory ignores this; **Vlasov's Theory of Thin-Walled Beams** handles it.

### Limitation 8: No Stress Concentrations
The theory gives linear stress distribution, but actual stress at **notches, holes, and sudden changes in cross-section** is much higher (stress concentration factors needed).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Setup — Bending of a Beam Element

> [!TIP]
> **The following block diagram illustrates the physical geometry of a bent beam element — the foundation of the moment-curvature derivation.**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["INITIAL STATE — Beam Segment Before Bending"]
        A1["Straight beam of length dx"]
        A2["Two cross-sections P and Q, originally parallel"]
        A3["Neutral axis along centroidal line"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph B["DEFORMED STATE — Same Segment After Bending"]
        B1["Subtends angle dθ at centre O"]
        B2["Radius of curvature R from O to N.A."]
        B3["Fibre at distance y ABOVE N.A. has length R minus y times dθ"]
        B4["Fibre at distance y BELOW N.A. has length R plus y times dθ"]
        B5["N.A. itself has unchanged length R times dθ"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B5
        B2 --> B3
        B2 --> B4
    end

    subgraph C["RESULTING STRAIN DISTRIBUTION"]
        C1["Top fibre: COMPRESSED, strain equals minus y by R"]
        C2["Bottom fibre: STRETCHED, strain equals plus y by R"]
        C3["At N.A.: ZERO strain, fibre length unchanged"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3
    end

    A ==> B
    B ==> C
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Moment-Curvature Derivation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["INPUT: Bending Moment M at section"] --> GEOM["Geometry of Bending: Strain equals y over R"]
    GEOM --> HOOKE["Apply Hooke Law: Stress equals E times y over R"]
    HOOKE --> EQUIL["Moment Equilibrium: M equals integral of stress times y dA"]
    EQUIL --> INERTIA["Recognise I equals integral of y squared dA"]
    INERTIA --> REL["M equals EI over R — MOMENT CURVATURE RELATION"]
    REL --> SMALL["Small slope assumption: 1 over R equals d squared y over d x squared"]
    SMALL --> ODE["Governing ODE: EI d squared y over d x squared equals M of x"]
    ODE --> INT1["First integration: EI slope equals integral of M dx plus C1"]
    INT1 --> INT2["Second integration: EI y equals double integral plus C1 x plus C2"]
    INT2 --> BC["Apply boundary conditions to find C1 and C2"]
    BC --> OUT["OUTPUT: Deflection curve y equals y of x"]
```

## 4.3 Sign Convention & Directional Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SIGN["STANDARD KTU SIGN CONVENTION"]
        S1["X axis: Positive along beam axis, left to right"]
        S2["Y axis: Positive DOWNWARD for deflection y"]
        S3["M positive: Sagging moment — bottom in tension, top in compression"]
        S4["Curvature positive: Concave UP — gives positive d squared y over d x squared when y is positive downward"]
        S1 --> S2
        S2 --> S3
        S3 --> S4
    end

    subgraph REGIONS["STRESS REGIONS OF BENT BEAM"]
        R1["Above N.A.: COMPRESSION zone, y is negative, sigma is negative"]
        R2["At N.A.: NEUTRAL zone, y is zero, sigma is zero"]
        R3["Below N.A.: TENSION zone, y is positive, sigma is positive"]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3
    end

    SIGN ~~~ REGIONS
```

## 4.4 Classification of Beam Theory — When to Use What

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["Beam to be analysed"] --> Q1{"Span to depth ratio L by h?"}
    Q1 -- "Greater than 10" --> EB["Use EULER-BERNOULLI Theory — Equation M equals EI over R"]
    Q1 -- "Between 5 and 10" --> TIM["Use TIMOSHENKO Theory — Add shear deformation term"]
    Q1 -- "Less than 5" --> DEEP["DEEP BEAM — Use 2D Elasticity or FEM"]
    Q1 -- "Very large deflection" --> NL["NON-LINEAR Geometry — Use exact curvature formula"]
    EB --> VALID1["Valid: Most KTU problems"]
    TIM --> VALID2["Valid: Short composite beams, deep transfer girders"]
    DEEP --> VALID3["Valid: Pile caps, corbels, deep foundation beams"]
    NL --> VALID4["Valid: Flexible robotic arms, fishing rods"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Semester B.Tech S4, Model Question Paper]`

**State the moment-curvature relation for the bending of beams and explain the significance of the term $EI$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The moment-curvature relation is given by:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI} \quad \text{or} \quad M = \frac{EI}{R}$$

**Significance of $EI$ (Flexural Rigidity):**
- $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** of the material, indicating its intrinsic stiffness.
- $I$ is the **Second Moment of Area** of the cross-section, indicating the geometric resistance to bending.
- The product $EI$ is called the **flexural rigidity** of the beam — a measure of the beam's resistance to bending deformation.
- A higher $EI$ results in a **smaller curvature** $1/R$ for a given moment $M$, meaning stiffer beams bend less.
- $EI$ is assumed **constant** along the length of a prismatic beam (Euler-Bernoulli assumption).

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the meaning of E and I individually, 1 mark for the meaning of EI and its significance.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Semester B.Tech S4, Supplementary Exam]`

**List any six assumptions made in the derivation of the moment-curvature relation $M = EI/R$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six key assumptions are:

1. **Hooke's law is valid** — the material is linearly elastic and isotropic, and stress is proportional to strain.
2. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis); shear deformation is neglected.
3. **Small deflections and small slopes** — slope $\frac{dy}{dx} \ll 1$, allowing the approximate curvature formula $\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$.
4. The beam is **initially straight** and unloaded, with a **uniform cross-section** along its length (so $EI$ is constant).
5. The **neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis** of the cross-section (requires symmetric loading in the plane of symmetry and no axial force).
6. The **radius of curvature $R$ is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions, and **self-weight** of the beam is neglected unless specified.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 0.5 marks per correct assumption. Maximum 3 marks for any 6 listed.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students **write the moment-curvature equation with the wrong sign** when applying it. Always check: if sagging moment is positive, downward deflection is positive, the equation becomes $EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = +M(x)$.
> - In derivation questions, students often **skip the geometric reasoning step** (deriving $\varepsilon = y/R$). Examiners allocate at least **2 marks** specifically for this step.
> - When asked for "assumptions and limitations", students often confuse the two. **Assumptions** are *postulates used in derivation*; **Limitations** are *scenarios where the theory fails*. Treat them separately.
> - Do not forget to **state the units** of $EI$ (N·m²) and $R$ (m) in your final answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3, Full Question]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the moment-curvature relation $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$ starting from the assumption that plane sections remain plane. Clearly state all the assumptions used in your derivation.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over its entire span. Using the moment-curvature relation, set up the differential equation of the deflection curve and state the boundary conditions. (Do not solve the equation — only set it up.)**

---

#### Solution to Question A, Part (a)

**Step 1: Assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli Theory (2 marks)**

1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
2. Plane sections originally normal to the axis remain plane and normal after bending.
3. Shear deformation is neglected.
4. The beam is initially straight, prismatic, and of uniform $EI$.
5. The neutral axis passes through the centroid; cross-section is symmetric about the plane of bending.
6. Deflections and slopes are small.

**Step 2: Geometry of bending — Strain derivation (2 marks)**

Consider an elemental length $dx$ of the beam, subtending an angle $d\theta$ at centre of curvature $O$.

- Length of neutral axis fibre: $L_{NA} = R \cdot d\theta$
- Length of a fibre at distance $y$ from N.A. (above, towards $O$): $L_y = (R - y) \cdot d\theta$
- Change in length of the fibre: $\Delta L = (R - y) d\theta - R d\theta = -y \, d\theta$
- Strain in the fibre: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_{NA}} = \dfrac{-y \, d\theta}{R \, d\theta} = -\dfrac{y}{R}$

**Step 3: Hooke's Law (1 mark)**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = -\frac{Ey}{R}$$

The magnitude of stress is $\vert \sigma \vert = \dfrac{E \vert y \vert}{R}$.

**Step 4: Moment equilibrium (2 marks)**

The internal resisting moment $M$ is the moment of the bending stress distribution about the N.A.:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA \\
  &= \int_A \frac{Ey}{R} \cdot y \, dA \\
  &= \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Recognition of $I$ and final result (1 mark)**

Since $I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$, we obtain:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{R} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}}$$

---

#### Solution to Question A, Part (b)

**Step 1: Bending moment expression (3 marks)**

For a simply supported beam of span $L$ with UDL $w$ per unit length:
- Reactions at each support: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$
- Bending moment at a section distance $x$ from the left support:

$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$$

**Step 2: Differential equation of deflection curve (3 marks)**

Using the moment-curvature relation with the small-slope assumption $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$:

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x) = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$$

Or equivalently:

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{wLx}{2} - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

**Step 3: Boundary conditions (1 mark)**

For a simply supported beam:
- At $x = 0$: $y = 0$ (deflection zero at left support)
- At $x = L$: $y = 0$ (deflection zero at right support)

These two conditions will be used after integration to determine the constants of integration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3, Full Question]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**State and explain any seven limitations of the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory (moment-curvature relation) with specific reference to practical engineering scenarios where it fails.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the governing differential equation $EI\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ of the elastic curve of a beam from the moment-curvature relation. State clearly the approximation used in the derivation and justify when it is valid.**

---

#### Solution to Question B, Part (a)

**Seven Limitations of Euler-Bernoulli Theory (1 mark each):**

1. **Fails for deep beams** (where $L/h < 10$): In deep transfer girders or short cantilever corbels, shear deformation becomes comparable to bending deformation, and the theory under-predicts the deflection. *Real scenario: Pile caps in foundation design.*

2. **Neglects shear deformation**: The plane-sections-remain-perpendicular assumption ignores shear strain. For materials with low shear modulus $G$ (e.g., wood, FRP composites), the shear deflection is significant. *Real scenario: Plywood beams, GFRP pedestrian bridges.*

3. **Inapplicable to plastic analysis**: The theory is purely elastic and cannot model **plastic hinge formation** or moment redistribution in indeterminate structures. *Real scenario: Limit-state design of continuous RC beams requires plastic analysis beyond Euler-Bernoulli.*

4. **No stress concentrations**: The theory assumes a smooth, linear stress distribution, but real beams have **holes, notches, and sudden changes in cross-section** that cause local stress amplification. *Real scenario: Coping of beam flanges, web openings for services in buildings.*

5. **Ignores cross-sectional warping**: For thin-walled open sections (I-beams, channel sections), the cross-section may **warp** out-of-plane. Euler-Bernoulli ignores this. *Real scenario: Lateral-torsional buckling of slender I-beams.*

6. **Fails for very flexible beams with large deflections**: For slender elements undergoing large rotations (e.g., fishing rods, robotic manipulators), the small-slope assumption $\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \approx 0$ is violated. *Real scenario: Antenna masts under wind loading, fishing rod tip deflection.*

7. **Cannot capture local buckling**: For thin-walled sections, **local buckling** of flanges or webs occurs before global failure. This is a stability phenomenon not predicted by the linear beam theory. *Real scenario: Cold-formed steel sections, thin-walled aluminium extrusions.*

---

#### Solution to Question B, Part (b)

**Step 1: Start from the moment-curvature relation (2 marks)**

From the derivation, we have:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

**Step 2: Exact curvature formula from differential calculus (2 marks)**

For a deflected beam axis $y = y(x)$, the exact radius of curvature is given by:

$$R = \frac{\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}{\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}$$

Inverting:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

**Step 3: Apply the small-slope approximation (2 marks)**

**Approximation Used**: For small deflections, the slope $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is very small (typically less than 0.01 rad or about 0.6°). Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \approx 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2} \approx 1$$

So:

$$\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

**Justification of validity**: This approximation is valid when the deflection $y$ is small compared to the span $L$ — typically $y \leq L/250$ in structural design. For most practical KTU problems, this holds true.

**Step 4: Equate and obtain the ODE (1 mark)**

Equating the two expressions for $\frac{1}{R}$:

$$\frac{M}{EI} = \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

$$\boxed{EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

This is the **Governing Differential Equation of the Elastic Curve** of the beam.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 1 hour before the KTU exam.**

### 🔑 Core Equations (Must Memorize)

- **Moment-Curvature Relation**: $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ or $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$
- **Approximate Curvature**: $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ (small slopes)
- **Exact Curvature**: $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2y/dx^2}{\left[1+(dy/dx)^2\right]^{3/2}}$
- **Governing ODE of Elastic Curve**: $EI\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$
- **Maximum Bending Stress**: $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I}$

### 🔑 Top 10 Assumptions (High-Frequency Exam Favourite)

1. ✅ Hooke's law valid (linear elastic, isotropic, homogeneous)
2. ✅ Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to N.A.
3. ✅ Shear deformation neglected
4. ✅ Small deflections, small slopes
5. ✅ Beam initially straight and prismatic
6. ✅ Uniform $EI$ along length
7. ✅ N.A. coincides with centroidal axis
8. ✅ Self-weight neglected
9. ✅ $R \gg$ beam cross-section dimensions
10. ✅ No torsional deformation, loading in plane of symmetry

### 🔑 Top 5 Limitations (Examiner's Favourite)

1. ❌ Fails for deep beams ($L/h < 10$)
2. ❌ Fails for short beams
3. ❌ Cannot capture shear deformation
4. ❌ Cannot model local buckling / warping / stress concentrations
5. ❌ Elastic only — cannot model plastic behaviour

### 🔑 Key Conceptual Distinctions

- **Assumptions** = Postulates used in derivation (assumed true).
- **Limitations** = Scenarios where the theory breaks down (actually false).
- **Sign Convention** = $EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ with sagging-positive $M$ and downward-positive $y$.

### 🔑 Common Sign-Convention Pitfalls to Avoid

| Situation | Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Sagging beam, $y$ positive downward | Writing $EI\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -M$ | Write $EI\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = +M$ |
| Hogging moment region (cantilever) | Forgetting the negative sign in $M(x)$ | Insert the sign correctly: $M(x) = -wx^2/2$ etc. |
| Macaulay brackets | Forgetting to apply $\langle x-a \rangle^n = 0$ for $x < a$ | Always use Macaulay brackets for discontinuous loadings |

### 🔑 Units to Remember (Always Mention in Exams)

- $M$ in **N·m** (or kN·m)
- $E$ in **N/m² = Pa** (or GPa)
- $I$ in **m⁴** (or mm⁴)
- $EI$ in **N·m²**
- $R$ in **m**
- $y$ in **m** (deflection)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner's Advice**: The Moment-Curvature Relation is the **single most important equation** of Module 3. If you master it along with the differential equation $EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$, you have the foundation to solve **every problem** that follows — Double Integration, Macaulay's Method, Moment-Area, and Conjugate Beam. **Spend 80% of your preparation time on this foundational topic.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Integration Method, Macaulay's Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Double Integration Method, Macaulay's Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double Integration Method** is a classical method of structural analysis used to determine the **slope** and **deflection** of a beam by integrating the governing differential equation of the elastic curve **twice**, subject to appropriate boundary and continuity conditions.

The method is rooted in the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory**, which states that for a prismatic beam subjected to bending, the curvature at any section is directly proportional to the bending moment at that section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Differential Equation (Elastic Line Equation):**
> $$EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$
> where $E$ = Young's Modulus, $I$ = Second Moment of Area (both constant for a uniform beam), $y$ = vertical deflection, and $M(x)$ = bending moment as a function of $x$.

**Macaulay's Method** is an elegant extension of the double integration method developed by **W. H. Macaulay (1919)**. It uses **singularity functions (Macaulay's brackets)** to write a single, unified expression for the bending moment that automatically "switches off" loading contributions at points where they do not physically act. This avoids the cumbersome need to write separate moment equations for different segments of a beam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Macaulay's Bracket Notation:** $\langle x - a \rangle^n$
> - Evaluated as $(x - a)^n$ when $x > a$
> - Evaluated as $0$ when $x \le a$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Slope is the Velocity, the Deflection is the Distance Traveled":**
> Imagine driving a car along a curved road. The **bending moment** $M(x)$ at any point of the road is like the **steering wheel input** you give. The **slope** $\frac{dy}{dx}$ at any point is like the **direction (angle)** your car is pointing at that instant — it is the *first integral* of the steering input. The **deflection** $y$ is like how far your car has *drifted sideways* from the straight centerline — it is the *second integral* (cumulative effect).
>
> - First integration (one sum) → slope
> - Second integration (cumulative sum) → deflection
> - Just like physics: $v = \int a \, dt$ and $x = \int v \, dt$, here:
> $$EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\, dx + C_1 \quad \text{and} \quad EI\, y = \int \!\!\int M(x)\, dx^2 + C_1 x + C_2$$

Now, **Macaulay's brackets** are like **automatic gates or filters** on the road. Suppose there is a speed breaker (a point load) only after a certain distance $a$. The bracket $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ is a clever filter that says: *"Only contribute to my journey AFTER I have crossed the distance $a$."* This avoids having to break the journey into multiple road segments and writing separate equations for each — Macaulay wrote the **entire journey as a single equation**.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units (SI) |
|:------:|:--------|:------------------:|
| $E$ | Young's Modulus of beam material | **N/m²** or **Pa** (or GPa) |
| $I$ | Second Moment of Area of cross-section | **m⁴** or **mm⁴** |
| $EI$ | Flexural Rigidity | **N·m²** |
| $M(x)$ | Bending moment at section $x$ | **N·m** or **kN·m** |
| $\theta = \frac{dy}{dx}$ | Slope of the beam | **radians** (dimensionless) |
| $y$ | Vertical deflection | **m** or **mm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Double Integration Method

- **Step 1 — Establish Coordinate System:** Fix the origin at the left support (cantilever) or at any convenient point. Take $x$ along the beam axis and $y$ along the deflection direction (typically downward positive in beam convention).

- **Step 2 — Compute Reactions:** Using equilibrium equations ($\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$), find support reactions.

- **Step 3 — Write $M(x)$:** Cut the beam at a generic section $x$ and write the bending moment. **In simple cases** (single load type), one $M(x)$ expression suffices.

- **Step 4 — First Integration (Slope Equation):**
  $$EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\, dx + C_1$$
  This gives the slope $\theta = \frac{dy}{dx}$ as a function of $x$ with one integration constant $C_1$.

- **Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection Equation):**
  $$EI\, y = \int\!\!\int M(x)\, dx^2 + C_1 x + C_2$$
  This yields the deflection $y(x)$ with a second constant $C_2$.

- **Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions:** Substitute known values of slope or deflection at supports to evaluate $C_1$ and $C_2$.
  - **Simply supported beam:** $y = 0$ at both ends (slope is free — not prescribed).
  - **Cantilever beam:** $y = 0$ AND $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at the fixed end.
  - **Fixed beam:** $y = 0$ AND $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at both ends (requires superposition method; pure double integration cannot solve this directly because $M(x)$ depends on unknown redundants).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of Plain Double Integration:** When the loading has discontinuities (point loads, UDLs starting/ending at intermediate points, concentrated moments), the moment function $M(x)$ **changes form** at every discontinuity. One would need to write **separate moment equations** for each region, integrate each separately, and apply **continuity conditions** (matching slope and deflection at interior junctions). This becomes algebraically heavy.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Macaulay's Method

- **Step 1 — Write a single $M(x)$ using Macaulay's brackets** spanning the **entire beam length** without breaking into segments.

- **Step 2 — Standard Singularity Functions Used:**
  - Point load $W$ at $x = a$ → contribution to $M(x)$: $-W\langle x - a \rangle^1$
  - UDL $w$ starting at $x = a$ and continuing → contribution: $-\dfrac{w}{2}\langle x - a \rangle^2$
  - UVL (linearly varying load) starting at $x = a$ → contribution: $-\dfrac{w}{6}\langle x - a \rangle^3$
  - Applied couple $M_0$ at $x = a$ → contribution: $+M_0 \langle x - a \rangle^0$
    *(Note: sign depends on sense and chosen sign convention.)*

- **Step 3 — Integrate twice** (as in the double integration method) to obtain:
  $$EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\, dx + C_1$$
  $$EI\, y = \int\!\!\int M(x)\, dx^2 + C_1 x + C_2$$

- **Step 4 — Evaluate the brackets at the end** ($x = L$): at that point, **all brackets evaluate to their normal expressions**.

- **Step 5 — Apply only TWO boundary conditions** (one at each support) to find $C_1$ and $C_2$. No continuity matching is needed — the brackets handle that automatically!

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Macaulay's Method Works:** When you integrate $\langle x - a \rangle^n$, you get $\dfrac{\langle x - a \rangle^{n+1}}{n+1}$. For $x < a$, the bracket is zero, so the entire term stays zero throughout. This means the moment "effectively turns on" only after $x = a$, mimicking the physical reality of a load that exists only on one side of the section.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Card — Deflection of Beams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:--------|
| 1 | Elastic Line Equation | $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | Foundation equation |
| 2 | First Integration (Slope) | $EI\theta = \int M(x)\, dx + C_1$ | $\theta = \frac{dy}{dx}$ |
| 3 | Second Integration (Deflection) | $EIy = \int\!\!\int M(x)\, dx^2 + C_1 x + C_2$ | Two constants |
| 4 | Macaulay's Bracket | $\langle x - a \rangle^n = (x-a)^n$ if $x>a$, else $0$ | Singularity function |
| 5 | Contribution of Point Load $W$ at $a$ | $-W\langle x - a \rangle^1$ | Sign: downward load gives negative moment contribution to right of cut |
| 6 | Contribution of UDL $w$ from $a$ | $-\dfrac{w}{2}\langle x - a \rangle^2$ | UDL starts at $x=a$ |
| 7 | Contribution of UVL (zero at $a$, $w$ at $b$) | $-\dfrac{w_0}{6(b-a)}\langle x - b \rangle^3 + \dfrac{w_0}{6(b-a)}\langle x - a \rangle^3$ | Use with care |
| 8 | Contribution of Couple $M_0$ at $a$ | $M_0 \langle x - a \rangle^0$ | Sign depends on sense |
| 9 | Integration of Bracket | $\int \langle x - a \rangle^n dx = \dfrac{\langle x - a \rangle^{n+1}}{n+1} + \text{const}$ | Direct rule |
| 10 | Boundary Conditions (Simply Supported) | $y(0) = 0, \; y(L) = 0$ | Two equations, two unknowns |
| 11 | Boundary Conditions (Cantilever at $x=0$) | $y(0) = 0, \; \theta(0) = 0$ | Fixed end |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** Adopt **sagging moment = positive** AND **downward deflection = positive** consistently. If you use the opposite convention, the equation changes sign. KTU examiners deduct marks for inconsistent sign conventions.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Girders:** Under Indian Railway Loading Standards (IRS) or IRC class A loading, vehicles create concentrated point loads at varying positions. Macaulay's method lets engineers compute deflection envelopes efficiently.
- **Machine Tool Spindles:** Designers must limit deflection to within micron tolerances for precision machining — these methods give first-cut analytical estimates.
- **Aircraft Wings:** Spar deflection under aerodynamic loading is estimated using such beam-deflection methods.
- **Robotic Arms:** Cantilever-type beams with tip payloads — deflection directly affects positional accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Governing Equation $EI\,\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$

Consider an initially straight beam of uniform flexural rigidity $EI$. After loading, its neutral axis deflects into a curve $y(x)$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the deformed shape.**
For a small element of length $dx$, the radius of curvature $\rho$ at that point is related to the slope $\frac{dy}{dx}$ by the classical curvature formula (for small slopes):

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho} \approx \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$$

This is the geometric curvature — purely a property of the deflected shape.

**Step 2 — Mechanics of bending (Euler-Bernoulli).**
From the bending stress distribution $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ and the linear strain $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{\rho}$, Hooke's law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ gives:

$$\dfrac{M y}{I} = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate the common factor $y$** (the distance from neutral axis):

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$$

**Step 4 — Equate geometry and mechanics:**

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}$$

$$\boxed{EI\,\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

This is the **Master Differential Equation of the Elastic Curve** — the cornerstone of beam deflection analysis.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Double Integration Method on a Cantilever

**Problem:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$. A point load $W$ acts downward at the free end. Find the slope and deflection at the free end.

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
The fixed end provides vertical reaction $R = W$ and moment reaction $M_R = WL$.

**Step 2 — Bending moment at section $x$:**
Cut the beam at distance $x$ from the fixed end. Considering the right-hand portion (length $L - x$), only the point load $W$ is on it, located at distance $(L - x)$ from the cut.

$$M(x) = -W(L - x)$$

(Negative because a downward load on the right side creates a hogging — concave downward — moment.)

**Step 3 — First integration:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int -W(L - x)\, dx + C_1$$

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -WLx + \dfrac{Wx^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second integration:**

$$EI\,y = \int \left(-WLx + \dfrac{Wx^2}{2} + C_1\right) dx + C_2$$

$$EI\,y = -\dfrac{WLx^2}{2} + \dfrac{Wx^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Boundary conditions:**
At $x = 0$ (fixed end): deflection is zero, slope is zero.

- $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\Big|_{x=0} = 0 \implies \dfrac{1}{EI}\left(0 + 0 + C_1\right) = 0 \implies C_1 = 0$
- $y(0) = 0 \implies \dfrac{1}{EI}\left(0 + 0 + 0 + C_2\right) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$

**Step 6 — Slope and deflection at free end $x = L$:**

$$\theta_L = \dfrac{dy}{dx}\Big|_{x=L} = \dfrac{1}{EI}\left(-WL \cdot L + \dfrac{WL^2}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{WL^2}{2EI}$$

The negative sign indicates the beam slopes downward (consistent with our sign convention — negative slope for downward tilting).

$$y_L = \dfrac{1}{EI}\left(-\dfrac{WL^2 \cdot L}{2} + \dfrac{W L^3}{6}\right) = -\dfrac{WL^3}{6EI} + \dfrac{WL^3}{6EI} = -\dfrac{WL^3}{3EI}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} = -\dfrac{WL^2}{2EI} \quad \text{and} \quad \delta_{\max} = -\dfrac{WL^3}{3EI}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnitude Check:** These match the standard textbook results for a cantilever with end point load — confirming correctness.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Macaulay's Method on a Simply Supported Beam with Intermediate UDL

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over the **entire span**, and a concentrated load $W$ at the **mid-span**. Find the maximum deflection.

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} + \dfrac{W}{2}$.

**Step 2 — Macaulay's moment expression (for $0 \le x \le L$, taking section to the right of A):**

Consider a section at distance $x$ from A. The bending moment is:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w}{2}\langle x - 0 \rangle^2 - \dfrac{W}{2}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^1$$

Substituting $R_A$:

$$M(x) = \left(\dfrac{wL}{2} + \dfrac{W}{2}\right) x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2} - \dfrac{W}{2}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^1$$

**Step 3 — First integration (Slope):**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \left(\dfrac{wL}{2} + \dfrac{W}{2}\right)\dfrac{x^2}{2} - \dfrac{w}{2} \cdot \dfrac{x^3}{3} - \dfrac{W}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\langle x - L/2 \rangle^2}{2} + C_1$$

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \left(\dfrac{wL}{4} + \dfrac{W}{4}\right) x^2 - \dfrac{w x^3}{6} - \dfrac{W}{4}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^2 + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second integration (Deflection):**

$$EI\,y = \left(\dfrac{wL}{4} + \dfrac{W}{4}\right)\dfrac{x^3}{3} - \dfrac{w}{6} \cdot \dfrac{x^4}{4} - \dfrac{W}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\langle x - L/2 \rangle^3}{3} + C_1 x + C_2$$

$$EI\,y = \left(\dfrac{wL}{12} + \dfrac{W}{12}\right) x^3 - \dfrac{w x^4}{24} - \dfrac{W}{12}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^3 + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Boundary conditions (Simply Supported):**

- **At $x = 0$:** $y = 0$. Substituting:
  $0 = 0 - 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$
  
- **At $x = L$:** $y = 0$. Substituting (note that $L > L/2$, so all brackets evaluate):
  $$0 = \left(\dfrac{wL}{12} + \dfrac{W}{12}\right) L^3 - \dfrac{w L^4}{24} - \dfrac{W}{12}\left(L - \dfrac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1 L$$
  
  $$0 = \dfrac{wL^4}{12} + \dfrac{WL^3}{12} - \dfrac{wL^4}{24} - \dfrac{W}{12} \cdot \dfrac{L^3}{8} + C_1 L$$
  
  $$0 = \dfrac{2wL^4 - wL^4}{24} + \dfrac{WL^3}{12} - \dfrac{WL^3}{96} + C_1 L$$
  
  $$0 = \dfrac{wL^4}{24} + \dfrac{8WL^3 - WL^3}{96} + C_1 L$$
  
  $$0 = \dfrac{wL^4}{24} + \dfrac{7WL^3}{96} + C_1 L$$
  
  $$C_1 = -\dfrac{wL^3}{24} - \dfrac{7WL^2}{96}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum deflection (by symmetry at $x = L/2$):**

At $x = L/2$, the bracket $\langle x - L/2 \rangle$ becomes zero, so:

$$EI\,y_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{wL}{12} + \dfrac{W}{12}\right)\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \dfrac{w}{24}\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^4 + C_1 \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$$

$$EI\,y_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{wL}{12} + \dfrac{W}{12}\right)\dfrac{L^3}{8} - \dfrac{wL^4}{384} + \left(-\dfrac{wL^3}{24} - \dfrac{7WL^2}{96}\right)\dfrac{L}{2}$$

$$EI\,y_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^4}{96} + \dfrac{WL^3}{96} - \dfrac{wL^4}{384} - \dfrac{wL^4}{48} - \dfrac{7WL^3}{192}$$

Combining $w$-terms:
$$\dfrac{wL^4}{96} - \dfrac{wL^4}{384} - \dfrac{wL^4}{48} = \dfrac{4wL^4 - wL^4 - 8wL^4}{384} = -\dfrac{5wL^4}{384}$$

Combining $W$-terms:
$$\dfrac{WL^3}{96} - \dfrac{7WL^3}{192} = \dfrac{2WL^3 - 7WL^3}{192} = -\dfrac{5WL^3}{192}$$

$$EI\,y_{\max} = -\dfrac{5wL^4}{384} - \dfrac{5WL^3}{192}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = -\dfrac{5wL^4}{384\,EI} - \dfrac{5WL^3}{192\,EI}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Sub-Cases:**
> - Setting $W = 0$: $y_{\max} = -\dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ → standard result for a UDL on a simply supported beam ✓
> - Setting $w = 0$: $y_{\max} = -\dfrac{5WL^3}{192EI} = -\dfrac{WL^3}{38.4\,EI}$... wait, let me re-check. The standard result for a central point load on a simply supported beam is $\dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$. Let's recompute.
> 
> Re-doing the $W$-only case: from $C_1 = -\dfrac{7WL^2}{96}$:
> 
> $$EI\,y_{\max} = \dfrac{WL^3}{96} - \dfrac{7WL^3}{192} = \dfrac{2WL^3 - 7WL^3}{192} = -\dfrac{5WL^3}{192}$$
> 
> Hmm, that gives $\dfrac{5}{192}$ instead of $\dfrac{1}{48} = \dfrac{4}{192}$. **Let me re-examine the calculation.**

**Re-examination (W-only case):**

Going back to the slope equation with $w = 0$:

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{W}{4}x^2 - \dfrac{W}{4}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^2 + C_1$$

At $x = 0$: $y = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$.

At $x = L$: $y = 0$:

$$0 = \dfrac{W L^3}{12} - \dfrac{W}{12}(L/2)^3 + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \dfrac{WL^3}{12} - \dfrac{W L^3}{96} + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \dfrac{8WL^3 - WL^3}{96} + C_1 L = \dfrac{7WL^3}{96} + C_1 L$$

$$C_1 = -\dfrac{7WL^2}{96}$$

At $x = L/2$:

$$EI\,y_{\max} = \dfrac{W}{12}\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1 \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$$

$$= \dfrac{WL^3}{96} - \dfrac{7WL^2}{96} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{WL^3}{96} - \dfrac{7WL^3}{192}$$

$$= \dfrac{2WL^3 - 7WL^3}{192} = -\dfrac{5WL^3}{192}$$

This is inconsistent with the standard $\dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Self-Correction Trigger:** The discrepancy suggests an algebraic error in the moment equation setup. Re-examining the standard derivation: for a simply supported beam with central point load $W$, $R_A = R_B = W/2$. The moment expression is:
> 
> $$M(x) = \dfrac{W}{2}x - \dfrac{W}{2}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^1 \quad (\text{not } \dfrac{W}{2}\cdot 2x \text{ form})$$
> 
> **My error above:** I wrote $R_A = \dfrac{wL}{2} + \dfrac{W}{2}$, but then multiplied by $x$ — that is correct. However, the W contribution should be $-\dfrac{W}{2}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^1$ for the right-side cut, but the **coefficient is $W$, not $W/2$** — because the full point load $W$ contributes once.
> 
> Re-derivation (correct): $M(x) = \left(\dfrac{wL}{2} + \dfrac{W}{2}\right)x - \dfrac{wx^2}{2} - W \langle x - L/2 \rangle^1$ (the point load $W$ enters with full magnitude).
> 
> In my earlier writing I had $\dfrac{W}{2}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^1$, which double-counts (since the reaction already includes $W/2$ but the point load's "extra" contribution is $W$, not $W/2$). **The correct treatment is:** the reaction at A accounts for $W/2$ upward; the remaining $W/2$ is implicitly transferred. For Macaulay's, the cleanest approach is:
> 
> Take the moment considering **forces to the LEFT of the section**:
> $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2} - W \langle x - L/2 \rangle^1$$
> with $R_A = \dfrac{wL}{2} + \dfrac{W}{2}$. So $M(x) = \left(\dfrac{wL}{2} + \dfrac{W}{2}\right)x - \dfrac{wx^2}{2} - W \langle x - L/2 \rangle^1$.

**Corrected integration:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \left(\dfrac{wL}{4} + \dfrac{W}{4}\right) x^2 - \dfrac{w x^3}{6} - \dfrac{W \langle x - L/2 \rangle^2}{2} + C_1$$

$$EI\,y = \left(\dfrac{wL}{12} + \dfrac{W}{12}\right) x^3 - \dfrac{w x^4}{24} - \dfrac{W \langle x - L/2 \rangle^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- $y(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$
- $y(L) = 0$:

$$0 = \left(\dfrac{wL}{12} + \dfrac{W}{12}\right) L^3 - \dfrac{w L^4}{24} - \dfrac{W}{6}\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \dfrac{wL^4}{12} + \dfrac{WL^3}{12} - \dfrac{wL^4}{24} - \dfrac{WL^3}{48} + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \dfrac{2wL^4 - wL^4}{24} + \dfrac{4WL^3 - WL^3}{48} + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \dfrac{wL^4}{24} + \dfrac{3WL^3}{48} + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \dfrac{wL^4}{24} + \dfrac{WL^3}{16} + C_1 L$$

$$C_1 = -\dfrac{wL^3}{24} - \dfrac{WL^2}{16}$$

**At $x = L/2$ (bracket vanishes):**

$$EI\,y_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{wL}{12} + \dfrac{W}{12}\right)\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \dfrac{w}{24}\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^4 + C_1 \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$$

$$= \dfrac{wL^4}{96} + \dfrac{WL^3}{96} - \dfrac{wL^4}{384} - \dfrac{wL^4}{48} - \dfrac{WL^3}{32}$$

Combining $w$-terms:
$$\dfrac{wL^4}{96} - \dfrac{wL^4}{384} - \dfrac{wL^4}{48} = \dfrac{4wL^4 - wL^4 - 8wL^4}{384} = -\dfrac{5wL^4}{384}$$

Combining $W$-terms:
$$\dfrac{WL^3}{96} - \dfrac{WL^3}{32} = \dfrac{WL^3 - 3WL^3}{96} = -\dfrac{2WL^3}{96} = -\dfrac{WL^3}{48}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = -\dfrac{5wL^4}{384\,EI} - \dfrac{WL^3}{48\,EI}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Sub-Cases (Corrected):**
> - Setting $W = 0$: $y_{\max} = -\dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ ✓ (matches standard)
> - Setting $w = 0$: $y_{\max} = -\dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$ ✓ (matches standard)
> - Both contributions are linearly superposed as expected by Maxwell-Betti reciprocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lessons from the Self-Correction Above (KTU Examiner Insight):**
> 1. **Always double-check sign conventions** — the point load in Macaulay's equation must appear with magnitude $W$, not $W/2$, when the reaction $R_A = W/2$ already accounts for half.
> 2. **Standard result verification** is a powerful self-check. Always verify against known special cases ($W = 0$ or $w = 0$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Method

| Feature | Double Integration | Macaulay's Method |
|:--------|:-------------------|:------------------|
| Number of moment equations | One per segment | One for whole beam |
| Number of integration constants | $2 \times$ (number of segments) | **Always 2** |
| Need for continuity equations at intermediate points | **Yes** | **No** |
| Handles point loads, UDLs, couples at intermediate points | Awkwardly | **Elegantly** |
| Computational effort | Increases with each discontinuity | Roughly constant |
| Applicable to fixed beams? | Only with redundants method | Only with redundants method |
| KTU exam prevalence | High (introductory) | **Very High** (full-mark problems) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Macaulay's Method Algorithm

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete procedural logic of Macaulay's method from loading specification to final deflection evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Beam Specified with Loading and Supports"] --> B["Step 1: Compute Support Reactions R_A and R_B"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Identify all Load Locations: Point Loads, UDL Starts/Ends, Couples"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Write Single M of x Expression using Macaulay Brackets"]
    D --> D1["R_A times x - w times bracket squared divided by 2 - W times bracket linear ... etc"]
    D1 --> E["Step 4: First Integration to obtain EI dy/dx = theta"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Second Integration to obtain EI y = deflection"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions at Supports"]
    G --> G1["Simply Supported: y at 0 equals 0 AND y at L equals 0"]
    G1 --> H["Step 7: Solve for Integration Constants C1 and C2"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Substitute C1 and C2 back into Deflection Equation"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Identify Location of Maximum Deflection using Symmetry or d2y/dx2 equals 0"]
    J --> K["END: Report Slope theta of x and Deflection y of x"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Each rectangular node represents a procedural step in Macaulay's method. The branching at **Step 6** distinguishes between support types. KTU students are expected to internalize this sequence — examiners often award partial credit for showing the procedural logic even if a final algebraic error slips in.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Singularity Function Library

The following block diagram maps each physical loading scenario to its corresponding Macaulay's bracket representation in the moment equation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOAD["Physical Loading on Beam"]
        L1["Concentrated Load W at x equals a"]
        L2["UDL w starting at x equals a"]
        L3["UVL zero at a, peak w at b"]
        L4["Applied Couple M0 at x equals a"]
    end

    subgraph MAC["Macaulay Bracket Form in M of x"]
        M1["minus W times bracket x minus a to power 1"]
        M2["minus w divided by 2 times bracket x minus a to power 2"]
        M3["minus w divided by 6 times bracket x minus b to power 3 plus w divided by 6 times bracket x minus a to power 3"]
        M4["plus or minus M0 times bracket x minus a to power 0"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Contribution to Slope and Deflection"]
        O1["Integrate once for slope, twice for deflection"]
    end

    L1 --> M1
    L2 --> M2
    L3 --> M3
    L4 --> M4
    M1 --> O1
    M2 --> O1
    M3 --> O1
    M4 --> O1
```

---

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Double Integration vs Macaulay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DI["DOUBLE INTEGRATION METHOD"]
        DI1["Write M of x separately for each segment"]
        DI2["Integrate each segment separately"]
        DI3["Apply boundary conditions at supports"]
        DI4["Apply continuity of slope and deflection at each junction"]
        DI5["Solve large system of equations for all constants"]
        DI1 --> DI2 --> DI3 --> DI4 --> DI5
    end

    subgraph MM["MACAULAY METHOD"]
        MM1["Write single M of x for entire beam using brackets"]
        MM2["Integrate twice for whole beam"]
        MM3["Apply only 2 boundary conditions"]
        MM4["Brackets automatically handle discontinuities"]
        MM5["Solve 2 equations for C1 and C2"]
        MM1 --> MM2 --> MM3 --> MM4 --> MM5
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Insight:** The Macaulay branch has fewer decision nodes and no continuity-matching step. This is the **key advantage** that makes Macaulay's method the KTU-preferred approach for non-uniformly loaded beams.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the governing differential equation of the elastic curve for a prismatic beam. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Equation:** $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$
>
> **Physical Significance:**
> - The **left-hand side** $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ represents the **internal bending resistance** of the beam. The factor $EI$ is the **flexural rigidity**, and $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ is the curvature of the deflected shape.
> - The **right-hand side** $M(x)$ is the **external bending moment** applied at section $x$ due to loading and reactions.
> - The equation states that **at every point along the beam, the curvature produced by the bending moment is directly proportional to that moment and inversely proportional to the flexural rigidity.**
> - This equation is the foundation for computing slopes and deflections by integrating twice.
>
> *(Word count: ~95 — appropriate for 3-mark answer)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain Macaulay's bracket notation $\langle x - a \rangle^n$. How does it simplify the integration process?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Macaulay's bracket $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ is defined as:
> $$\langle x - a \rangle^n = \begin{cases} (x - a)^n & \text{if } x > a \\ 0 & \text{if } x \le a \end{cases}$$
>
> **Simplification it brings:**
> 1. **Single Moment Equation:** Instead of writing separate $M(x)$ for each segment of the beam, a single equation covering the whole beam length can be written. Loads automatically "switch off" before their point of application.
> 2. **Only Two Constants of Integration:** Unlike plain double integration (where $n$ segments require $2n$ constants and continuity matching at junctions), Macaulay's method always requires only $C_1$ and $C_2$.
> 3. **No Continuity Equations Needed:** The bracket structure inherently enforces continuity of slope and deflection at load discontinuities.
> 4. **Algebraic Efficiency:** Reduces the computational burden significantly for beams with multiple point loads, UDLs, and couples.
>
> *(Word count: ~110 — appropriate for 3-mark answer)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **30 kN/m** over the **left half** (from $x = 0$ to $x = 3$ m) and a concentrated load of **60 kN** at the **mid-span** ($x = 3$ m). Determine the slope at the supports and the maximum deflection using **Macaulay's method**. Take $EI$ as constant.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Slope at Supports [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
Let $R_A$ at $x = 0$ and $R_B$ at $x = 6$ m.

$$\sum M_A = 0: \quad R_B \cdot 6 - 30 \cdot 3 \cdot 1.5 - 60 \cdot 3 = 0$$
$$6 R_B = 135 + 180 = 315 \implies R_B = 52.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$\sum F_y = 0: \quad R_A = (30 \times 3) + 60 - 52.5 = 90 + 60 - 52.5 = 97.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Macaulay's moment expression (section at distance $x$ from A):**

The UDL of 30 kN/m acts from $x = 0$ to $x = 3$. To express it using Macaulay's brackets that "switch off" after $x = 3$, we can equivalently consider the UDL **starting at $x = 0$** AND a **negative UDL of 30 kN/m starting at $x = 3$**. Equivalently, write it directly as a UDL from $0$ to $3$ using the trick of $\langle x \rangle^2 - \langle x - 3 \rangle^2$ inside the integral.

The cleanest form is:

$$M(x) = 97.5\,x - \dfrac{30}{2}\langle x - 0 \rangle^2 + \dfrac{30}{2}\langle x - 3 \rangle^2 - 60 \langle x - 3 \rangle^1$$

The "+30/2 × ⟨x−3⟩²" term subtracts out the UDL beyond $x = 3$.

**Step 3 — First integration:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 97.5 \cdot \dfrac{x^2}{2} - \dfrac{30}{2} \cdot \dfrac{x^3}{3} + \dfrac{30}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\langle x - 3 \rangle^3}{3} - 60 \cdot \dfrac{\langle x - 3 \rangle^2}{2} + C_1$$

$$EI\,\theta = 48.75\,x^2 - 5\,x^3 + 5\,\langle x - 3 \rangle^3 - 30\,\langle x - 3 \rangle^2 + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second integration:**

$$EI\,y = 48.75 \cdot \dfrac{x^3}{3} - 5 \cdot \dfrac{x^4}{4} + 5 \cdot \dfrac{\langle x - 3 \rangle^4}{4} - 30 \cdot \dfrac{\langle x - 3 \rangle^3}{3} + C_1 x + C_2$$

$$EI\,y = 16.25\,x^3 - 1.25\,x^4 + 1.25\,\langle x - 3 \rangle^4 - 10\,\langle x - 3 \rangle^3 + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Boundary conditions:**

$y(0) = 0$: $0 = 0 - 0 + 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$

$y(6) = 0$ (all brackets evaluate since $6 > 3$):

$$0 = 16.25 \cdot 216 - 1.25 \cdot 1296 + 1.25 \cdot (3)^4 - 10 \cdot (3)^3 + 6 C_1$$

Computing each term:
- $16.25 \times 216 = 3510$
- $1.25 \times 1296 = 1620$
- $1.25 \times 81 = 101.25$
- $10 \times 27 = 270$

$$0 = 3510 - 1620 + 101.25 - 270 + 6 C_1$$
$$0 = 1721.25 + 6 C_1$$
$$C_1 = -286.875 \text{ kN·m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Slopes at supports:**

At $x = 0$:
$$EI\,\theta_A = 0 - 0 + 0 - 0 + C_1 = -286.875$$
$$\boxed{\theta_A = -\dfrac{286.875}{EI} \text{ rad} = -\dfrac{0.286875 \times 10^6}{EI} \text{ rad (with EI in N·m}^2\text{)}}$$

By symmetry of loading? — No, the loading is **NOT symmetric** (UDL only on left half). So $\theta_B \ne -\theta_A$.

At $x = 6$:
$$EI\,\theta_B = 48.75 \times 36 - 5 \times 216 + 5 \times 27 - 30 \times 9 + C_1$$
$$= 1755 - 1080 + 135 - 270 - 286.875$$
$$= 253.125$$
$$\boxed{\theta_B = \dfrac{253.125}{EI} \text{ rad} \approx +\dfrac{0.253125 \times 10^6}{EI}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Maximum Deflection [7 marks]**

The maximum deflection occurs where $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ (slope is zero). Since loading is asymmetric, we must find this point by setting the slope equation to zero and solving numerically or by trial.

**Slope equation for $0 \le x \le 3$ (brackets vanish):**

$$EI\,\theta = 48.75\,x^2 - 5\,x^3 - 286.875 = 0$$

Let us try $x = 3$:
$$EI\,\theta(3) = 48.75 \times 9 - 5 \times 27 - 286.875 = 438.75 - 135 - 286.875 = 16.875$$

Try $x = 2.9$:
$$EI\,\theta(2.9) = 48.75 \times 8.41 - 5 \times 24.389 - 286.875 = 409.99 - 121.94 - 286.875 = 1.175$$

Try $x = 2.85$:
$$EI\,\theta(2.85) = 48.75 \times 8.1225 - 5 \times 23.149 - 286.875 = 395.97 - 115.74 - 286.875 = -6.645$$

So the root lies between $x = 2.85$ and $x = 2.9$. Linear interpolation:
$$x_{\max} \approx 2.85 + 0.05 \times \dfrac{6.645}{6.645 + 1.175} \approx 2.85 + 0.05 \times 0.850 = 2.893 \text{ m}$$

**Deflection at $x \approx 2.89$ m (brackets vanish since $2.89 < 3$):**

$$EI\,y = 16.25 \cdot x^3 - 1.25 \cdot x^4 - 286.875 x$$

$$= 16.25 \times (2.893)^3 - 1.25 \times (2.893)^4 - 286.875 \times 2.893$$

Computing:
- $(2.893)^3 \approx 24.221$
- $(2.893)^4 \approx 70.07$
- $16.25 \times 24.221 \approx 393.59$
- $1.25 \times 70.07 \approx 87.59$
- $286.875 \times 2.893 \approx 830.04$

$$EI\,y_{\max} \approx 393.59 - 87.59 - 830.04 = -524.04$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} \approx -\dfrac{524.04}{EI} \text{ m (with appropriate units)}}$$

The negative sign indicates **downward** deflection (sagging, as expected under downward loads).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A cantilever beam of length **4 m** is fixed at $x = 0$ and carries:
- A point load of **20 kN** at the free end ($x = 4$ m).
- A uniformly distributed load of **10 kN/m** over the entire span.

Determine the **slope and deflection at the free end** using the **double integration method**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Setting up the moment equation and first integration [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Reactions at the fixed end:**
- Vertical reaction $R = 20 + 10 \times 4 = 60$ kN (upward)
- Moment reaction $M_R = 20 \times 4 + 10 \times 4 \times 2 = 80 + 80 = 160$ kN·m

**Step 2 — Bending moment at section $x$:**
Take the section at distance $x$ from the fixed end. Consider the LEFT portion (fixed end side).

$$M(x) = -M_R + R \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$
$$M(x) = -160 + 60x - 5x^2$$

**Step 3 — First integration:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\, dx = -160x + 30x^2 - \dfrac{5x^3}{3} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second integration:**

$$EI\,y = -80x^2 + 10x^3 - \dfrac{5x^4}{12} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Boundary conditions (fixed end at $x = 0$):**

$y(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}\Big|_{x=0} = 0 \implies C_1 = 0$

Therefore:
$$EI\,\theta = -160x + 30x^2 - \dfrac{5x^3}{3}$$
$$EI\,y = -80x^2 + 10x^3 - \dfrac{5x^4}{12}$$

---

**Part (b) — Slope and deflection at the free end ($x = 4$ m) [7 marks]**

**At $x = 4$ m:**

$$EI\,\theta(4) = -160(4) + 30(16) - \dfrac{5(64)}{3}$$
$$= -640 + 480 - \dfrac{320}{3}$$
$$= -160 - 106.667$$
$$= -266.667$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\text{free}} = -\dfrac{266.667}{EI} \text{ rad} \approx -\dfrac{0.26667 \times 10^6}{EI} \text{ rad}}$$

$$EI\,y(4) = -80(16) + 10(64) - \dfrac{5(256)}{12}$$
$$= -1280 + 640 - \dfrac{1280}{12}$$
$$= -640 - 106.667$$
$$= -746.667$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{\text{free}} = -\dfrac{746.667}{EI} \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by superposition:** A cantilever with end point load $W$ gives $\delta = \dfrac{WL^3}{3EI} = \dfrac{20 \times 64}{3EI} = \dfrac{426.67}{EI}$. A cantilever with full UDL gives $\delta = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI} = \dfrac{10 \times 256}{8EI} = \dfrac{320}{EI}$. Sum: $\dfrac{746.67}{EI}$ ✓ (matches exactly).
>
> **Slope verification:** $\theta_W = \dfrac{WL^2}{2EI} = \dfrac{20 \times 16}{2EI} = \dfrac{160}{EI}$; $\theta_w = \dfrac{wL^3}{6EI} = \dfrac{10 \times 64}{6EI} = \dfrac{106.67}{EI}$. Sum: $\dfrac{266.67}{EI}$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Note:** The verification by superposition, while not required, earns **bonus understanding marks** and demonstrates mastery — KTU examiners appreciate when students cross-check results using two different methods.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams (Module 3 — Beam Deflection):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often mix sagging-positive with hogging-positive within the same solution. Pick ONE convention and stick with it throughout. KTU follows the **standard sagging-positive convention**.
>
