> 2. **Forgetting to Apply Continuity at Junctions (in plain Double Integration) (–3 marks):** When a beam has multiple loads creating segments, students forget that slope and deflection must match at intermediate points. With Macaulay's method this is automatic; with plain double integration it is mandatory.
>
> 3. **Wrong Bracket Coefficient (–2 marks):** For a UDL $w$ starting at $x = a$, the Macaulay term is $-\dfrac{w}{2}\langle x - a \rangle^2$, NOT $-w\langle x - a \rangle^2$. The factor of 1/2 comes from integration of the load distribution over the half-region.
>
> 4. **Point Load Coefficient Error (–2 marks):** The term for a point load $W$ at $x = a$ is $-W\langle x - a \rangle^1$, NOT $-\dfrac{W}{2}\langle x - a \rangle^1$. The reaction at the support already accounts for half — do NOT halve again.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Evaluate Brackets at End (–1 to –2 marks):** When applying the boundary condition $y(L) = 0$, every bracket becomes an explicit numerical term. Students sometimes leave the bracket unevaluated, leaving $C_1$ in an unsolvable form.
>
> 6. **Units Inconsistency (–1 mark):** If load is in kN and length in m, then $EI$ must be in **kN·m²**, deflections in **m**, and slopes in **radians**. Mixing units is a frequent deduction.
>
> 7. **Missing $C_2$ (–1 mark):** Forgetting the constant of integration in the deflection equation is a classic oversight. Both $C_1$ and $C_2$ must be evaluated.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Double Integration & Macaulay's Method**

- **Foundation equation:** $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ — the elastic curve equation, derived from Euler-Bernoulli beam theory and Hooke's law.

- **Two integrations give:** First integration → slope ($\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$); Second integration → deflection ($y$).

- **Two constants always:** $C_1$ and $C_2$ appear regardless of beam complexity (in Macaulay's method).

- **Boundary conditions:**
  - Simply supported: $y = 0$ at both ends; slope is **free**.
  - Cantilever (fixed at $x=0$): $y = 0$ AND $\theta = 0$ at $x = 0$.
  - Fixed-fixed: $y = 0$ AND $\theta = 0$ at **both** ends — requires redundant force method.

- **Macaulay's bracket:** $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ is **zero for $x \le a$** and equals $(x-a)^n$ for $x > a$.

- **Common Macaulay terms:**
  - Point load $W$ at $x=a$: $-W\langle x-a \rangle^1$
  - UDL $w$ starting at $x=a$: $-\dfrac{w}{2}\langle x-a \rangle^2$
  - Couple $M_0$ at $x=a$: $\pm M_0 \langle x-a \rangle^0$

- **Standard results to memorize** (these are KTU favorites):
  - SS beam + central point load: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$ at mid-span.
  - SS beam + full UDL: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ at mid-span.
  - Cantilever + end point load: $\delta = \dfrac{WL^3}{3EI}$, $\theta = \dfrac{WL^2}{2EI}$.
  - Cantilever + full UDL: $\delta = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$, $\theta = \dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$.

- **Superposition principle:** For beams carrying multiple simple loads, the total deflection at any point is the **algebraic sum** of deflections due to each load acting individually (provided the beam remains in the linear elastic regime).

- **Always verify** your answer by checking special cases ($W = 0$, $w = 0$, etc.) against known textbook formulas.

- **Macaulay's method is preferred** for any beam with **more than one segment** — it eliminates the need for continuity matching.

- **Plain double integration** is suitable for **single, uniform loading** (e.g., full UDL on a simply supported beam) — fewer algebraic steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress on an Inclined Plane due to Uniaxial Stress, Biaxial Stress, and Two Normal Stresses Accompanied by Shear Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stress on an Inclined Plane due to Uniaxial Stress, Biaxial Stress, and Two Normal Stresses Accompanied by Shear Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Concept Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress on an Inclined Plane** refers to the resolution of the internal stress state at a point in a loaded body into **normal (direct) stress** `σ_θ` and **tangential (shear) stress** `τ_θ` acting on a plane whose outward normal is inclined at a generic angle `θ` to a chosen reference axis (conventionally the x-axis).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCMUT402 – Mechanics of Solids**, this topic is a foundational gateway to **Mohr's Circle**, **principal stress analysis**, and **failure criteria** (Maximum Shear Stress Theory & Distortion Energy Theory). The state of stress at any point in a 2D continuum is fully described by **three independent components**: `σ_x`, `σ_y`, and `τ_xy`. Knowing these, the stress on *any* arbitrarily inclined plane passing through that point can be uniquely determined.

## 1.2 The Three Canonical Stress States

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Categorization**: The syllabus explicitly asks students to treat three progressively complex loading cases.

### (a) Uniaxial Stress State
A stress condition in which **only one principal normal stress** acts on the element, while the other two are identically zero.

$$
\sigma_x = \sigma \quad ; \quad \sigma_y = 0 \quad ; \quad \tau_{xy} = 0
$$

**Real-World Analogy**: Imagine pulling a soft rubber band with both hands along a single direction. The band is being pulled along *one axis only* — this is the purest form of uniaxial tension. A circular bar under a pure axial tensile load (like a cable supporting a hanging lamp) experiences exactly this state at every interior point.

### (b) Biaxial Stress State
A stress condition in which **two principal normal stresses** act on the element along mutually perpendicular axes, with no shear stress.

$$
\sigma_x \neq 0 \quad ; \quad \sigma_y \neq 0 \quad ; \quad \tau_{xy} = 0
$$

**Real-World Analogy**: Think of a rectangular pressure vessel's wall in plane stress. The wall is stretched horizontally by internal pressure (hoop stress) and vertically by a different amount (longitudinal stress), but the two faces do not slide on each other — no shear, only biaxial tension.

### (c) Two Normal Stresses Accompanied by Shear Stresses (General State)
The **most general 2D stress state**, where normal stresses `σ_x`, `σ_y` and complementary shear stress `τ_xy` all act simultaneously.

$$
\sigma_x \neq 0 \quad ; \quad \sigma_y \neq 0 \quad ; \quad \tau_{xy} \neq 0
$$

**Real-World Analogy**: A shaft under combined axial load and torsion. The fibers on the surface experience direct stress from the axial pull *and* shear stress from the twisting moment. The two combine to produce a more complex stress state on any inclined plane you cut through the shaft.

## 1.3 Physical Significance for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> 1. It is the **pre-requisite building block** for Mohr's Circle (Module 4 onwards).
> 2. It is **directly tested** in Part A (3-mark definitions) and Part B (14-mark analytical problems).
> 3. It links directly to **Course Outcome CO2**: "Apply stress transformation principles to analyze 2D stress states."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Foundation

The mathematical backbone of stress transformation rests on **two fundamental principles**:

1. **Cauchy's Stress Principle**: The traction vector (force per unit area) on any plane through a point is uniquely determined by the stress tensor at that point.
2. **Static Equilibrium**: When a small wedge-shaped element is isolated from a stressed body, the sum of forces in any direction must equal zero (since the element is in static equilibrium).

### Key Logic Steps for Stress Resolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Logic (Memorize for KTU Valuation):**
> 1. Isolate an infinitesimal **wedge (triangular) element** with the inclined face of interest.
> 2. Resolve all applied forces on the two perpendicular faces into `x` and `y` components.
> 3. Resolve the unknown stresses `σ_θ` and `τ_θ` on the inclined face into `x` and `y` components.
> 4. Apply `ΣF_x = 0` and `ΣF_y = 0` to obtain two equations.
> 5. Solve simultaneously for `σ_θ` and `τ_θ`.

## 2.2 The Three Stress States — Formulation

### Case 1: Uniaxial Stress

The inclined plane has its **outward normal at angle θ** from the x-axis. Setting `σ_y = 0` and `τ_xy = 0` in the general equations:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \sigma \cos^2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = \sigma \sin\theta \cos\theta = \frac{\sigma}{2}\sin(2\theta)
$$

**Engineering Insight**: For a uniaxial tension `σ`, the maximum normal stress on an inclined plane is `σ` (at `θ = 0°`, i.e., on a plane perpendicular to the load). The maximum shear stress is `σ/2` and occurs on a plane inclined at `θ = 45°` to the load axis. This is why **ductile materials under tension often fail on planes at 45°** to the load.

### Case 2: Biaxial Stress

With `τ_xy = 0`, but `σ_x` and `σ_y` both non-zero:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta)
$$

**Engineering Insight**: In biaxial loading, the maximum shear stress on an inclined plane is `(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2` and occurs at `θ = 45°`. The principal planes themselves (`θ = 0°` and `θ = 90°`) carry no shear — only normal stress.

### Case 3: General 2D Stress (Two Normal + Shear)

The full general equations — the **most important result for KTU examinations**:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention for KTU**: The shear stress `τ_θ` is taken as **positive** if it acts in the counter-clockwise sense on the inclined face. The angle `θ` is measured **counter-clockwise** from the x-axis to the outward normal of the inclined plane.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of KTU 2024 numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Stress State | Normal Stress on Inclined Plane | Shear Stress on Inclined Plane | Principal Stress | Max Shear Stress | Principal Plane Angle |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uniaxial** `\sigma_x = \sigma` | `\sigma \cos^2\theta` | `\dfrac{\sigma}{2}\sin(2\theta)` | `\sigma_1 = \sigma, \sigma_2 = 0` | `\tau_{max} = \sigma/2` at `\theta = 45°` | `\theta_p = 0°` |
| 2 | **Biaxial** (no shear) | `\sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta` | `\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta)` | `\sigma_x, \sigma_y` themselves | `\tau_{max} = \vert\sigma_x - \sigma_y\vert/2` at `\theta = 45°` | `\theta_p = 0°, 90°` |
| 3 | **General 2D** (with shear) | `\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)` | `-\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)` | `\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}` | `\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}` | `\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}` |
| 4 | **Invariant Relation** | `\sigma_{\theta} + \sigma_{\theta+90°} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y` (Sum of normal stresses on perpendicular planes is invariant) | `\tau_{\theta} = -\tau_{\theta+90°}` (Shear stresses on perpendicular planes are equal and opposite) | N/A | N/A | N/A |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Practical Use of Stress Transformation |
|---|---|
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Calculating stresses on welded joints inclined to the longitudinal axis |
| **Shaft Design** | Finding maximum stress on helical spring wire, which is at an angle to the axis |
| **Dam & Retaining Wall Analysis** | Principal stress trajectories used to predict crack propagation direction |
| **Composite Laminates** | Off-axis ply stress transformation in fiber-reinforced materials |
| **Geotechnical Engineering** | Slope stability analysis where failure planes occur at specific inclinations |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Stress on Inclined Plane — General 2D Case

### Setup

Consider a 2D rectangular element subjected to stresses `σ_x`, `σ_y` (tensile positive) and complementary shear stress `τ_xy` (positive counter-clockwise) on its faces. We wish to find the stresses `σ_θ` and `τ_θ` on a plane whose outward normal makes an angle `θ` (measured counter-clockwise) with the x-axis.

### Step 1: Isolate the Wedge Element

Cut the element along a plane whose normal is at angle `θ` to the x-axis. This creates a **triangular wedge** with:
- Inclined face (hypotenuse) of area `dA`
- Vertical face of area `dA \cdot \sin\theta` (this face has outward normal along `-x` direction)
- Horizontal face of area `dA \cdot \cos\theta` (this face has outward normal along `-y` direction)

### Step 2: Identify Forces on Each Face

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used**: On a face with outward normal in the **positive coordinate direction**, the stresses `σ_x`, `σ_y`, `τ_xy` are taken positive in the positive coordinate directions (tensile normal; counter-clockwise shear). On opposite (negative) faces, the signs of all stresses flip (Newton's third law).

**On the vertical face** (area `dA \sin\theta`, outward normal along `-x`):
- Normal force (along x-axis): `-\sigma_x \cdot dA \sin\theta`
- Shear force (along y-axis): `-\tau_{xy} \cdot dA \sin\theta`

**On the horizontal face** (area `dA \cos\theta`, outward normal along `-y`):
- Normal force (along y-axis): `-\sigma_y \cdot dA \cos\theta`
- Shear force (along x-axis): `-\tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cos\theta`

**On the inclined face** (area `dA`, outward normal at angle `θ` to x-axis):
- Normal force: `+\sigma_{\theta} \cdot dA` along the outward normal direction
- Shear force: `+\tau_{\theta} \cdot dA` along the tangent direction (perpendicular to the normal)

### Step 3: Resolve Inclined Face Forces into Cartesian Components

The outward normal direction makes angle `θ` with the x-axis, so:
- Normal force x-component: `+\sigma_{\theta} \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta`
- Normal force y-component: `+\sigma_{\theta} \cdot dA \cdot \sin\theta`

The tangent direction (counter-clockwise from the normal) makes angle `(θ + 90°)` with the x-axis, so:
- Shear force x-component: `-\tau_{\theta} \cdot dA \cdot \sin\theta`
- Shear force y-component: `+\tau_{\theta} \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta`

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign on the shear x-component** follows the convention that positive `τ_θ` acts in the counter-clockwise sense on the inclined face, which translates to a `-x` component when the normal is in the first quadrant.

### Step 4: Apply Equilibrium — Sum of Forces in x-Direction

$$
\sum F_x = 0
$$

$$
-\sigma_x \cdot dA \sin\theta - \tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cos\theta + \sigma_{\theta} \cdot dA \cos\theta - \tau_{\theta} \cdot dA \sin\theta = 0
$$

Dividing through by `dA` and rearranging:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} \cos\theta - \tau_{\theta} \sin\theta = \sigma_x \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos\theta \quad \cdots \text{(Eq. 1)}
$$

### Step 5: Apply Equilibrium — Sum of Forces in y-Direction

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
-\sigma_y \cdot dA \cos\theta - \tau_{xy} \cdot dA \sin\theta + \sigma_{\theta} \cdot dA \sin\theta + \tau_{\theta} \cdot dA \cos\theta = 0
$$

Dividing through by `dA` and rearranging:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} \sin\theta + \tau_{\theta} \cos\theta = \sigma_y \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta \quad \cdots \text{(Eq. 2)}
$$

### Step 6: Solve the System for `σ_θ`

Multiply Equation 1 by `cos\theta` and Equation 2 by `sin\theta`, then add:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} \cos^2\theta - \tau_{\theta}\sin\theta\cos\theta + \sigma_{\theta} \sin^2\theta + \tau_{\theta}\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin^2\theta
$$

The `τ_θ` terms cancel, and `cos²θ + sin²θ = 1`, so:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \sigma_y \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta)
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}
$$

Applying the double-angle identities `2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)` and `cos²θ + sin²θ = 1`:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a useful intermediate form**, but the **standard KTU textbook form** uses `cos(2θ)` paired with the difference `(σ_x − σ_y)/2`. We can convert by using the identity `sin(2θ) = 1 − cos(2θ) − 2sin²θ` ... a cleaner way is to use the alternative derivation below.

### Alternative Standard Derivation (KTU Preferred Form)

Starting from Eq. 1 and Eq. 2, multiply Eq. 1 by `cosθ` and Eq. 2 by `sinθ` but use a **different approach** — apply the transformation `θ → θ + 90°` to obtain the **complementary equation**, then add the two.

For the plane at angle `(θ + 90°)` (i.e., the perpendicular plane), the normal stresses and shear stresses are:

$$
\sigma_{\theta + 90°} = \sigma_x \sin^2\theta + \sigma_y \cos^2\theta - \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta + 90°} = (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta - \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) = -\tau_{\theta}
$$

**Adding the two complementary equations** for `σ_θ` and `σ_{θ+90°}`:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} + \sigma_{\theta+90°} = \sigma_x(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) + \sigma_y(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) = \sigma_x + \sigma_y
$$

This proves the **stress invariant**: `σ_θ + σ_{θ+90°} = σ_x + σ_y` (always).

**Multiplying Eq. 1 by `sinθ`** and **Eq. 2 by `cosθ`** and **subtracting** leads to the expression for `τ_θ`. After algebra and using double-angle identities:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)}
$$

### Step 7: Verify with Special Case (Uniaxial)

Set `σ_x = σ`, `σ_y = 0`, `τ_{xy} = 0`:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma + 0}{2} + \frac{\sigma - 0}{2}\cos(2\theta) + 0 = \frac{\sigma}{2}[1 + \cos(2\theta)] = \frac{\sigma}{2} \cdot 2\cos^2\theta = \sigma \cos^2\theta \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma - 0}{2}\sin(2\theta) + 0 = -\frac{\sigma}{2}\sin(2\theta)
$$

The sign difference (negative `sin(2θ)`) arises from the **sign convention** — some textbooks define `τ_θ` positive when acting on the face in a way that creates clockwise rotation. KTU follows the **counter-clockwise positive convention**, so we may write `τ_θ = (σ/2) sin(2θ)` if we measure the angle from the face's normal appropriately. **The magnitude is `σ/2` at `θ = 45°` — which is the key engineering result.**

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Principal Stresses

### Setup

**Principal stresses** are the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** at a point, occurring on planes where the shear stress is zero. Set `τ_θ = 0` in the shear equation:

$$
-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This gives **two values of `2θ_p` separated by `180°`**, hence two values of `θ_p` separated by `90°`. These are the two principal plane orientations.

### Solve for the Principal Stresses

Substituting the value of `θ_p` (or `2θ_p`) into the normal stress equation. A more elegant approach is the **invariant method**: the principal stresses are the roots of the **characteristic equation**:

$$
\sigma^2 - (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\sigma + \left[\sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2\right] = 0
$$

This is obtained by eliminating `θ` from the `σ_θ` and `τ_θ` equations using the identity `sin²(2θ) + cos²(2θ) = 1`. The roots are:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

By convention, `σ_1 ≥ σ_2`. Both are **real** because the term under the square root is always non-negative.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Maximum Shear Stress

The shear stress `τ_θ` is maximum when its derivative with respect to `θ` is zero:

$$
\frac{d\tau_{\theta}}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos(2\theta) - 2\tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta) = 0
$$

This gives:

$$
\tan(2\theta_s) = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}
$$

Notice that `2θ_s = 2θ_p \pm 90°`, i.e., the planes of maximum shear stress are **inclined at 45° to the principal planes**.

The maximum shear stress is:

$$
\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On the plane of maximum shear stress, the normal stress is NOT zero** — it equals the **average normal stress** `(σ_x + σ_y)/2`. This is a subtle but vital point for KTU examinations.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Uniaxial Stress as a Special Case

Set `σ_y = 0`, `τ_{xy} = 0` in the general formulas:

**Normal stress** on plane at angle `θ`:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{\sigma}{2}\cos(2\theta) = \frac{\sigma}{2}[1 + \cos(2\theta)] = \sigma \cos^2\theta
$$

**Shear stress** on the same plane:

$$
\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma}{2}\sin(2\theta)
$$

The **magnitude** of the shear stress is `(\sigma/2) \sin(2\theta)`, which is maximum at `θ = 45°`, giving `τ_max = σ/2`. At this plane, the normal stress is `σ/2` (not zero!).

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Biaxial Stress as a Special Case

Set `τ_{xy} = 0` only:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta)
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta)
$$

Maximum shear: `τ_max = (σ_x − σ_y)/2` at `θ = 45°`. Principal stresses are simply `σ_x` and `σ_y` themselves (the given axes are already the principal axes).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Transformation Logic Flow

The following block-level functional flow maps the conceptual sequence from a 2D stress state to the inclined plane stresses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[2D Element at a Point] --> B[Identify Stress Components: sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy]
    B --> C{Select Stress State Category}
    C -->|Case 1| D1[Uniaxial: sigma_x = sigma, others zero]
    C -->|Case 2| D2[Biaxial: sigma_x and sigma_y nonzero, tau_xy = zero]
    C -->|Case 3| D3[General 2D: All three components nonzero]
    D1 --> E[Choose Inclined Plane Angle theta]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Transformation Equations]
    F --> G1[Compute Normal Stress sigma_theta]
    F --> G2[Compute Shear Stress tau_theta]
    G1 --> H{Optimization Step}
    G2 --> H
    H -->|Set tau_theta to zero| I1[Find Principal Stresses sigma_1, sigma_2]
    H -->|Set d_tau_d_theta to zero| I2[Find Maximum Shear Stress tau_max]
    I1 --> J[Compute Principal Plane Angle theta_p]
    I2 --> K[Max Shear Plane Angle = theta_p plus 45 degrees]
    J --> L[Final Output: Stress State on Any Inclined Plane]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Wedge Element Free-Body Setup

The diagram below illustrates the analytical model used in the derivation — a triangular wedge cut from a 2D stressed element.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph W["WEDGE ELEMENT — Free Body Diagram"]
        direction TB
        VFace["Vertical Face: Area dA sin theta, Normal along minus x"]
        HFace["Horizontal Face: Area dA cos theta, Normal along minus y"]
        IFace["Inclined Face: Area dA, Normal at angle theta from plus x axis"]
        VFace --- IFace
        HFace --- IFace
    end
    VFace -->|sigma_x and tau_xy forces| Fx1[Force Components in x and y]
    HFace -->|sigma_y and tau_xy forces| Fx1
    IFace -->|Unknown sigma_theta and tau_theta| Fx1
    Fx1 --> Eq[Apply Sum Fx equals zero AND Sum Fy equals zero]
    Eq --> Result[Result: sigma_theta and tau_theta in terms of theta]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Case Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Stress on Inclined Plane: Master Framework] --> Case1
    Start --> Case2
    Start --> Case3
    Case1[Case 1: Uniaxial — sigma_x equals sigma, sigma_y equals zero, tau_xy equals zero] --> F1[sigma_theta equals sigma cos squared theta]
    Case1 --> F2[tau_theta equals sigma over 2 times sin of 2 theta]
    Case1 --> F3[Max shear at theta equals 45 degrees equals sigma over 2]
    Case2[Case 2: Biaxial — sigma_x, sigma_y nonzero, tau_xy equals zero] --> G1[sigma_theta equals sigma_x cos squared theta plus sigma_y sin squared theta]
    Case2 --> G2[tau_theta equals sigma_x minus sigma_y over 2 times sin of 2 theta]
    Case2 --> G3[Principal axes coincide with given axes]
    Case3[Case 3: General — All three nonzero] --> H1[sigma_theta equals sigma_x plus sigma_y over 2 plus sigma_x minus sigma_y over 2 cos 2 theta plus tau_xy sin 2 theta]
    Case3 --> H2[tau_theta equals minus sigma_x minus sigma_y over 2 sin 2 theta plus tau_xy cos 2 theta]
    Case3 --> H3[Principal stresses from characteristic equation]
    Case3 --> H4[Max shear equals sqrt of squared half difference plus tau_xy squared]
```

## 4.4 Concept Map: From Given State to Final Result

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GIVEN[Given: sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy, and theta] --> CALC[Apply Transformation Formulas]
    CALC --> COMP1[Compute sigma_theta]
    CALC --> COMP2[Compute tau_theta]
    COMP1 --> CHECK{Special Angle Check}
    COMP2 --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|theta equals zero| P1[sigma equals sigma_x, tau equals zero]
    CHECK -->|theta equals 90 deg| P2[sigma equals sigma_y, tau equals zero]
    CHECK -->|theta equals 45 deg| P3[Max shear condition]
    CHECK -->|theta equals theta_p| P4[Principal stress condition, tau equals zero]
    P1 --> OUTPUT[Final Answer: Stress State on Inclined Plane]
    P2 --> OUTPUT
    P3 --> OUTPUT
    P4 --> OUTPUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with respect to stress on an inclined plane:**
(a) Principal stress
(b) Principal plane
(c) Maximum shear stress

#### Model Answer:

> (a) **Principal Stress**: The maximum and minimum values of normal stress occurring at a point in a stressed body, on planes where the shear stress is zero. Mathematically, `σ_1,2 = (σ_x + σ_y)/2 ± √[((σ_x − σ_y)/2)² + τ_xy²]`.

> (b) **Principal Plane**: A plane on which the shear stress is zero and only normal stress acts. Two such planes exist at every point, oriented at angle `θ_p` where `tan(2θ_p) = 2τ_xy / (σ_x − σ_y)`. The two principal planes are mutually perpendicular.

> (c) **Maximum Shear Stress**: The largest value of shear stress at a point, occurring on planes oriented at 45° to the principal planes. Its value is `τ_max = √[((σ_x − σ_y)/2)² + τ_xy²] = (σ_1 − σ_2)/2`.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between uniaxial, biaxial, and general 2D stress states. Give one practical example of each.**

#### Model Answer:

| Stress State | Description | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniaxial** | Only one normal stress component is non-zero; the other normal stress and all shear stresses are zero. | A straight circular bar under pure axial tensile load (e.g., a wire hanging a lamp) |
| **Biaxial** | Two perpendicular normal stresses are non-zero; shear stress is zero. | A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel wall (hoop + longitudinal stresses, no shear) |
| **General 2D** | Both normal stresses and complementary shear stress are non-zero. | The surface of a shaft subjected to combined axial tension and torsion |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors**: Forgetting that `τ_xy` in the formula is the **complementary shear stress** on the original element, not the shear on the inclined plane. Mixing up positive/negative shear directions can flip your final answer.
> 2. **Angle Definition Confusion**: Students often confuse whether `θ` is measured to the **plane** or to the **normal of the plane**. In KTU, `θ` is measured to the **outward normal** of the inclined plane.
> 3. **Units Inconsistency**: All stresses must be in **MPa** (or consistent units). Mixing kN/mm² with N/m² is a guaranteed zero.
> 4. **Principal Plane Quadrant**: When `tan(2θ_p)` is negative, the angle `2θ_p` lies in the 2nd or 4th quadrant. Don't blindly take the principal angle in the 1st quadrant — check by substituting back.
> 5. **Forgetting the Invariant Check**: The sum `σ_1 + σ_2 = σ_x + σ_y` must always hold. Use it as a self-check before writing the final answer.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

At a point in a structural steel member, the stresses on a rectangular element are:

`σ_x = 80 MPa (tensile), σ_y = 40 MPa (compressive), τ_xy = 30 MPa`

**(a)** Determine the **normal and shear stresses** on a plane whose outward normal makes an angle of **30° counter-clockwise** with the x-axis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the **principal stresses** and the **orientation of the principal planes**. Also compute the **maximum shear stress** at the point. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Given:**
`σ_x = 80 MPa, σ_y = −40 MPa, τ_xy = 30 MPa, θ = 30°`

**Step 1: Compute `2θ = 60°`** and find trigonometric values:

$$
\cos(2\theta) = \cos(60°) = 0.5
$$

$$
\sin(2\theta) = \sin(60°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
$$

**Step 2: Apply the normal stress transformation formula:**

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} + \frac{80 - (-40)}{2}(0.5) + (30)(0.866)
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = 20 + (60)(0.5) + 25.98
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = 20 + 30 + 25.98 = 75.98 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the shear stress transformation formula:**

$$
\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = -(60)(0.866) + (30)(0.5)
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = -51.96 + 15.00 = -36.96 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign indicates the shear stress acts in the **clockwise** sense on the inclined face (per KTU counter-clockwise positive convention).

**Result**: On the inclined plane at `θ = 30°`:
- Normal stress: `σ_θ = 75.98 MPa (tensile)`
- Shear stress: `τ_θ = 36.96 MPa (clockwise)`

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Compute the average normal stress (center of Mohr's circle):**

$$
\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the radius of Mohr's circle (which equals the maximum shear stress):**

$$
R = \tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{(60)^2 + (30)^2}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} = 67.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the principal stresses:**

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R = 20 + 67.08 = 87.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R = 20 - 67.08 = -47.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the principal plane angle:**

$$
\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2(30)}{80 - (-40)} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5
$$

$$
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_p = 13.28°
$$

**Step 5: Verify by substitution** (Substitute `θ = 13.28°` in the `τ_θ` formula — should yield zero).

**Final Answer**:
- Principal stresses: `σ_1 = 87.08 MPa, σ_2 = -47.08 MPa`
- Principal plane angles: `θ_p = 13.28°` and `θ_p + 90° = 103.28°`
- Maximum shear stress: `τ_max = 67.08 MPa`
- Orientation of max shear plane: `θ_s = θ_p + 45° = 58.28°`

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A circular bar of **40 mm diameter** is subjected to an axial tensile load of **120 kN**.

**(a)** Find the **normal and tangential stresses** on a plane whose outward normal makes an angle of **30°** with the axis of the bar. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the **maximum shear stress** in the material and the angle of the plane on which it acts. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Given:**
- Diameter `d = 40 mm`, so area `A = \pi d²/4 = \pi (40)²/4 = 1256.64 mm²`
- Axial tensile load `P = 120 kN = 120,000 N`
- `θ = 30°` (angle between normal to inclined plane and bar axis)
- This is a **uniaxial stress state** (only axial stress, no transverse, no shear on the original faces).

**Step 1: Compute the axial stress:**

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{120,000}{1256.64} = 95.49 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the uniaxial stress transformation:**

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta = 95.49 \times \cos^2(30°)
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = 95.49 \times (0.866)^2 = 95.49 \times 0.75 = 71.62 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin(2\theta) = \frac{95.49}{2}\sin(60°)
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = 47.75 \times 0.866 = 41.35 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Result**:
- Normal stress on inclined plane: `σ_θ = 71.62 MPa`
- Shear (tangential) stress on inclined plane: `τ_θ = 41.35 MPa`

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Maximum shear stress for uniaxial case** (at `θ = 45°`):

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} = \frac{95.49}{2} = 47.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Angle of maximum shear plane:**

The plane of maximum shear is at `θ = 45°` from the bar axis (since `τ_θ = (σ_x/2) sin(2θ)` is maximum when `2θ = 90°`).

**Step 3: Normal stress on the max shear plane** (often asked as a sub-part):

$$
\sigma_{45°} = \sigma_x \cos^2(45°) = 95.49 \times 0.5 = 47.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that the **normal stress on the plane of maximum shear is NOT zero** — it equals `σ_x/2 = τ_max`. This is a frequent KTU trick question.

**Final Answer**:
- Maximum shear stress: `τ_max = 47.75 MPa`
- Plane orientation: `θ = 45°` from bar axis
- Normal stress on that plane: `σ_{45°} = 47.75 MPa`

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- **General 2D stress transformation formulas** (the two most important equations):
  - `σ_θ = (σ_x + σ_y)/2 + (σ_x − σ_y)/2 · cos(2θ) + τ_xy · sin(2θ)`
  - `τ_θ = −(σ_x − σ_y)/2 · sin(2θ) + τ_xy · cos(2θ)`

- **Three special cases** in KTU Module 4:
  - **Uniaxial**: `σ_θ = σ cos²θ`, `τ_θ = (σ/2) sin(2θ)` — both `σ_y = 0` and `τ_xy = 0`
  - **Biaxial**: same form with `σ_x, σ_y` nonzero and `τ_xy = 0`
  - **General 2D**: full formula with all three components

- **Principal stress condition**: Set `τ_θ = 0` → `tan(2θ_p) = 2τ_xy / (σ_x − σ_y)`. Solve for `σ_1,2` from the characteristic equation or directly substitute.

- **Maximum shear stress condition**: `τ_max = √[((σ_x − σ_y)/2)² + τ_xy²] = (σ_1 − σ_2)/2`, occurring on planes at `45°` to the principal planes.

- **Stress invariants** (always true, use as self-check):
  - `σ_θ + σ_{θ+90°} = σ_x + σ_y`
  - `τ_θ = −τ_{θ+90°}`
  - `σ_1 + σ_2 = σ_x + σ_y`

- **Sign convention (KTU 2024 standard)**:
  - Normal stresses: tensile positive, compressive negative
  - Shear stress: positive when it tends to rotate the element **counter-clockwise**
  - Angle `θ`: measured **counter-clockwise** from the x-axis to the **outward normal** of the inclined plane

- **Common engineering maxima** (frequently asked as one-liners):
  - For uniaxial: `τ_max = σ/2` at `θ = 45°`
  - For biaxial: `τ_max = (σ_x − σ_y)/2` at `θ = 45°`
  - Normal stress on max shear plane = `σ_avg = (σ_x + σ_y)/2` (NOT zero!)

- **Conversion units commonly used in KTU numericals**:
  - 1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ N/m²
  - 1 GPa = 1000 MPa = 10⁹ N/m²
  - Force: kN → N (multiply by 1000) before dividing by area in mm²

- **Self-verification step** (always do this before submitting):
  1. Sum of principal stresses = `σ_x + σ_y` ✓
  2. At `θ = 0°`: `σ_θ = σ_x` and `τ_θ = τ_xy` ✓
  3. At `θ = 90°`: `σ_θ = σ_y` and `τ_θ = −τ_xy` ✓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Planes and Stresses, Mohr's Circle of Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principal Planes and Stresses, Mohr's Circle of Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **two-dimensional state of stress** at a point in a loaded body, the **normal stresses** $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ acting on two mutually perpendicular planes, along with the **complementary shear stress** $\tau_{xy}$ (which by the theorem of complementary shear stresses equals $\tau_{yx}$ in magnitude), constitute a **general state of stress**.

When we rotate our coordinate axes by an arbitrary angle $\theta$, the stresses on the new (rotated) planes change continuously. However, there exist **two specific orientations** of the cutting plane at which the **shear stress vanishes entirely**, leaving only **pure normal (direct) stress** acting on the plane. These unique orientations are called the **Principal Planes**, and the corresponding pure normal stresses are called the **Principal Stresses** (denoted $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$, where $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$).

By convention, **$\sigma_1$ is the maximum principal stress (algebraically largest)** and **$\sigma_2$ is the minimum principal stress (algebraically smallest)**. Note that on a principal plane, since $\tau = 0$, the corresponding stress on the plane perpendicular to it is automatically the other principal stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> The Principal Stresses are the **extreme values** of the normal stress that can occur at a point as the orientation of the reference axes is varied through $0^\circ$ to $360^\circ$. The maximum shear stress occurs on planes oriented at **45°** to the principal planes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Coin" Intuition

Imagine holding a **rectangular rubber stamp** under a load. The forces on the front and back faces are not necessarily pure pushes or pulls — they often involve some "twisting" (shear) component too. Now, slowly rotate this stamp around the line of sight (like spinning a coin on a table, but observing the stresses instead of motion).

- At **certain specific orientations**, the twisting (shear) component **completely disappears**. The faces of the stamp then experience only pure push or pure pull — these orientations are the **principal planes**, and the pure pushes/pulls are the **principal stresses**.
- At **orientations exactly 45° offset** from these positions, the shear stress becomes **maximum**, while the normal stress takes the **average value** of the two principal stresses.

This is exactly what we do mathematically — we search for the angle $\theta$ that **eliminates** shear stress on a plane, and the resulting normal stress is the principal stress.

### 1.3 Standard Sign Convention & Key Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) |
|----------|--------|-------------------|
| Normal stress | $\sigma$ | **N/m² (Pa)** or **MPa** = $10^6$ **N/m²** |
| Shear stress | $\tau$ | **N/m² (Pa)** or **MPa** |
| Orientation angle | $\theta$ | **radians** or **degrees** |
| Stress tensor order | — | **2nd order tensor** in 3-D |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **Tensile normal stresses** are taken as **positive (+)**.
> - **Compressive normal stresses** are taken as **negative (−)**.
> - **Shear stresses** that tend to rotate the element **clockwise** are taken as **positive (+)**; counter-clockwise rotations are **negative (−)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stress Transformation on an Inclined Plane — The Foundation

Consider a 2-D element with stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ acting on its faces. Cut this element along a plane whose **outward normal** makes an angle $\theta$ with the **x-axis** (measured counter-clockwise). The stresses acting on this inclined plane are:

- **Normal stress** $\sigma_\theta$ (perpendicular to the inclined plane)
- **Shear stress** $\tau_\theta$ (acting along the inclined plane)

By **equilibrium of the wedge-shaped element** (the Cauchy stress tetrahedron in 2-D), we obtain the transformation equations.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

1. **Set up the wedge:** Cut the original element along a line whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. The wedge has two known faces (x-face and y-face) and one inclined face.

2. **Resolve forces along x-direction:** Sum of forces along x equals zero.

3. **Resolve forces along y-direction:** Sum of forces along y equals zero.

4. **Solve for $\sigma_\theta$:** This is the normal component of the stress vector on the inclined plane, obtained by projecting the stress vector onto the outward normal.

5. **Solve for $\tau_\theta$:** This is the tangential component (along the plane).

6. **Differentiate $\sigma_\theta$ with respect to $\theta$** and set to zero to find the orientation at which normal stress is **maximum or minimum** — these are the **principal planes**.

7. **Differentiate $\tau_\theta$ with respect to $\theta$** and set to zero to find the orientation at which shear stress is **maximum or minimum** — these are the **planes of maximum shear**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ | Normal stress on plane at angle $\theta$ | **MPa** |
| 2 | $\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$ | Shear stress on plane at angle $\theta$ | **MPa** |
| 3 | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Principal stresses (max & min) | **MPa** |
| 4 | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Orientation of principal planes | **radians/degrees** |
| 5 | $\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Maximum shear stress | **MPa** |
| 6 | $\tau_{\min} = -\tau_{\max}$ | Minimum shear stress | **MPa** |
| 7 | $\tan 2\theta_s = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | Orientation of max shear planes | **radians/degrees** |
| 8 | $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45^\circ$ | Relationship between max shear and principal planes | **degrees** |
| 9 | Center of Mohr's Circle $C = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2},\, 0\right)$ | Average normal stress | **MPa** |
| 10 | Radius of Mohr's Circle $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Equal to $\tau_{\max}$ | **MPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention Check:** In formula (2) above, the negative sign is **mandatory** when using the **standard sign convention** (clockwise shear positive). Many KTU answer scripts lose marks because students omit this sign.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure vessel design (boilers, storage tanks):** Principal stresses determine whether a cylindrical shell will yield (yield criterion: max shear theory, principal stress theory).
- **Brittle material failure (cast iron, concrete):** Maximum **tensile** principal stress governs crack initiation — used in Mohr's strength theory.
- **Shaft design under combined bending and torsion:** Principal stresses help compute the equivalent von Mises stress for fatigue life estimation.
- **Bridge truss members, aircraft wings, ship hulls:** Determining stress-free directions is critical for **fiber-reinforced composite layups**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stresses on an Inclined Plane (Equilibrium of Wedge)

Let the original element have stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$. Cut along a line whose outward normal makes angle $\theta$ (counter-clockwise from x-axis). Let the inclined plane have unit length along the normal direction.

**Geometry of the wedge:**
- The face perpendicular to the x-axis has projected area = $\sin\theta$ (if inclined face area = 1).
- The face perpendicular to the y-axis has projected area = $\cos\theta$.

> **Step 1: Forces on the x-face** (area = $\sin\theta$):
> - Normal force: $\sigma_x \sin\theta$ acting along x-direction.
> - Shear force: $\tau_{xy} \sin\theta$ acting along y-direction.

> **Step 2: Forces on the y-face** (area = $\cos\theta$):
> - Normal force: $\sigma_y \cos\theta$ acting along y-direction.
> - Shear force: $\tau_{xy} \cos\theta$ acting along x-direction.

> **Step 3: Forces on the inclined face** (area = 1):
> - Resolved into $R_x$ (along x) and $R_y$ (along y).

> **Step 4: Equilibrium along x-direction** ($\Sigma F_x = 0$):

$$
R_x = \sigma_x \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos\theta
$$

> **Step 5: Equilibrium along y-direction** ($\Sigma F_y = 0$):

$$
R_y = \sigma_y \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta
$$

> **Step 6: Resolve $R_x$ and $R_y$ onto the inclined plane's normal and tangent directions.**

The outward normal to the inclined plane has components $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$, and the tangent direction (90° counter-clockwise from the normal) has components $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$.

> **Step 7: Normal stress $\sigma_\theta$** = projection of stress vector along the normal direction:

$$
\sigma_\theta = R_x \cos\theta + R_y \sin\theta
$$

> **Step 8: Substitute** the expressions for $R_x$ and $R_y$:

$$
\sigma_\theta = (\sigma_x \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos\theta)\cos\theta + (\sigma_y \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta)\sin\theta
$$

> **Step 9: Expand** the products:

$$
\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \cos\theta\sin\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin^2\theta
$$

> **Step 10: Group terms** using double-angle identities:
> - $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$
> - $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$
> - $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \qquad \textbf{(Final Result)}
$$

> **Step 11: Shear stress $\tau_\theta$** = projection onto tangent direction $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$:

$$
\tau_\theta = -R_x \sin\theta + R_y \cos\theta
$$

> **Step 12: Substitute and expand** using double-angle identities:

$$
\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta \qquad \textbf{(Final Result)}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Principal Stresses

The normal stress $\sigma_\theta$ is **maximum or minimum** when its derivative with respect to $\theta$ is zero.

> **Step 1: Differentiate** $\sigma_\theta$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$

> **Step 2: Set derivative equal to zero** for extremum:

$$
-(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0
$$

> **Step 3: Rearrange:**

$$
(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta = 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$

> **Step 4: Solve** for $2\theta$:

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

This gives **two values of $\theta_p$ separated by $90^\circ$** — corresponding to the two principal planes.

> **Step 5: To find $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$, use the identity:**

From Step 2, we have $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$. Construct a right triangle where:
- $\text{opposite side} = 2\tau_{xy}$
- $\text{adjacent side} = \sigma_x - \sigma_y$
- $\text{hypotenuse} = \sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}$

Therefore:
- $\sin 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}}$
- $\cos 2\theta_p = \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}}$

> **Step 6: Substitute** into $\sigma_\theta$ equation:

$$
\sigma = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}} + \tau_{xy} \cdot \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}
$$

> **Step 7: Combine** the last two terms over the common denominator:

$$
\sigma = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}{2\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}
$$

> **Step 8: Recognize** that $(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2 = 4\left[\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2\right]$, giving:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \qquad \textbf{(Principal Stresses)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Shear Stress

> **Step 1: Differentiate** $\tau_\theta$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$
\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

> **Step 2: Set derivative to zero:**

$$
-(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta = 0
$$

> **Step 3: Solve:**

$$
\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}
$$

> **Step 4: Multiply numerator and denominator** of formula (4) by $-1$ and compare:

$$
\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{1}{\tan 2\theta_p} = \cot\left(90^\circ - 2\theta_p\right)
$$

This means $2\theta_s = 2\theta_p \pm 90^\circ$, hence $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45^\circ$.

> **Step 5: Substitute into** $\tau_\theta$ formula and maximize. The maximum value is:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

> **Step 6: On planes of maximum shear,** the **normal stress** equals the **average** $\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ (since the $\cos 2\theta_s$ and $\sin 2\theta_s$ terms in $\sigma_\theta$ produce zero net contribution).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mohr's Circle of Stress (Graphical Method)

The expressions for $\sigma_\theta$ and $\tau_\theta$ can be recognized as the parametric equations of a **circle**.

> **Step 1: Square** both transformation equations:

$$
\left(\sigma_\theta - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta\right)^2
$$

$$
\tau_\theta^2 = \left(-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta\right)^2
$$

> **Step 2: Add** the two equations. Using the identity $\sin^2 2\theta + \cos^2 2\theta = 1$:

$$
\left(\sigma_\theta - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2
$$

> **Step 3: Recognize** the standard form of a circle equation:

$$
\left(\sigma_\theta - \sigma_c\right)^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = R^2
$$

where:
- Center: $\sigma_c = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ (on the $\sigma$-axis)
- Radius: $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$

This is **Mohr's Circle of Stress**, plotted in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane with **$\sigma$ along the abscissa** and **$\tau$ along the ordinate**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mohr's Circle — Functional Flow Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below illustrates the logical flow for constructing and interpreting Mohr's Circle, starting from given stresses, locating key points, and reading off principal stresses and maximum shear stress.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> B[Compute Center C = sigma_avg, 0]
    B --> C[Compute Radius R = sqrt of half-diff squared plus tau_xy squared]
    C --> D[Plot Point X: sigma_x, tau_xy on circle]
    D --> E[Plot Point Y: sigma_y, minus tau_xy on circle]
    E --> F[Verify XY chord passes through C and is a diameter]
    F --> G[Locate Sigma_1: Rightmost intersection of circle with sigma-axis]
    G --> H[Locate Sigma_2: Leftmost intersection of circle with sigma-axis]
    H --> I[Locate Tau_max: Topmost point of circle, value equals R]
    I --> J[Locate Tau_min: Bottommost point of circle, value equals minus R]
    J --> K[Measure angle from X to Sigma_1 axis = 2 theta_p]
    K --> L[Measure angle from X to Tau_max point = 2 theta_s]
    L --> M[Output: sigma_1, sigma_2, tau_max, theta_p, theta_s]
    M --> N[End]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Principal Planes and Maximum Shear Planes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Principal Plane 1 - Orientation theta_p] --> R1[Normal stress sigma_1, Shear zero]
    P2[Principal Plane 2 - Orientation theta_p + 90 degrees] --> R2[Normal stress sigma_2, Shear zero]
    S1[Max Shear Plane 1 - Orientation theta_p + 45 degrees] --> T1[Shear stress tau_max, Normal stress sigma_avg]
    S2[Max Shear Plane 2 - Orientation theta_p - 45 degrees] --> T2[Shear stress minus tau_max, Normal stress sigma_avg]
    P1 -.Rotation by 45 degrees.-> S1
    P1 -.Rotation by minus 45 degrees.-> S2
    P2 -.Rotation by 45 degrees.-> S2
```

### 4.3 Construction Algorithm for Mohr's Circle — Textual Schematic

| Step | Action | Resulting Feature |
|------|--------|-------------------|
| **1** | Set up $\sigma$-axis (horizontal) and $\tau$-axis (vertical) | Coordinate system |
| **2** | Mark point $X = (\sigma_x, +\tau_{xy})$ | **X-face point** on circle |
| **3** | Mark point $Y = (\sigma_y, -\tau_{xy})$ | **Y-face point** on circle |
| **4** | Draw line XY — it should be a **diameter** | Chord verification |
| **5** | Find midpoint of XY: $C = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$ | **Center** |
| **6** | Measure distance $CX = CY = R$ | **Radius** |
| **7** | Draw circle with center $C$ and radius $R$ | **Mohr's Circle** |
| **8** | Mark intersections with $\sigma$-axis: $(\sigma_1, 0)$ and $(\sigma_2, 0)$ | **Principal stresses** |
| **9** | Mark top and bottom of circle: $(C_\sigma, +R)$ and $(C_\sigma, -R)$ | **Max shear stresses** |
| **10** | Angle $\angle XCX_1 = 2\theta_p$ (measured on the circle, **clockwise** if $\tau_{xy}$ is plotted positive upward) | **Principal angle** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention for Angle on Mohr's Circle:**
> In Mohr's Circle, **angles are doubled** ($2\theta$ in the physical plane = $2\theta$ on the circle, but the angle on the circle is measured as an arc from the reference point). On the circle, the angle is measured in the **same sense as physical rotation** when using the **standard plotting convention** (positive $\tau$ plotted upward, positive $\sigma$ to the right). Always verify this by checking the direction of point Y from point X.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define principal stresses and principal planes. Show that on a principal plane the shear stress is zero.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Principal Plane:** A plane on which the **shear stress is zero** and only **normal stress acts** is called a **principal plane**.
>
> **Principal Stress:** The **normal stress acting on a principal plane** is called the **principal stress**. At any point in a stressed body, there exist **three principal stresses** (in 2-D, two), designated $\sigma_1$ (maximum) and $\sigma_2$ (minimum).

**Proof that shear stress is zero on a principal plane:**

The shear stress on an inclined plane is given by:
$$
\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$

For a principal plane, by definition $\tau_\theta = 0$:

$$
-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta_p = 0
$$

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

Substituting this value of $\theta_p$ back into the shear stress equation gives $\tau_{\theta_p} = 0$, confirming that the shear stress on the principal plane vanishes. The corresponding normal stress is:
$$
\sigma_p = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State and explain Mohr's circle of stress. Mention its salient features.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Mohr's Circle of Stress** is a **two-dimensional graphical representation** of the stress transformation equations, plotted in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane.

**Mathematical Basis:**

The transformation equations can be rearranged into the standard form of a circle:

$$
\left(\sigma_\theta - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2
$$

**Salient Features:**

1. The circle has its **center on the $\sigma$-axis** at $C = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$.
2. The **radius** is $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
3. The **principal stresses** $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are the **extreme abscissae** where the circle intersects the $\sigma$-axis.
4. The **maximum shear stress** equals the **radius** $R$ and occurs at the **topmost** point of the circle.
5. On planes of maximum shear, the **normal stress** equals the **average** $\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$.
6. Angles on the Mohr's circle are **double** the corresponding physical angles.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU ESE):** Answer **any ONE** full question from each module. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for normal and tangential stresses on an inclined plane in a 2-D stress system. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** At a point in a stressed body, the stresses on two mutually perpendicular planes are: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa (clockwise shear). Determine:
1. The **principal stresses** and their orientations.
2. The **maximum shear stress** and its orientation.
3. The **normal and shear stresses** on a plane whose normal makes $30^\circ$ with the x-axis. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B-A

#### **Part (a) — Derivation**

The complete derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student is expected to:

> 1. Sketch the wedge-shaped element with stresses on x-face, y-face, and inclined face (normal at angle $\theta$).
> 2. Write force equilibrium equations along x and y directions.
> 3. Resolve the resultant force along normal and tangent to the inclined plane.
> 4. Use double-angle identities to simplify to the final equations:

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$

**\[7 Marks\]** — Awarded for: free body diagram (2 marks), equilibrium equations (2 marks), trigonometric simplification (2 marks), final expressions (1 mark).

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem**

**Given:** $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa.

**Step 1: Compute average stress:**

$$
\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute half-difference:**

$$
\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 - 40}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Principal stresses:**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm \sqrt{(20)^2 + (30)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm \sqrt{400 + 900} = 60 \pm \sqrt{1300}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_1 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 4: Orientation of principal planes:**

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5
$$

$$
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) \approx 56.31^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_p \approx 28.15^\circ}
$$

(The second principal plane is at $\theta_p + 90^\circ = 118.15^\circ$.)

**Step 5: Maximum shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{(20)^2 + (30)^2} = \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6: Orientation of max shear planes:**

$$
\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{40}{60} = -\frac{2}{3}
$$

$$
2\theta_s = \tan^{-1}(-2/3) \approx -33.69^\circ
$$

$$
\theta_s \approx -16.85^\circ \quad (\text{or } 73.15^\circ)
$$

> **Verification:** $\theta_s = \theta_p - 45^\circ = 28.15^\circ - 45^\circ = -16.85^\circ$ ✓

**Step 7: Stresses on plane at $\theta = 30^\circ$:**

$$
\cos(2 \times 30^\circ) = \cos 60^\circ = 0.5
$$

$$
\sin(2 \times 30^\circ) = \sin 60^\circ = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
$$

**Normal stress:**

$$
\sigma_{30^\circ} = 60 + (20)(0.5) + (30)(0.866)
$$

$$
\sigma_{30^\circ} = 60 + 10 + 25.98 = \boxed{95.98 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{30^\circ} = -(20)(0.866) + (30)(0.5)
$$

$$
\tau_{30^\circ} = -17.32 + 15 = \boxed{-2.32 \text{ MPa}}
$$

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is **Mohr's circle**? Explain the procedure to construct it. With the help of Mohr's circle, derive the expressions for principal stresses and maximum shear stress. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A circular bar of diameter 60 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 90 kN and a torque of 2 kN·m simultaneously. Find:
1. The **principal stresses** in the material.
2. The **maximum shear stress** in the material. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B-B

#### **Part (a) — Mohr's Circle Theory**

The complete derivation is presented in **Section 3.4** of these notes. The procedure to construct Mohr's circle is:

1. Plot axes: $\sigma$ horizontal, $\tau$ vertical.
2. Locate point $X = (\sigma_x, +\tau_{xy})$ and $Y = (\sigma_y, -\tau_{xy})$.
3. Join XY — it is a diameter. Center $C$ is the midpoint.
4. Draw the circle with center $C$ and radius $R = CX = CY$.
5. The **principal stresses** are the $\sigma$-intercepts of the circle.
6. The **maximum shear stress** equals the radius $R$.

**Key derivations:**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm R
$$

$$
R = \tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

**\[7 Marks\]** — Awarded for: definition (1 mark), construction steps (2 marks), derivation of principal stress (2 marks), derivation of $\tau_{\max}$ (2 marks).

---

#### **Part (b) — Combined Loading Numerical**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 60$ mm, so radius $r = 30$ mm = $0.03$ m
- Axial load $P = 90$ kN = $90{,}000$ N (tensile)
- Torque $T = 2$ kN·m = $2000$ N·m

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.06)^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Axial (normal) stress on x-face:**

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90{,}000}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 31.83 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 31.83 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Polar moment of inertia:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi}{32} d^4 = \frac{\pi}{32} (0.06)^4 = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 4: Shear stress due to torsion:**

$$
\tau_{xy} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{2000 \times 0.03}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 47.15 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 47.15 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: On the y-face**, there is no axial load, so $\sigma_y = 0$ MPa.

**Step 6: Principal stresses:**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{31.83 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{31.83}{2}\right)^2 + (47.15)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 15.915 \pm \sqrt{(15.915)^2 + (47.15)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 15.915 \pm \sqrt{253.3 + 2223.1} = 15.915 \pm \sqrt{2476.4}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2476.4} \approx 49.76 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_1 = 15.915 + 49.76 = 65.68 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 15.915 - 49.76 = -33.85 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 7: Maximum shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{2476.4} \approx \boxed{49.76 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> **Verification using $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$:**
>
> $$
> \tau_{\max} = \frac{65.68 - (-33.85)}{2} = \frac{99.53}{2} = 49.76 \text{ MPa} \quad ✓
> $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign of $\tau_{xy}$:** Forgetting the negative sign in the shear stress transformation equation (formula 2 above). This propagates errors into both $\theta_p$ and $\theta_s$.
> 2. **Angle measurement:** Confusing the orientation of principal planes ($\theta_p$) with that of maximum shear planes ($\theta_s$). Remember: $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45^\circ$.
> 3. **Mohr's circle angle doubling:** Students often forget that the angle on Mohr's circle is **$2\theta$**, so the actual physical angle is **half** of what they measure on the circle.
> 4. **Unit consistency:** Mixing mm with m, or N with kN, is the #1 source of numerical errors. **Always convert to SI base units first.**
> 5. **Polar moment of inertia formula:** For a **solid circular shaft**, $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$, not $\dfrac{\pi d^4}{16}$ (which is for hollow shafts' polar moment about the same axis using mean radius).
> 6. **Sign of $\sigma_2$:** Many students incorrectly take only the magnitude of $\sigma_2$. The minimum principal stress can be **negative (compressive)** — this is critical for brittle material design.
> 7. **Forgetting "show that on a principal plane, shear is zero":** In definition-type 3-mark questions, KTU examiners specifically look for this proof — leaving it out costs full marks.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Principal planes** are orientations where shear stress is **zero** — only normal stress acts.
- ✅ **Two principal stresses** exist in 2-D: $\sigma_1$ (maximum) and $\sigma_2$ (minimum), and they act on **mutually perpendicular planes**.
- ✅ The transformation equation for **normal stress** on an inclined plane is:
$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$
- ✅ The transformation equation for **shear stress** on an inclined plane is:
$$
\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$
- ✅ Principal stresses:
$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$
- ✅ Maximum shear stress:
$$
\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}
$$
- ✅ Principal plane orientation:
$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$
- ✅ Max shear plane orientation:
$$
\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45^\circ
$$
- ✅ **Mohr's Circle** is a graphical tool with:
  - **Center** at $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$
  - **Radius** = $\tau_{\max}$
- ✅ On planes of **maximum shear**, the **normal stress equals the average** of $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$.
- ✅ **Invariant quantity:** $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ (sum of normal stresses is invariant under rotation).
- ✅ For **combined axial + torsion**: $\sigma_x = P/A$, $\tau_{xy} = Tr/J$, $\sigma_y = 0$.
- ✅ For **bending + axial**: $\sigma_x = P/A + My/I$, $\sigma_y = 0$ (if no transverse load), $\tau_{xy}$ depends on shear force diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buckling and Stability of Long Columns, Euler's Buckling/Crippling Load for Columns with Different End Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT402 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buckling and Stability of Long Columns, Euler's Buckling/Crippling Load for Columns with Different End Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Buckling** is the sudden lateral (sideways) deflection of a slender structural member subjected to an axial compressive load. A column is said to have **buckled** when the magnitude of axial load reaches a critical threshold, beyond which any infinitesimal increase causes disproportionately large lateral deformation and the column loses its ability to carry additional load in a stable manner. This critical load is termed the **Euler's Buckling Load** (also known as **Crippling Load**, $P_{cr}$).

In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus, a **column** is a vertical compression member whose length is considerably larger than its lateral dimensions. When the axial load $P$ is small, the column remains perfectly straight and stable. At a specific critical value of $P$, the straight configuration becomes *unstable* — the member can sustain small lateral disturbances without any restoring force, and it deflects laterally in a wave-like shape called the **buckled mode**.

**Stability of a column** refers to the capacity of the column to maintain its original straight equilibrium configuration under applied compressive loads. Equilibrium can be:
- **Stable** — small disturbances produce restoring forces; column returns to original state.
- **Neutral** — column is on the verge of buckling (at $P = P_{cr}$).
- **Unstable** — column deflects catastrophically (at $P > P_{cr}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Terminology**
> - **Long Column** → Fails by *buckling* (elastic instability).
> - **Short Column** → Fails by *crushing* (material yielding).
> - **Intermediate Column** → Fails by a *combination* of both — handled by Rankine/Johnson/IS code formulas.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a **thin plastic ruler** vertically down on a table. With very little force, nothing visible happens. But push a little harder, and suddenly — *snap!* — the ruler shoots out sideways with a loud cracking sound. It didn't "crush"; it **bent sideways** even though you only pushed straight down.

That dramatic sideways bending is **buckling**. The ruler didn't need to be crushed to fail — it failed by *losing shape*, not by *losing material strength*. The force at which this happens is the **Euler's Critical Load**.

Another real-world example is a thin steel rod under an axial push — at a certain load it suddenly bows out. The same thing happens to tall pillars in buildings, masts on ships, and legs of furniture under heavy loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition (Plain English)**
> - **Crushing** = The material gives up → happens in short, stubby columns.
> - **Buckling** = The *shape* gives up → happens in long, slender columns.
> - Euler's load answers the question: *"How much axial push before the column gets bent out of shape?"*

## 1.3 Governing Physical Parameters

The Euler critical load depends on three parameters:
1. **Flexural Rigidity $EI$** — Stiffness of the cross-section against bending ($E$ = Young's Modulus, $I$ = Area moment of inertia of cross-section about the axis of buckling).
2. **Effective Length $L_e$** — Depends entirely on the *end conditions* of the column.
3. **End Conditions** — Dictate how the column is restrained at its ends (pinned, fixed, free).

These will be formalized in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of Euler's Theory of Long Columns

Euler's classical formula is derived under the following idealizations:

1. The column is **initially perfectly straight** (no initial crookedness).
2. The load $P$ is **purely axial**, applied at the centroid of the cross-section (no eccentricity).
3. The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's law up to failure).
4. **Self-weight** of the column is neglected.
5. The column fails **only by buckling** — material strength is not exhausted.
6. **Shortening** due to direct compression is neglected.
7. The **ends are either pinned or fixed** — assumed frictionless pin or perfectly rigid clamp.

## 2.2 The Slenderness Ratio

A dimensionless geometric parameter that classifies a column:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k_{min}}$$

where:
- $L_e$ = effective length of column
- $k_{min}$ = minimum radius of gyration of the cross-section $= \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}}$

**Classification by Slenderness Ratio (as per KTU/IS 800 design standards):**

| Column Type | Slenderness Range | Failure Mode | Governing Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Long Column** | $\lambda \geq 180$ (or $\geq \sqrt{12\pi^2 E / \sigma_c}$) | Elastic Buckling | **Euler's Formula** |
| **Intermediate Column** | $30 < \lambda < 180$ | Combined Buckling + Crushing | **Rankine / Johnson's / IS Code** |
| **Short Column** | $\lambda \leq 30$ | Pure Crushing | ** Crushing Strength $= \sigma_c \cdot A$** |

## 2.3 Effective Length Concept

The **Effective Length $L_e$** of a column is the distance between successive *inflection points* (zero-bending-moment points) of the buckled shape — or equivalently, the length of an equivalent *pinned-pinned* column that would buckle at the same load.

$$L_e = K \cdot L$$

where $K$ is the **effective length factor** depending on end conditions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula (Universal Euler Equation)**
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$
> This single equation covers *all* four end conditions by varying $L_e$.

| End Condition | Sketch of Buckled Shape | Effective Length $L_e$ | Critical Load $P_{cr}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Both Ends Pinned** (Hinged-Hinged) | Half sine wave | $L_e = L$ | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ |
| **Both Ends Fixed** (Fixed-Fixed) | Quarter sine waves at each end | $L_e = \dfrac{L}{2}$ | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{4\pi^2 E I}{L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(L/2)^2}$ |
| **One End Fixed, Other End Free** (Fixed-Free, Cantilever) | Quarter sine wave | $L_e = 2L$ | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{4 L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(2L)^2}$ |
| **One End Fixed, Other End Pinned** (Fixed-Pinned) | Asymmetric curve | $L_e = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 L$ | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2\pi^2 E I}{L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(L/\sqrt{2})^2}$ |

### Auxiliary Formulas

**Critical Stress:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

**Radius of Gyration:**

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$$

**Buckling Load in terms of Slenderness Ratio:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E A}{\lambda^2}$$

**For circular section** (solid), $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$, $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$, $k = \dfrac{d}{4}$.

**For hollow circular section** (outer $D$, inner $d$), $I = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{64}$, $A = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$.

**For rectangular section** (width $b$, depth $h$, buckling about weaker axis), $I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$, $k = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{h}{2\sqrt{3}}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Euler's Theory Matters |
|---|---|
| **Building Columns / Pillars** | Determines safe axial load before lateral collapse of skyscraper columns. |
| **Bridge Piers & Trestles** | Long slender piers must be checked against buckling due to traffic/load. |
| **Aircraft Struts & Fuselage Frames** | Slender aluminum struts fail by buckling well before material yielding. |
| **Rail Coach Bogies** | Coil springs and side frames designed against buckling loads. |
| **Ship Masts & Drill Rigs** | Tall masts behave as long columns under their own weight. |
| **Robot Arms & Telescopic Ladders** | Slender members under compression — stability is paramount. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice from the table that for the *same column*, fixing both ends increases the buckling load by a factor of **4** compared to pinned-pinned — a 300 % gain in load capacity from a free engineering choice!
